column4: Severe chest pain
column5: Crushing chest pain radiating to left arm, diaphoresis, nausea, shortness of breath, pain started 2 hours ago and is not relieved by rest
column6: Hypertension for 10 years on lisinopril, high cholesterol on atorvastatin, 30-year smoking history (1 pack per day), family history of early heart disease (father had MI at age 55)
column7: ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
column8: Emergency cardiac catheterization performed within 90 minutes showing 95% occlusion of LAD. Drug-eluting stent placed successfully. Started on dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81mg + clopidogrel 75mg), metoprolol 25mg BID, increased atorvastatin to 80mg, continued lisinopril. Enrolled in cardiac rehabilitation program. Smoking cessation counseling provided.
column9: Excellent recovery with full return of cardiac function. EF 55% on discharge echo. Patient quit smoking and completed 12-week cardiac rehab program. No angina at 6-month follow-up. Returned to work with no restrictions.

column4: Chest tightness and overwhelming fatigue
column5: Intermittent chest pressure for 3 days, worsening with minimal exertion, extreme fatigue requiring frequent rest, mild shortness of breath, no radiation of pain
column6: Type 2 diabetes for 8 years (A1C typically 8.5%), obesity with BMI 34, sedentary lifestyle, no regular exercise
column7: Non-ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI)
column8: Medical management initiated with aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin drip, metoprolol, high-dose statin. Cardiac biomarkers peaked at 6 hours. Scheduled for elective cardiac catheterization in 48 hours showing 70% RCA stenosis. Successful stenting performed. Diabetes management optimized with metformin increase and addition of GLP-1 agonist. Comprehensive lifestyle counseling.
column9: Stable condition. Angina controlled with medications. Successfully lost 15 pounds in cardiac rehab. A1C improved to 7.2%. Scheduled for 6-week cardiac catheterization follow-up. Walking 30 minutes daily.

column4: Sharp chest pain worse with breathing
column5: Sudden onset sharp right chest pain 2 hours after landing from 14-hour flight from Tokyo, pain increases with deep breathing and coughing, mild shortness of breath, no recent trauma
column6: No significant past medical history, travels internationally frequently for consulting work (2-3 times monthly), generally healthy and active
column7: Pulmonary Embolism (segmental PE in right lower lobe)
column8: CT angiography confirmed PE. Initiated therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin drip followed by transition to rivaroxaban 15mg BID for 3 weeks then 20mg daily. Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula. Compression ultrasound negative for DVT. Thrombophilia workup sent. Prescribed compression stockings for future travel. Education on warning signs.
column9: Complete resolution of symptoms. Repeat CT at 3 months showed resolution of clot. On long-term anticoagulation (plan for 6 months minimum). Thrombophilia screen negative. Using compression stockings for all flights >4 hours. No complications.

column4: Persistent cough with fever and confusion
column5: Productive cough for 5 days with yellow-green sputum, fever up to 101.5°F, increasing difficulty breathing, new onset confusion noted by family, decreased oral intake
column6: COPD (FEV1 45% predicted) on chronic inhalers, former smoker quit 5 years ago (40 pack-year history), previous hospitalization for pneumonia 2 years ago
column7: Community-Acquired Pneumonia with Sepsis
column8: Admitted to ICU for sepsis management. Broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated (ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + azithromycin 500mg IV daily). Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation. Supplemental oxygen via high-flow nasal cannula. Sputum culture grew Streptococcus pneumoniae. Completed 7-day antibiotic course. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral. Vaccinated with Pneumovax 23 and Prevnar 20.
column9: Gradual recovery over 8 days. Required 2 days in ICU for hemodynamic support. Mental status returned to baseline. Discharged on home oxygen (2L) with plan to wean. Received pneumococcal and influenza vaccines. Follow-up chest X-ray in 6 weeks showed resolution.

column4: Severe difficulty breathing and wheezing
column5: Acute onset severe wheezing and shortness of breath, using accessory muscles to breathe, unable to speak full sentences, peak flow 40% of personal best, symptoms started after exposure to classroom pet
column6: Poorly controlled asthma with multiple ED visits (5 in past year), known allergies to cats, dust mites, pollen. On albuterol PRN only - not on daily controller medication
column7: Acute Severe Asthma Exacerbation
column8: Continuous nebulized albuterol and ipratropium for 1 hour. IV methylprednisolone 60mg. Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >92%. Gradual improvement after 3 hours. Transitioned to oral prednisone taper. Started on inhaled corticosteroid/LABA combination (Advair) twice daily. Provided spacer device and technique education. Asthma action plan created. Allergy consultation scheduled.
column9: Significant improvement within 24 hours. Discharged after 2 days with new controller medication regimen. Peak flows normalized. Mother trained on recognizing warning signs. Follow-up with pediatric pulmonologist in 2 weeks. No ED visits in subsequent 6 months.

column4: Sudden right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking
column5: Acute onset right arm and leg weakness noticed while making breakfast, slurred speech, right facial droop, symptoms began approximately 90 minutes ago per family, patient attempting to communicate but words not clear
column6: Atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation due to fall risk), hypertension on amlodipine, previous TIA 3 years ago with full recovery, lives alone
column7: Acute Ischemic Stroke (Left Middle Cerebral Artery territory)
column8: NIHSS score 12 on arrival. Head CT negative for hemorrhage. IV tPA (alteplase 0.9mg/kg) administered within 3-hour window. Neurology consultation immediate. Monitored in stroke unit. Developed mild hemorrhagic transformation on day 2 but clinically stable. Started on rivaroxaban for stroke prevention. Intensive physical and speech therapy initiated. Transferred to inpatient rehabilitation facility.
column9: Partial recovery with persistent mild right arm weakness (4/5 strength) and mild expressive aphasia. Able to walk with assistive device. Discharged to acute inpatient rehab on day 6. After 3 weeks rehab, moved to assisted living facility. Continued outpatient therapy. At 3 months, independent with ADLs with minor deficits.

column4: Worst headache of my life
column5: Sudden onset severe "thunderclap" headache while exercising at gym, reached maximum intensity within seconds, associated nausea and vomiting, photophobia, neck stiffness developed
column6: History of migraines (3-4 per month) usually relieved by sumatriptan, high-stress job in finance, positive family history (mother had cerebral aneurysm rupture at age 52)
column7: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage from ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm
column8: Emergency CT head showed subarachnoid blood in basal cisterns. Neurosurgery consulted immediately. CTA revealed 7mm anterior communicating artery aneurysm. Underwent endovascular coiling within 8 hours. Nimodipine 60mg every 4 hours for vasospasm prevention. External ventricular drain placed for hydrocephalus management. ICU monitoring for 10 days. No vasospasm or rebleeding. EVD removed day 8.
column9: Excellent recovery with no neurological deficits. Hydrocephalus resolved without need for permanent shunt. Discharged home on day 12 with follow-up imaging scheduled at 6 months and 1 year. Returned to work after 6 weeks with no restrictions. Advised on blood pressure control and avoiding straining.

column4: Witnessed seizure
column5: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 2 minutes witnessed by coworker, post-ictal confusion for 20 minutes, tongue biting noted, no previous seizure history
column6: Bicycle accident 2 weeks ago with brief loss of consciousness, discharged from ED at that time with normal head CT, no follow-up obtained, works as graphic designer
column7: Post-Traumatic Seizure secondary to previous head injury
column8: Loaded with levetiracetam 1500mg IV followed by 500mg BID oral. Neurology consultation obtained. Repeat head CT showed small area of encephalomalacia from previous injury. MRI brain confirmed post-traumatic changes. 24-hour video EEG monitoring showed no epileptiform activity during admission. Comprehensive seizure education provided. Driving restrictions explained per state law.
column9: No additional seizures during hospitalization or in 6-month follow-up. Continued on levetiracetam with plan to attempt medication wean after 2 years if seizure-free. Driving privilege restored after 6 months seizure-free per state regulations. Regular neurology follow-up maintained.

column4: Severe right lower abdominal pain
column5: Pain began periumbilically 8 hours ago then migrated to right lower quadrant, now constant and severe (8/10), worse with movement, nausea with one episode vomiting, anorexia, low-grade fever 100.8°F
column6: Generally healthy, no significant past medical history, works as accountant, rare medical visits
column7: Acute Appendicitis
column8: Physical exam positive for McBurney point tenderness, positive Rovsing sign. CT abdomen/pelvis confirmed acute appendicitis without perforation. Emergency laparoscopic appendectomy performed within 3 hours of diagnosis. Pathology confirmed acute appendicitis. Post-op pain controlled with IV ketorolac and oral oxycodone. Started on clear liquids post-op day 1, advanced to regular diet.
column9: Uncomplicated post-operative recovery. Discharged home on post-op day 2 with oral pain medications. Incision sites healing well at 2-week follow-up. No complications. Cleared to return to work after 2 weeks with no lifting restrictions. Full recovery within 4 weeks.

column4: Vomiting blood
column5: Two episodes of coffee-ground emesis over past 6 hours, black tarry stools for 2 days, lightheadedness with standing, weakness, takes ibuprofen 800mg three times daily for severe arthritis pain
column6: Rheumatoid arthritis for 10 years, daily NSAID use without proton pump inhibitor prophylaxis, no previous GI bleeding, no alcohol use
column7: Upper GI Bleed secondary to bleeding duodenal ulcer
column8: Initial hemoglobin 7.2 (baseline 13.5). Two units PRBC transfused. IV pantoprazole 80mg bolus then 8mg/hour infusion. Emergency EGD within 4 hours showed actively bleeding 1.5cm duodenal ulcer. Successful thermal coagulation and epinephrine injection. Biopsy obtained for H. pylori. Started oral PPI 40mg BID. NSAIDs discontinued. Gastroenterology follow-up arranged.
column9: Hemoglobin stabilized at 10.1. No further bleeding. H. pylori positive - completed triple therapy (clarithromycin, amoxicillin, PPI) for 14 days. Discharged day 3 on PPI therapy. Rheumatology consulted for NSAID alternatives. Switched to celecoxib with continued PPI. Repeat EGD in 8 weeks showed healed ulcer. No recurrent bleeding.

column4: High fever and body rash
column5: Fever up to 103°F for 3 days, diffuse macular rash appeared yesterday, severe headache, joint pain (especially knees and wrists), muscle aches, extreme fatigue. Returned from 2-week volunteer medical mission in Honduras 5 days ago.
column6: Healthy, no chronic medical conditions, up to date on routine vaccines, takes oral contraceptives, no regular medications
column7: Dengue Fever (confirmed by NS1 antigen and IgM)
column8: Supportive care with IV hydration (careful fluid balance monitoring), acetaminophen for fever and pain (avoiding NSAIDs due to bleeding risk), daily CBC monitoring for thrombocytopenia and hematocrit. Platelet count nadir 85,000 on day 4. No signs of plasma leakage or hemorrhagic manifestations. Contact precautions and mosquito netting used. Infectious disease consultation.
column9: Gradual improvement starting day 5. Fever curve resolved by day 7. Platelet count recovered to 145,000 by discharge. No hemorrhagic features developed. Rash resolved completely. Full recovery with return to baseline health. Educated on mosquito bite prevention for future travel. No long-term complications.

column4: Severe diarrhea and dehydration
column5: Profuse watery diarrhea for 3 days (15+ episodes daily), crampy abdominal pain, nausea, low-grade fever, significant dehydration with orthostatic hypotension, completed 10-day course of clindamycin for dental infection 5 days ago
column6: Recent antibiotic use (clindamycin for dental abscess), otherwise healthy and active, works as software engineer
column7: Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) Colitis
column8: Stool PCR positive for C. difficile toxin. Started oral vancomycin 125mg QID. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (4L in first 12 hours). Probiotic education provided. Contact isolation precautions. Discontinuation of triggering antibiotic. Daily monitoring of white blood cell count and clinical status. CT abdomen showed pancolitis without toxic megacolon or perforation.
column9: Significant clinical improvement by day 4 of vancomycin therapy. Diarrhea decreased to 3-4 episodes daily. Discharged on day 5 to complete 14-day course of oral vancomycin at home. Educated on strict hand hygiene (soap and water, not sanitizer). Symptoms fully resolved by end of treatment. No recurrence at 30-day follow-up. Received probiotic recommendation.

column4: Extreme thirst and frequent urination
column5: Unquenchable thirst for several weeks, urinating every hour including multiple times overnight, unintentional weight loss of 20 pounds over 2 months, blurred vision, constant fatigue, nausea for 2 days
column6: Strong family history of diabetes (mother, two siblings), overweight with BMI 32, sedentary lifestyle, no regular medical care for past 5 years
column7: New Onset Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
column8: Admission glucose 580 mg/dL, pH 7.18, positive ketones. Insulin infusion protocol initiated per DKA pathway. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with potassium replacement. Anion gap closed after 18 hours. Transitioned to subcutaneous insulin (basal-bolus regimen). Comprehensive diabetes education including glucose monitoring, carbohydrate counting, insulin administration. Endocrinology consultation. Ophthalmology and podiatry referrals placed.
column9: DKA successfully resolved. Discharged on insulin glargine 20 units daily plus insulin lispro with meals. Enrolled in comprehensive diabetes management program. Lost 10 pounds in first month. A1C at 3 months: 7.8% (down from 13.2%). Regular endocrinology follow-up established. Using continuous glucose monitor.

column4: Racing heart and unexplained anxiety
column5: Persistent tachycardia (HR 110-130 at rest), hand tremors, heat intolerance with excessive sweating, unintentional weight loss of 15 pounds despite increased appetite, difficulty sleeping, anxiety and irritability, symptoms gradually worsening over 6 months
column6: No significant past medical history, works as elementary school teacher, symptoms initially attributed to work stress
column7: Graves Disease (Hyperthyroidism with diffuse toxic goiter)
column8: TSH <0.01, elevated free T4 and T3, positive TSH receptor antibodies. Thyroid ultrasound showed diffuse enlargement with increased vascularity. Started methimazole 15mg BID to reduce thyroid hormone production. Propranolol 20mg TID for symptom management (tremor, tachycardia, anxiety). Endocrinology consultation for long-term management planning. Discussed options: continued medical management vs radioactive iodine vs thyroidectomy.
column9: Symptoms significantly improved within 3 weeks of medication initiation. Heart rate normalized, tremors resolved, anxiety decreased. Weight stabilizing. Scheduled for radioactive iodine ablation in 3 months per patient preference. Close endocrine follow-up with plan to monitor for hypothyroidism post-treatment. Ophthalmology follow-up for baseline eye exam.

column4: Right hip pain after fall
column5: Slipped on ice patch on sidewalk this morning, fell onto right hip, immediate severe pain, completely unable to bear weight on right leg, right leg appears shortened and externally rotated
column6: Osteoporosis (T-score -3.2 on DEXA 1 year ago), taking calcium 1200mg and vitamin D 2000 IU daily, previous Colles fracture 3 years ago, generally independent with all ADLs
column7: Right Intertrochanteric Hip Fracture
column8: X-ray confirmed displaced intertrochanteric fracture. Orthopedic surgery consultation immediate. Preoperative cardiac clearance obtained. Underwent open reduction internal fixation with cephalomedullary nail on day of admission. Post-operative pain controlled with multimodal analgesia (acetaminophen, gabapentin, PRN opioids). Physical therapy initiated post-op day 1. Fall risk assessment and home safety evaluation. Osteoporosis treatment optimized with addition of alendronate.
column9: Good surgical outcome with stable fixation on post-op films. Progressing well with physical therapy - partial weight bearing with walker. Discharged to skilled nursing facility on day 5 for continued rehabilitation. Goal to return home in 2-3 weeks. At 6-week follow-up, weight bearing as tolerated with walker. Bone healing appropriately on X-ray. Home health PT arranged.

column4: Severe ankle injury during basketball
column5: Landed awkwardly after jumping for rebound, heard distinct "pop" sound, immediate severe pain and swelling in left ankle, unable to bear weight, tried to "walk it off" initially but pain too severe
column6: Athletic and healthy, plays basketball 4-5 times weekly, no previous significant injuries, college student
column7: Severe Left Ankle Sprain (Grade 2-3 ATFL sprain)
column8: Ottawa ankle rules met. X-rays negative for fracture. Significant swelling and ecchymosis. RICE protocol initiated (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation). Orthopedic walking boot provided. Pain controlled with NSAIDs. MRI ordered to assess ligament integrity showed grade 2 anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) sprain with partial tearing but no complete rupture. Orthopedic consultation recommended conservative management with aggressive physical therapy over surgical repair.
column9: Significant improvement with conservative treatment. Boot weaned at 4 weeks. Physical therapy focused on proprioception, strengthening, and return-to-sport activities. Full weight bearing without pain at 6 weeks. Gradual return to basketball over 12 weeks with ankle brace. At 3 months, returned to full athletic activity with no instability. Continued preventive ankle strengthening exercises.

column4: Severe headache and swelling in pregnancy
column5: Severe persistent headache for 2 days not relieved by acetaminophen, visual changes (seeing spots), significant swelling in hands and face (ring no longer fits), blood pressure 165/105 in clinic (baseline 115/70), currently 36 weeks pregnant with first baby
column6: Uncomplicated pregnancy until now, good prenatal care with all appointments kept, no chronic medical conditions, healthy and active prior to pregnancy
column7: Severe Preeclampsia at 36 weeks gestation
column8: Immediate admission to labor and delivery. Magnesium sulfate infusion initiated for seizure prophylaxis. Antihypertensive therapy with IV labetalol for blood pressure control. Continuous fetal monitoring showing reassuring heart rate. Maternal labs monitored (platelets, liver enzymes, creatinine). Decision made to proceed with induction of labor given severity and gestational age. Cervical ripening with Cervidil followed by Pitocin induction.
column9: Successful vaginal delivery after 14-hour labor of healthy baby girl weighing 6 lbs 2 oz with Apgar scores 8 and 9. Magnesium sulfate continued for 24 hours post-delivery. Blood pressure gradually improved, normalized by post-partum day 3. No seizures or other complications. Mother and baby discharged together on day 3. Blood pressure monitoring arranged for home health follow-up. Preeclampsia resolved postpartum as expected.

column4: Suicidal thoughts with plan
column5: Severe depression worsening over 6 months, constant thoughts of suicide for past 2 weeks, has specific plan (medication overdose), feels hopeless and worthless, recent job loss and relationship breakup, stopped taking antidepressant 2 months ago because "it wasn't working"
column6: Major depressive disorder diagnosed at age 19, previous suicide attempt at age 19 (overdose, hospitalized), has been on/off antidepressants, inconsistent with therapy, family history of depression (mother)
column7: Major Depressive Disorder, Severe, with Suicidal Ideation and Plan
column8: Immediate psychiatric evaluation and admission to inpatient psychiatric unit. Suicide precautions initiated (1:1 observation, environmental safety). Restarted SSRI (sertraline 50mg daily, plan to titrate up). Individual and group therapy daily. Family meeting arranged. Safety planning developed. Psychiatric consultation for medication management. Social work involved for job search resources and housing concerns.
column9: Mood significantly improved by day 7. Suicidal ideation resolved. Transitioned to partial hospitalization program (PHP) on day 8. Continued SSRI, titrated to 150mg daily with good response. Regular individual therapy established. Attending support groups. Found new employment at 6-week mark. At 3-month follow-up, mood stable, no suicidal thoughts, engaged in treatment.

column4: Extremely painful rash on right side of body
column5: Started with burning, tingling pain on right chest/back 2 days ago, yesterday developed clusters of small blisters following a band-like distribution, pain is severe (9/10) and constant, worse than childbirth per patient
column6: History of chickenpox as a child (age 7), otherwise healthy, recent high-stress period (caring for mother with cancer), not up to date on Shingrix vaccine
column7: Herpes Zoster (Shingles) - T5-T6 dermatome distribution
column8: Classic dermatomal vesicular rash confirmed by clinical examination. Started on oral valacyclovir 1000mg TID within 48 hours of rash onset (ideally <72 hours). Pain management with gabapentin 300mg TID (to prevent postherpetic neuralgia), acetaminophen, topical lidocaine patches. Cool compresses recommended. Patient education on contagiousness and preventing spread. Advised on Shingrix vaccine after resolution.
column9: Rash beginning to crust over by day 7. Acute pain significantly decreased but some persistent neuropathic pain. Completed 7-day course of antiviral. Gabapentin continued and gradually tapered over 6 weeks. Mild residual neuralgia at 3 months managed with low-dose gabapentin. Received Shingrix vaccine series after full recovery. No postherpetic neuralgia at 6 months.

column4: Barely urinating and severe swelling
column5: Urinated only once in past 24 hours (very small amount, dark), significant swelling in legs and face, nausea and vomiting, confusion, weakness, took ibuprofen 800mg three times daily for past week for back pain
column6: Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3 (baseline creatinine 1.8-2.0), diabetes type 2 for 15 years, hypertension, recent acute back pain led to NSAID use
column7: Acute Kidney Injury on Chronic Kidney Disease (AKI on CKD)
column8: Admission creatinine 5.8 (baseline 1.9), hyperkalemia (K+ 6.2), metabolic acidosis. Immediately discontinued all nephrotoxic medications including NSAIDs and metformin. Aggressive but careful IV fluid resuscitation. Kayexalate and insulin/dextrose for hyperkalemia. Nephrology consulted. Renal ultrasound showed no obstruction, chronically small kidneys. Avoided contrast studies. Close monitoring of electrolytes and urine output. Dietary potassium and protein restriction. Discussed possible need for dialysis if no improvement.
column9: Kidney function gradually improved with supportive care. Creatinine decreased to 3.2 by day 4, then 2.5 by discharge (day 6). Urine output normalized. Electrolytes stabilized. Avoided dialysis. Discharged with close nephrology follow-up. Strict education on avoiding NSAIDs. At 1-month follow-up, creatinine 2.1 (near baseline). Emphasizing kidney-protective measures and CKD management.

column4: Severe abdominal pain and missed period
column5: Sudden onset severe lower abdominal pain (worse on left side) for 2 hours, associated dizziness and shoulder pain, last menstrual period 7 weeks ago, sexually active
column6: Generally healthy, no chronic conditions, not on birth control currently, previous uncomplicated pregnancy ended in elective termination at age 17
column7: Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy (left tubal)
column8: Positive urine pregnancy test. Hemodynamically unstable (BP 85/50, HR 125). Bedside ultrasound showed free fluid in pelvis. Emergency gynecology consultation. Hemoglobin 7.1. Immediate transfer to OR for diagnostic laparoscopy which confirmed ruptured left tubal ectopic pregnancy. Left salpingectomy performed. Received 3 units packed RBCs. Post-op monitoring in ICU overnight then transferred to floor.
column9: Recovered well post-operatively. Hemoglobin stabilized at 10.5. Pain well controlled. Discharged on post-op day 3. Follow-up hCG levels appropriately decreased to negative. Counseled on future pregnancy risks and importance of early prenatal care. Started on oral contraceptives per patient request. Discussed ovarian reserve testing given single remaining tube.

column4: Fever and stiff neck
column5: High fever (104°F) for 2 days, severe headache, stiff neck noted by parents, vomiting, lethargy, photophobia, irritable when awake, attending daycare where several other children recently ill
column6: Previously healthy child, up to date on vaccinations including Hib and pneumococcal conjugate vaccines, no chronic medical conditions
column7: Bacterial Meningitis (Streptococcus pneumoniae)
column8: Immediate lumbar puncture after blood cultures obtained. CSF showed pleocytosis with neutrophil predominance, elevated protein, low glucose - consistent with bacterial meningitis. Started empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) within 30 minutes of arrival. Admitted to PICU. Gram stain showed gram-positive diplococci. Culture confirmed S. pneumoniae sensitive to ceftriaxone - vancomycin discontinued. Dexamethasone given with first antibiotic dose.
column9: Significant clinical improvement by day 3. Fever resolved, mental status normalized. Completed 10-day course of IV ceftriaxone. Hearing screen normal before discharge. Neurological exam normal. Discharged home on day 11. Neurodevelopmental follow-up at 3 months showed no deficits. Full recovery with no sequelae.

column4: Fall with hip pain and inability to stand
column5: Found on floor by home health aide this morning, unsure how long patient was down, reports tripping over rug, severe left hip pain, unable to stand or walk
column6: Dementia (moderate stage), lives alone with home health aide visits twice daily, history of falls (3 in past year), osteoporosis, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, hypertension, previous stroke 5 years ago with residual right-sided weakness
column7: Left Hip Fracture with Rhabdomyolysis from Prolonged Immobilization
column8: X-ray confirmed left femoral neck fracture. Labs showed elevated creatinine (2.8, baseline 1.1), CK 15,000, myoglobin 800. Aggressive IV hydration for rhabdomyolysis. Warfarin held. Orthopedic surgery consulted - underwent hip hemiarthroplasty on hospital day 2. Geriatric consultation for delirium management and fall prevention. Social work consulted for discharge planning given safety concerns. Physical therapy intensive course.
column9: Kidney function recovered (creatinine 1.3 at discharge). Surgery uncomplicated. However, developed delirium requiring brief antipsychotic use. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker. Family meeting resulted in decision for assisted living placement. Discharged to skilled nursing facility on day 9, then transitioned to assisted living after 3 weeks. Follow-up at 2 months showed good recovery with walker use.

column4: Swollen and painful calf
column5: Left calf swollen, warm, red, and tender for 2 days, pain with walking, returned from cross-country drive 3 days ago (drove 18 hours with minimal stops)
column6: Obesity (BMI 38), sedentary lifestyle, family history of blood clots (mother had PE at age 55), recently started new job with long commute
column7: Deep Vein Thrombosis (Left Popliteal and Tibial Veins)
column8: Doppler ultrasound confirmed extensive DVT. No signs of PE on assessment. Started therapeutic anticoagulation with rivaroxaban 15mg BID (for 21 days then 20mg daily). Leg elevation, compression stockings. Thrombophilia workup sent (deferred some tests until off anticoagulation). Patient education on anticoagulation, leg care, and activity. Weight loss counseling and referral.
column9: Pain and swelling significantly improved by 1 week. At 3-month follow-up, repeat ultrasound showed partial resolution of clot. Continuing anticoagulation for at least 3 months, possibly longer depending on risk factor assessment. Lost 12 pounds with dietary changes. Using compression stockings. No bleeding complications.

column4: Severe allergic reaction
column5: Ate lunch at new restaurant 20 minutes ago (Thai food), developed itching of lips and mouth, progressing to hives all over body, swelling of lips and tongue, difficulty swallowing, feeling of throat tightness, difficulty breathing
column6: Known allergies to shellfish and tree nuts (previous mild reactions with hives), carries EpiPen but left at home, no previous anaphylaxis
column7: Anaphylaxis (suspected peanut exposure)
column8: Immediate treatment with IM epinephrine 0.3mg (two doses given 5 minutes apart due to ongoing symptoms), IV diphenhydramine 50mg, IV methylprednisolone 125mg, supplemental oxygen, IV fluid resuscitation. Symptoms gradually improved over 30 minutes. Monitored for biphasic reaction - observed for 6 hours in ED. Epinephrine auto-injector prescribed x2. Allergy/immunology referral placed.
column9: Complete resolution of symptoms. No biphasic reaction during observation period. Discharged with two epinephrine auto-injectors and detailed instructions. Advised to wear medical alert bracelet. Follow-up with allergist arranged - confirmed severe peanut allergy via testing. Patient now carries two EpiPens at all times and carefully reads all food labels.

column4: Sudden shortness of breath at rest
column5: Acute onset dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, bilateral leg swelling for 1 week, weight gain of 8 pounds, decreased urine output
column6: CHF (EF 30% on last echo), atrial fibrillation, hypertension, CKD Stage 2
column7: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
column8: IV furosemide 80mg bolus then drip, strict fluid restriction 1.5L daily, oxygen supplementation, cardiac monitoring. BNP on admission 4800. Echo repeated showing EF 28%. Uptitrated carvedilol and lisinopril after stabilization. Cardiology and nephrology co-management.
column9: Lost 12 pounds of fluid over 5 days. BNP decreased to 850. Symptoms resolved. Discharged on optimized regimen including spironolactone added. Close follow-up with heart failure clinic. At 1 month, stable and adherent to fluid and sodium restrictions.

column4: Chronic cough and progressive shortness of breath
column5: Dry hacking cough for 8 months, worsening exertional dyspnea, never smoker, no occupational exposures, no pets. CT chest ordered by PCP showed bilateral basal predominant ground-glass opacities with honeycombing.
column6: Hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, gastroesophageal reflux disease
column7: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF)
column8: Pulmonary function tests confirmed restrictive pattern (FVC 62% predicted, DLCO 48%). Bronchoscopy with BAL performed. High-resolution CT pattern diagnostic of IPF. Started nintedanib 150mg BID. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral. Supplemental oxygen for exertion. Lung transplant evaluation initiated given progressive disease.
column9: Tolerated nintedanib with manageable GI side effects. FVC decline slowed compared to untreated natural history. Enrolled in clinical trial. Completed pulmonary rehab. Listed for lung transplant evaluation. Requires 2L oxygen with exertion. Ongoing close monitoring.

column4: Palpitations and near-fainting episode
column5: Episode of near-syncope while walking upstairs, heart racing irregularly, episode lasted 15 minutes and self-terminated, similar shorter episodes 3 times in past month, no chest pain
column6: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy diagnosed at age 40, family history (brother died suddenly at age 38 while playing sports)
column7: Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response in HCM
column8: Admitted for monitoring. Telemetry confirmed AF with RVR up to 160bpm. Rate controlled with diltiazem IV. TEE performed to rule out thrombus. Cardioverted successfully to sinus rhythm. Started on dofetilide for rhythm control. Anticoagulated with apixaban. ICD placement discussed given HCM with syncope - patient agreed. ICD implanted on day 3.
column9: Sinus rhythm maintained post-cardioversion. ICD implanted without complications. Discharged on dofetilide and apixaban. Cardiology follow-up in 2 weeks. Device check at 1 month showing appropriate sensing. No further AF episodes or ICD shocks at 3-month follow-up.

column4: Intermittent leg pain with walking
column5: Right calf and thigh pain that begins after walking 2 blocks, relieved by rest within 2-3 minutes, pain free at rest, progressive over 6 months, occasionally wakes with cold feet at night
column6: Peripheral artery disease known for 3 years, diabetes type 2, hypertension, 40-year smoking history (quit 2 years ago)
column7: Critical Limb Ischemia - Right Lower Extremity
column8: ABI 0.45 on right. CT angiography showed severe multisegment disease. Vascular surgery consulted. Underwent endovascular intervention with angioplasty and stenting of right superficial femoral artery. Post-procedure ABI improved to 0.78. Dual antiplatelet therapy. Wound care for small ischemic ulcer on right great toe. Supervised exercise program.
column9: Significant improvement in walking distance - now unlimited. Ischemic ulcer healed at 6 weeks. ABI maintained at 0.82 at 3 months. Continued smoking cessation support. Diabetes optimization. Enrolled in structured exercise program. No limb-threatening events.

column4: Severe back pain and inability to walk
column5: Acute severe low back pain with bilateral leg weakness that woke him from sleep, difficulty urinating, bowel incontinence this morning, pain radiating down both legs
column6: Lumbar stenosis known for 5 years, prostate cancer treated with radiation 3 years ago, hypertension, diabetes
column7: Cauda Equina Syndrome from Lumbar Disc Herniation
column8: MRI lumbar spine showed massive L4-L5 disc herniation with cauda equina compression. Emergency neurosurgery consultation. Underwent emergency L4-L5 decompressive laminectomy and microdiscectomy within 4 hours of presentation. Foley catheter placed. Post-op monitoring of motor and sensory function.
column9: Significant improvement in leg strength within 24 hours post-op. Bladder function returned on day 3. Discharged to inpatient rehab on day 5. At 6-week follow-up, walking without assistance, bladder continence fully restored, mild residual tingling in feet. Returned to baseline functional status at 3 months.

column4: Severe joint pain, butterfly rash
column5: Facial rash across cheeks and nose for 2 weeks, joint pain affecting hands, wrists, and knees bilaterally, fatigue, hair loss noticed over past month, low-grade fever, mouth sores
column6: No significant past medical history, takes oral contraceptives
column7: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) - New Diagnosis
column8: ANA positive 1:640, anti-dsDNA elevated, complement levels low. CBC showed leukopenia and mild thrombocytopenia. Urinalysis showed proteinuria - nephrology consulted. Renal biopsy performed confirming Class III lupus nephritis. Started on hydroxychloroquine 400mg daily, prednisone 40mg daily with taper plan, mycophenolate mofetil 1500mg BID for nephritis.
column9: Significant improvement in symptoms at 6 weeks. Rash resolved. Joint pain controlled. Proteinuria decreased by 60% at 3 months. Prednisone tapered to 5mg. Hydroxychloroquine continued long-term. Rheumatology follow-up every 3 months. Educated on sun protection, infection precautions, contraception with lupus.

column4: Progressive difficulty swallowing
column5: Difficulty swallowing solids for 3 months, now having trouble with liquids as well, unintentional weight loss of 18 pounds, occasional regurgitation, no heartburn
column6: 60-pack-year smoking history, heavy alcohol use (10 drinks daily for 30 years), hypertension
column7: Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (T3N1M0, Stage III)
column8: EGD showed ulcerating mid-esophageal mass. Biopsy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. PET/CT showed regional lymph node involvement, no distant metastases. Nutritional support via PEG tube. Concurrent chemoradiation (carboplatin/paclitaxel with 50.4Gy radiation). Multidisciplinary tumor board review. Surgical evaluation for possible esophagectomy after neoadjuvant therapy.
column9: Completed chemoradiation with significant tumor response on restaging PET/CT. Tolerated treatment with manageable mucositis and fatigue. Underwent Ivor-Lewis esophagectomy 6 weeks post-chemoradiation. Pathology showed near-complete response (ypT1N0). Recovering well. Dysphagia improving with swallowing therapy.

column4: Testicular swelling and pain
column5: Painless right testicular swelling noted 3 weeks ago, now mild aching discomfort, no history of trauma or infection, otherwise healthy
column6: Cryptorchidism as infant (corrected surgically at age 2)
column7: Testicular Germ Cell Tumor (Non-seminomatous)
column8: Testicular ultrasound confirmed solid heterogeneous right testicular mass. Tumor markers: AFP markedly elevated, beta-hCG mildly elevated, LDH elevated. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Underwent right radical orchiectomy. Pathology: mixed germ cell tumor (embryonal carcinoma 70%, yolk sac 30%). Staging: Stage IIB. Sperm banking performed prior to chemotherapy.
column9: Tolerated 3 cycles of BEP chemotherapy with manageable toxicity. Tumor markers normalized after cycle 2. Restaging CT showed significant reduction in retroperitoneal nodes. Surveillance imaging at 3 and 6 months showed continued response. Excellent prognosis. Testosterone normal from remaining testis. Follow-up with oncology every 3 months.

column4: Severe abdominal pain and jaundice
column5: Right upper quadrant pain radiating to right shoulder for 6 hours, associated nausea, vomiting, fever 102°F, skin turning yellow, dark urine noted this morning
column6: Gallstones known for 5 years (declined elective cholecystectomy), obesity BMI 36, type 2 diabetes
column7: Acute Cholangitis (Charcot's Triad)
column8: AST/ALT elevated 4x normal, bilirubin 6.8, WBC 18,000. MRCP confirmed choledocholithiasis with common bile duct dilation. Emergency ERCP performed with successful stone extraction and sphincterotomy. IV piperacillin-tazobactam started. Jaundice improved rapidly. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed on day 3 after stabilization.
column9: Excellent recovery. Liver enzymes normalized by day 4. Completed 7-day antibiotic course. Discharged day 5. No biliary complications. Follow-up imaging confirmed clear biliary system. Lost 20 pounds over subsequent 3 months with dietary modifications. No recurrence.

column4: Severe eating disorder symptoms
column5: Admitted by parents - found with BMI 14.2, syncope at home, weakness, cold hands and feet, refusing all food, heart rate 38 bpm found on home monitoring
column6: Anorexia nervosa for 2 years, previous outpatient treatment unsuccessful, anxiety disorder, high-achieving student
column7: Anorexia Nervosa, Severe, with Cardiac Complications (Bradycardia)
column8: Telemetry admission for bradycardia. Electrolytes repleted (severe hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia). Nasogastric tube placed for nutritional rehabilitation after patient refusal. Refeeding syndrome protocol followed - calories increased slowly. Cardiac monitoring continuous. Psychiatry consultation. Family-based therapy initiated. Multidisciplinary team: psychiatry, dietitian, pediatrics, cardiology.
column9: Heart rate normalized to 58-65 bpm by day 7 as nutrition improved. Weight increased from 82 to 90 pounds by discharge (day 21). Transferred to residential eating disorder program. Family therapy ongoing. At 6-month follow-up BMI 16.8 with continued improvement. Long road to recovery acknowledged.

column4: Sudden vision loss in one eye
column5: Woke up this morning with complete painless vision loss in right eye, no headache, no other neurological symptoms, had noticed 'floaters' increasing over past month
column6: Hypertension on amlodipine, hypercholesterolemia, BMI 31, type 2 diabetes
column7: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion (CRAO)
column8: Immediate ophthalmology consultation. Funduscopic exam showed cherry red spot classic for CRAO. Ocular massage performed. Intraocular pressure lowering with acetazolamide. Stroke workup initiated - MRI brain negative for infarct. Echocardiogram showed PFO. Carotid ultrasound showed mild atherosclerosis. Hematologic workup for hypercoagulability. Started on aspirin and statin.
column9: Partial visual recovery (20/100 from counting fingers). PFO closure discussed with cardiology. Started on anticoagulation. Neuro-ophthalmology follow-up arranged. Visual rehabilitation services engaged. Optimized cardiovascular risk factor management. Smoking cessation counseled.

column4: Confusion and agitation at home
column5: Family reports 3-day history of worsening confusion, talking to people who aren't there, not sleeping, refusing medications, baseline is fully independent and sharp
column6: Moderate Alzheimer dementia, hypertension, hypothyroidism, lives with daughter
column7: Delirium superimposed on Dementia (infectious cause - UTI)
column8: Urinalysis showed pyuria. Urine culture grew E. coli. Started on ceftriaxone IV. Thyroid levels checked (normal). Medication reconciliation - identified anticholinergic contributing agent. Non-pharmacologic delirium prevention: reorientation, sleep hygiene, early mobility, family presence encouraged. Gentle hydration. Avoided antipsychotics initially.
column9: Mental status returned toward baseline over 5 days of antibiotics. Hallucinations resolved. Transitioned to oral nitrofurantoin to complete 7-day course. Discharged home with daughter. Offending medication discontinued. Follow-up with primary care and geriatrics. Discussion of advanced care planning initiated.

column4: Chronic low back pain and leg pain worsened
column5: 20-year history of low back pain, now radiating pain down left leg to foot (L5 distribution), numbness in left foot, weakness with foot dorsiflexion (foot drop), despite 6 months of physical therapy and epidural injections
column6: Obesity BMI 42, type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea on CPAP, hypertension
column7: L4-L5 Disc Herniation with L5 Radiculopathy and Foot Drop
column8: MRI confirmed large L4-L5 disc herniation compressing left L5 nerve root. Surgical consultation obtained. Underwent L4-L5 microdiscectomy. Intraoperative neuromonitoring used. Foot dorsiflexion strength improved from 1/5 to 3/5 immediately post-op. Aggressive physical therapy started on day 2.
column9: Foot drop significantly improved - 4/5 strength at 6 weeks, full strength at 6 months. Leg pain resolved. Returned to modified work at 8 weeks. Physical therapy continued for core strengthening. Weight loss program engaged - lost 30 pounds in 6 months. No recurrence of disc herniation at 1-year follow-up.

column4: Rectal bleeding and change in bowel habits
column5: Bright red blood mixed with stool for 6 weeks, stool narrowing, increased frequency (5-6 times daily from 1-2 times), mild lower abdominal cramping, fatigue and pallor
column6: Family history of colon cancer (father at age 60), had colonoscopy at age 50 which was normal
column7: Rectal Adenocarcinoma (T3N1M0, Stage III)
column8: Colonoscopy showed 4cm partially obstructing rectal mass at 8cm from anal verge. Biopsy confirmed adenocarcinoma. MRI pelvis for local staging, CT chest/abdomen showed liver lesion requiring biopsy - confirmed benign cyst. CEA elevated at 12. Tumor board: neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by surgical resection. Preoperative chemoradiation with 5-FU completed.
column9: Good response to neoadjuvant therapy - tumor downsized. Underwent low anterior resection with total mesorectal excision with diverting ileostomy. Pathology: ypT2N0 - significant downstaging. Adjuvant FOLFOX chemotherapy planned. Recovering well. Ileostomy reversal planned at 3 months post-op. Oncology follow-up ongoing.

column4: Acute scrotal pain
column5: Sudden severe left scrotal pain while sleeping 3 hours ago, nausea, vomiting, left scrotum swollen and exquisitely tender, no trauma, no fever
column6: Healthy, no medical history, athlete
column7: Testicular Torsion (Left)
column8: Clinical diagnosis made immediately. Ultrasound doppler confirmed absent flow to left testis. Immediate OR - manual detorsion attempted with partial relief. Bilateral orchiopexy performed. Left testis viable on exploration - detorsed and fixed. Right testis prophylactically fixed.
column9: Left testis viable and well-perfused intraoperatively. Discharged home day 2 with oral pain medications. Follow-up testicular ultrasound at 4 weeks confirmed normal perfusion bilaterally. Full recovery with normal testicular function. Educated on symptoms requiring immediate medical care.

column4: Shortness of breath and leg edema on exertion
column5: Progressively worsening exertional dyspnea over 1 year, bilateral ankle swelling, orthopnea (3-pillow), reduced exercise tolerance, recent syncopal episode
column6: Aortic stenosis (moderate-severe on echo 1 year ago), hypertension, coronary artery disease s/p CABG 10 years ago
column7: Severe Aortic Stenosis with Symptomatic Heart Failure
column8: Echo showed aortic valve area 0.7cm2, mean gradient 52mmHg, LVEF 45%. High surgical risk given age and prior CABG. Multidisciplinary heart team review. Patient deemed good TAVR candidate. Underwent transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) with successful valve deployment. Discharged day 2 post-procedure.
column9: Dramatic improvement in symptoms at 1 month - walking 1 mile without dyspnea. LVEF improved to 55% at 3-month echo. No paravalvular leak. On DAPT for 3 months then aspirin alone. Cardiology follow-up every 6 months. Quality of life significantly improved. Resumed gardening and light activities.

column4: Postpartum hemorrhage
column5: Delivered vaginally 2 hours ago, now heavy ongoing vaginal bleeding, uterus feels soft and boggy on exam, estimated blood loss 1200ml so far, lightheaded
column6: G3P3, all vaginal deliveries, large baby 9 lbs 2 oz delivered after prolonged labor, no prior postpartum hemorrhage
column7: Postpartum Hemorrhage secondary to Uterine Atony
column8: Bimanual uterine massage initiated. IV oxytocin 40 units in 1L running. Methylergonovine 0.2mg IM. Carboprost 250mcg IM (no asthma). Balloon tamponade placed. Transfused 2 units PRBC and 2 units FFP. Interventional radiology on standby. Uterine artery embolization performed after balloon failure. Bleeding controlled.
column9: Hemoglobin stabilized at 8.2 following transfusion. Uterine artery embolization successful. No further bleeding. Foley catheter monitoring urine output. IV iron started for anemia. Discharged day 4 with iron supplementation. OB follow-up in 2 weeks. Hemoglobin 11.0 at postpartum visit. Future pregnancy counseling provided.

column4: Sudden painless vision change
column5: Curtain coming down over left vision that came and went twice yesterday, returned this morning and is not clearing, no pain, no headache
column6: Atrial fibrillation on aspirin (declined anticoagulation previously due to bleeding concerns), hypertension, hyperlipidemia
column7: Central Retinal Vein Occlusion with Macular Edema
column8: Dilated fundoscopic exam confirmed CRVO with flame hemorrhages in all quadrants. OCT showed significant macular edema. Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection given immediately. Stroke workup: MRI brain normal. Cardiology consultation. Risk-benefit discussion of anticoagulation revisited - patient agreed to apixaban given thromboembolic risk.
column9: Visual acuity improved from 20/200 to 20/50 after 3 monthly anti-VEGF injections. Macular edema significantly decreased on OCT. Patient now on apixaban without bleeding complications. Ophthalmology follow-up every 6-8 weeks. Continued anti-VEGF treatment as needed. Long-term vision prognosis guarded but stable.

column4: Severe abdominal pain and vomiting in teenager
column5: Severe diffuse abdominal pain for 12 hours, multiple episodes of vomiting, fever 101°F, diarrhea started 6 hours ago, looking very unwell, parents report she had strep throat treated 2 weeks ago
column6: Generally healthy, takes albuterol inhaler PRN for mild asthma, no prior surgeries
column7: Peritonitis from Perforated Meckel's Diverticulum
column8: WBC 22,000 with left shift. CT abdomen showed free air and free fluid, segment of bowel with wall thickening in right lower quadrant. Emergency pediatric surgery consultation. OR: found perforated Meckel's diverticulum. Resection of diverticulum with primary anastomosis. IV cefoxitin started. ICU post-op monitoring.
column9: Post-operative course complicated by paralytic ileus resolving by day 4. IV antibiotics continued for 5 days. Tolerating clear liquids by day 5, advanced to regular diet. Discharged day 8. Incision healing well at 2-week follow-up. Full recovery at 6 weeks. Parents and patient educated on prior Meckel's presentations.

column4: Severe migraine not responding to usual medications
column5: Severe right-sided throbbing headache for 72 hours, nausea, vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, tried sumatriptan 3 times with minimal effect, lying in dark room unable to function
column6: Migraines with aura since age 15 (averaging 4 per month), anxiety, oral contraceptives (switching away from estrogen given migraines with aura)
column7: Status Migrainosus
column8: IV access obtained. Prochlorperazine 10mg IV plus diphenhydramine 25mg IV. IV magnesium 1g over 1 hour. IV valproic acid 1000mg. IV ketorolac 30mg. Fluid hydration. Headache broke after combination therapy at 4 hours. Neurology consultation. Occipital nerve block given. Discussion of migraine prevention.
column9: Headache completely resolved within 6 hours. No nausea. Discharged with rescue medication plan. Started on topiramate 25mg for prevention (titrated to 100mg). Oral contraceptives changed to progestin-only given migraine with aura. At 3-month follow-up, migraines reduced to 1 per month with improved quality.

column4: Acute hip pain and deformity after minor fall
column5: Slipped getting out of bathtub, no significant fall, right hip immediately painful, unable to stand, right leg appears shortened and externally rotated, severe pain with any movement
column6: Metastatic prostate cancer (spine and pelvic metastases known), on enzalutamide, osteoporosis, hypertension
column7: Pathologic Hip Fracture from Metastatic Prostate Cancer
column8: X-ray confirmed displaced right femoral neck fracture with lytic lesion at fracture site. Orthopedic and oncology co-consultation. MRI pelvis confirmed extensive metastatic disease. Discussed goals of care - patient wants to remain ambulatory. Underwent cemented hemiarthroplasty. Radiation oncology consulted for post-op radiation to hip.
column9: Surgery uncomplicated. Weight bearing with walker from post-op day 1. Post-operative radiation to right hip completed (8 fractions). Pain significantly controlled. Discharged to inpatient rehab day 5. Walking with walker at 3 weeks. Ongoing palliative care involvement. Continued systemic therapy. Hospice discussions initiated given overall prognosis.

column4: Severe cellulitis with fever and confusion
column5: Right leg redness, warmth, swelling rapidly spreading over past 24 hours, fever 104°F, heart rate 128, blood pressure 85/50 on arrival, confused, leg skin becoming dusky and blistering
column6: Type 1 diabetes on insulin pump (A1C 11%), chronic venous insufficiency
column7: Necrotizing Fasciitis (Type I, Polymicrobial)
column8: LRINEC score 10 - high risk. Emergency surgical consultation. OR within 2 hours: confirmed necrotizing fasciitis with necrosis tracking along fascial planes. Extensive surgical debridement of right lower leg - non-viable tissue excised. Started on broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin, meropenem, clindamycin). ICU admission. Vasopressor support briefly required. Return to OR on day 2 for further debridement.
column9: Survived critical illness. Infection controlled after 3 surgical debridements. Wound managed with vacuum-assisted closure. Skin grafting performed on day 14. ICU stay 8 days, total hospitalization 28 days. Discharged to wound care center. Significant permanent scarring. Diabetes management optimized. At 6 months, walking with minor functional limitations.

column4: Severe nausea, vomiting and abdominal distension
column5: Unable to tolerate any oral intake for 3 days, abdominal distension and pain, no bowel movement or flatus for 4 days, vomiting bile-colored fluid, hears 'tinkling' sounds in abdomen
column6: 3 prior abdominal surgeries (appendectomy, two cesarean sections), no recent dietary changes
column7: Small Bowel Obstruction (Adhesive)
column8: X-ray and CT abdomen confirmed small bowel obstruction with transition point in right lower quadrant, no free air, no ischemic changes. NGT placed for decompression. IV fluid resuscitation. Serial abdominal exams. Failed conservative management after 48 hours. Surgery consulted. Diagnostic laparoscopy: single adhesive band lysed.
column9: Immediate improvement post-operatively - passing gas within 12 hours. NGT removed day 2. Tolerating clear liquids then regular diet by day 3. Discharged day 5. Follow-up at 2 weeks showed complete recovery. Educated on symptoms of recurrence requiring immediate evaluation. No recurrence at 6 months.

column4: Chest pain, cough, night sweats, weight loss
column5: Progressive chest pain, productive cough with blood-tinged sputum for 6 weeks, soaking night sweats, 25-pound weight loss, fever, fatigue. Immigrated from Southeast Asia 1 year ago.
column6: No significant prior medical history, BCG vaccinated as child, works in crowded restaurant kitchen, shares apartment with 8 people
column7: Pulmonary Tuberculosis (Smear-positive, Cavitary)
column8: CXR and CT showed right upper lobe cavitation with surrounding infiltrate. Sputum AFB smear 3+ positive. Airborne isolation immediately implemented. Genotypic testing for drug resistance. Contact investigation initiated. LTBI testing of contacts. Started four-drug RIPE therapy (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol). Directly observed therapy (DOT) arranged post-discharge.
column9: Sputum culture confirmed M. tuberculosis sensitive to all first-line drugs. Clinical improvement by week 2. Discharged on day 14 on DOT program. Monthly follow-up with sputum cultures. Culture negative at 2 months. Completed full 6-month course. CXR improvement at 6 months with residual scarring. Contact tracing identified 2 LTBI cases treated preventively.

column4: Severe flank pain radiating to groin
column5: Acute onset severe right flank pain radiating to right groin and labia 2 hours ago, colicky in nature (comes in waves), nausea, vomiting, microscopic hematuria on UA in office
column6: Recurrent kidney stones (3 prior episodes), primary hyperparathyroidism treated surgically 5 years ago, chronic dehydration (drinks less than 4 glasses of water daily)
column7: Right Ureteral Calculus (8mm stone at ureterovesical junction)
column8: CT KUB confirmed 8mm right distal ureteral stone. IV ketorolac and ondansetron for pain and nausea. IV hydration. Tamsulosin started to facilitate passage. Stone too large for spontaneous passage - urology consulted. Underwent ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy and ureteral stent placement. Stone fragments sent for analysis.
column9: Stone fragments: calcium oxalate. Ureteral stent removed at 2 weeks. Follow-up KUB confirmed no residual stones. Dietary modification counseling: increase fluids to 2.5L daily, reduce sodium and animal protein, moderate oxalate restriction. 24-hour urine collection to guide medical prophylaxis - hypercalciuria identified. Started hydrochlorothiazide. No recurrence at 1 year.

column4: Altered mental status and liver failure signs
column5: Family brought in confused patient, jaundiced skin and eyes, ascites (distended abdomen), asterixis on exam, last drink was yesterday, drinks 1-1.5 pints of whiskey daily for 15 years
column6: Alcohol use disorder, hepatitis C treated and cured 3 years ago, hypertension, depression
column7: Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure with Hepatic Encephalopathy
column8: Ammonia level 142. Grade 3 encephalopathy. Lactulose via NGT. Rifaximin started. Paracentesis: 4L removed (SBP prophylaxis with cefotaxime given pending cultures). Coagulopathy managed with vitamin K and FFP for procedures. Nutrition support. MELD score 28. Alcohol counseling and motivational interviewing. Addiction medicine consultation.
column9: Mental status improved to grade 1 within 72 hours with lactulose. Discharged day 10 on rifaximin and lactulose. Referred to addiction medicine and transplant hepatology. Listed for liver transplant evaluation. Maintained sobriety - verified at follow-up visits. MELD improved to 22 at 3 months. Actively being evaluated for transplant.

column4: First manic episode
column5: Parents report 5 days without sleep, spending thousands of dollars online, grandiose plans to start multiple businesses, talking very fast, hypersexual behavior (unusual for patient), irritable when family tries to redirect
column6: No prior psychiatric diagnosis, recently started college, family history of bipolar disorder (father)
column7: Bipolar I Disorder, Current Episode Manic, Severe
column8: Admitted to inpatient psychiatric unit. Started lithium 300mg TID with titration. Olanzapine 10mg nightly for acute agitation and sleep. Lorazepam PRN for agitation. Thyroid and kidney function baseline. Neurological workup negative. Family education on bipolar disorder. Mood charting education. Social work assessment.
column9: Sleep normalized within 4 days. Racing thoughts and pressured speech improved by day 7. Lithium level therapeutic at 0.9 by day 5. Discharged day 10 with outpatient psychiatry follow-up in 1 week. Continued lithium and olanzapine (taper planned). Engaged in therapy. Returned to college with academic accommodations.

column4: Severe shortness of breath and leg pain
column5: Acute severe dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardic and hypotensive on arrival, left leg swollen for 3 days, recent 10-hour flight from Europe 4 days ago, right heart strain on EKG
column6: Hypertension, recent knee replacement surgery 3 weeks ago, BMI 34
column7: Massive Pulmonary Embolism with Hemodynamic Instability
column8: BP 78/50, HR 135. Echo showed right heart strain, RV dilation. CT PE confirmed massive bilateral PE. Systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100mg over 2 hours) given after assessment - hemodynamic instability present. BP improved to 105/68 during infusion. Heparin drip initiated post-thrombolysis. ICU monitoring. Doppler confirmed bilateral DVT.
column9: Hemodynamics stabilized within 4 hours of thrombolysis. Transferred to step-down on day 3. Transitioned to rivaroxaban. Echo at 1 week showed improved RV function. Discharged day 7 on rivaroxaban for minimum 3 months. Thrombophilia workup initiated. IVC filter placed given recent surgery. Extended anticoagulation planned.

column4: Limping and refusing to walk
column5: Gradual right hip pain for 3 weeks, started limping last week, now refusing to put weight on right leg, no trauma, mild fever, irritable, limited internal rotation on exam
column6: Previously healthy, no significant medical history, active child
column7: Septic Arthritis of Right Hip
column8: WBC 18,500, ESR 72, CRP 8.5. X-ray normal. MRI showed joint effusion with synovitis. Bone scan positive. Orthopedic emergency - OR for arthroscopic washout and drainage of right hip joint. Cultures obtained at surgery. IV oxacillin started empirically. Culture grew Staphylococcus aureus MSSA. Targeted antibiotic therapy.
column9: Excellent response - fever broke, pain improved. IV antibiotics for 10 days then transitioned to oral clindamycin. Discharged day 12. Physical therapy for range of motion. Serial inflammatory marker monitoring. At 6-week follow-up, full pain-free range of motion, running normally. No avascular necrosis on 3-month MRI.

column4: Confusion and high blood pressure crisis
column5: Headache, blurred vision, confusion developed over several hours. BP 240/130 on arrival. Husband reports she ran out of antihypertensive medications 2 weeks ago and couldn't afford refills. Papilledema on fundoscopic exam.
column6: Hypertension for 12 years (on amlodipine and lisinopril when adherent), diabetes, CKD Stage 2, prior stroke 3 years ago (no deficits)
column7: Hypertensive Emergency with End-Organ Damage (Hypertensive Encephalopathy)
column8: ICU admission. IV nicardipine infusion started - BP lowered by 25% in first hour (target: avoid rapid overcorrection). Creatinine acutely elevated 2.1 from baseline 1.4. Troponin mildly elevated. MRI brain showed posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Serial neurological checks.
column9: BP controlled within 24 hours. Confusion resolved over 36 hours. PRES changes resolved on repeat MRI. Transitioned to oral antihypertensives. Social work engaged for medication assistance program enrollment. Patient enrolled in patient assistance program - medications provided free of charge. At 3 months, BP well controlled and adherent to medications.

column4: Found unresponsive
column5: Found by roommate unresponsive on floor, empty prescription bottles of oxycodone and lorazepam nearby, slow irregular breathing, pupils pinpoint, cyanotic lips, last seen okay 2 hours ago
column6: Opioid use disorder, on buprenorphine (not recently taking), anxiety disorder on lorazepam prescribed by different provider
column7: Opioid Overdose with Respiratory Depression
column8: IM naloxone 0.4mg given twice by paramedics with improvement. IV access, supplemental oxygen. Naloxone 0.4mg IV in ED with full reversal. Monitored for renarcotization - required naloxone infusion for 6 hours. Toxicology consulted. Patient cooperative after reversal. Addiction psychiatry consultation obtained. Brief intervention performed.
column9: Medically stable within 12 hours. Agreed to addiction counseling. Restarted on buprenorphine/naloxone during hospitalization. Linked to opioid use disorder treatment program. Naloxone kit prescribed and training given. Benzodiazepine prescriber notified. At 1-month follow-up, maintained on buprenorphine, attending outpatient therapy.

column4: Sudden severe headache during exercise
column5: Thunderclap headache during spinning class reaching maximum intensity in under a minute, neck stiffness, brief loss of consciousness reported by class instructor, vomited once
column6: No significant medical history, OCP use, occasional migraines but nothing like this, family history negative for aneurysm
column7: Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)
column8: CT head normal. CT angiography showed diffuse multifocal narrowing of cerebral arteries consistent with RCVS. Lumbar puncture: xanthochromia negative, no RBCs (SAH ruled out). MRI brain normal. Oral nimodipine 60mg every 4 hours started. OCP discontinued. Avoidance of physical exertion counseled.
column9: Thunderclap headaches resolved over 2 weeks. Serial vascular imaging at 4 weeks showed resolution of arterial narrowing. No new headache episodes. Repeat MRI normal. Neurological follow-up at 3 months. Advised against oral contraceptive use and high-intensity exercise for 3 months. Full recovery with no residual deficits.

column4: Worsening confusion and falls
column5: Wife reports husband has been progressively confused for 3 months with visual hallucinations (sees cats in the house), stiffness in arms and legs for 6 months, had 2 falls, seems to fluctuate - some days much better than others
column6: No prior psychiatric or neurological diagnosis, retired engineer, hypertension
column7: Lewy Body Dementia
column8: Neurological exam showed mild Parkinsonism, cognitive testing MMSE 19. No DRE in CSF. DaTscan showed reduced striatal dopamine uptake bilaterally. MRI brain showed mild global atrophy. Started on rivastigmine for cognitive symptoms (avoiding antipsychotics due to sensitivity risk). Carbidopa-levodopa at low dose for motor symptoms. Fall precautions.
column9: Hallucinations decreased significantly on rivastigmine. Gait improved with levodopa. Fall prevention program implemented. Home safety evaluation. Caregiver education and respite resources provided. At 6 months, maintaining independence with supervision. Enrolled in dementia support group. Advance care planning discussion initiated.

column4: Severe sore throat and difficulty swallowing
column5: Severe sore throat for 5 days getting progressively worse, now unable to swallow saliva, muffled voice ('hot potato voice'), trismus, bulging visible in right tonsillar area, fever 103°F, neck swollen
column6: Recurrent tonsillitis (4 episodes this year), otherwise healthy, college student
column7: Peritonsillar Abscess
column8: Needle aspiration performed yielding 8mL of pus. Culture sent. IV clindamycin started. Follow-up next day: reaccumulation requiring incision and drainage. Throat culture showed Streptococcus pyogenes. IV clindamycin continued. Transitioned to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate on day 3. ENT consultation for tonsillectomy planning.
column9: Dramatic improvement in dysphagia within 12 hours of I&D. Tolerating oral liquids then solids by day 3. Discharged day 4. Completed 10-day course of oral antibiotics. Referred to ENT - tonsillectomy scheduled given recurrent tonsillitis and peritonsillar abscess. Procedure performed 6 weeks later uneventfully. No further tonsillar issues.

column4: Sudden onset back pain
column5: Sudden severe mid-back pain while reaching for something in the pantry, can't stand straight, shortness of breath with movement, no trauma, has lost 2 inches of height over the past few years
column6: Osteoporosis (on calcium and vitamin D only, declined bisphosphonates), heart disease, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, COPD
column7: Acute Vertebral Compression Fracture (T8) with Pulmonary Compromise
column8: X-ray confirmed T8 compression fracture with 40% height loss. CT showed no cord involvement. Severe pain limiting deep breathing. Pain control with acetaminophen and low-dose oxycodone (careful in COPD). Pulmonary function monitoring. Orthopedic spine consultation. Underwent kyphoplasty after INR correction. Immediate pain relief post-procedure. Bisphosphonate initiated.
column9: Pain improved dramatically after kyphoplasty. Ambulating with walker day 2. Pulmonary function stable. Discharged day 4. Bisphosphonate therapy started (alendronate). Endocrinology referral for osteoporosis management. Fall prevention program. Calcium, vitamin D optimized. Follow-up imaging at 6 weeks showed stable fracture with no new compression fractures.

column4: Weakness and fatigue with drooping eyelid
column5: Progressive bilateral eyelid drooping for 6 weeks, double vision especially in evenings, jaw weakness when chewing, difficulty raising arms above head, symptoms worse as day progresses and better in the morning
column6: Hypothyroidism, no other significant medical history
column7: Myasthenia Gravis (Generalized)
column8: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies markedly positive. CT chest showed anterior mediastinal mass (thymoma). Edrophonium (Tensilon) test positive. EMG/NCS confirmed decremental response. Initiated pyridostigmine 60mg QID for symptomatic relief. Prednisone 20mg daily. Neurology consultation. Thoracic surgery for thymectomy. Plasmapheresis pre-operatively.
column9: Significant improvement with pyridostigmine and prednisone. Thymectomy performed. Pathology confirmed thymoma B2. Post-operatively, significant improvement in strength. Monitoring for myasthenic crisis. Discharged day 8. At 3 months, significant reduction in symptoms. Prednisone taper ongoing. Continued on pyridostigmine. Follow-up with neurology and oncology.

column4: Type 1 DKA at diagnosis
column5: Extreme thirst and frequent urination for 3 weeks, significant weight loss of 12 pounds, now vomiting continuously for 2 days, confused, rapid labored breathing (Kussmaul respirations)
column6: No prior medical history, healthy active teenager, family history of type 1 diabetes (maternal aunt)
column7: New Onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus presenting as Diabetic Ketoacidosis
column8: Glucose 520, pH 7.12, bicarbonate 8, large ketones, anion gap 28. Admitted to PICU. Insulin drip started per pediatric DKA protocol. Careful fluid resuscitation to avoid cerebral edema. Electrolyte monitoring every 2 hours. Endocrinology consulted. Anion gap closed at 18 hours. Transitioned to subcutaneous insulin. Comprehensive diabetes education: carb counting, insulin administration, CGM training.
column9: DKA resolved without complications (no cerebral edema). Transitioned to basal-bolus insulin regimen (glargine + aspart). CGM placed prior to discharge. Diabetes education completed by patient and parents over 3 days. Follow-up with pediatric endocrinology in 1 week. A1C at 3-month follow-up: 7.1%. Well-adjusted with excellent self-management skills.

column4: Shortness of breath and non-productive cough after chemo
column5: 6 weeks after cycle 3 of carboplatin/pemetrexed for lung cancer, now with shortness of breath, dry cough, low-grade fever. O2 sat 88% on room air on exertion.
column6: Non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma) Stage IIIA on carboplatin/pemetrexed, 30-pack-year smoking history (quit at diagnosis), hypertension
column7: Checkpoint Inhibitor Pneumonitis (Grade 3)
column8: CT chest showed new bilateral ground-glass infiltrates. Bronchoscopy with BAL performed - ruling out infection. Diagnosis of immunotherapy-related pneumonitis confirmed. Chemotherapy held. Started high-dose prednisone 1mg/kg/day. Supplemental oxygen. Pulmonology and oncology co-management. Infectious workup negative.
column9: Significant improvement in dyspnea at 2 weeks of prednisone. Repeat CT at 4 weeks showed marked resolution of infiltrates. O2 requirements decreased. Prednisone gradually tapered over 6 weeks. Chemotherapy regimen changed. Pulmonology follow-up. At 2 months, off supplemental oxygen and tolerating modified chemotherapy.

column4: Severe hyperemesis in pregnancy
column5: 10 weeks pregnant, 3 weeks of nausea and vomiting, now vomiting 12-15 times daily, unable to keep any food or water down, lost 12 pounds, felt her heart racing, severe weakness, ketones in urine on office testing
column6: G2P1, first pregnancy complicated by mild nausea managed outpatient, no chronic medical conditions, works as nurse
column7: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
column8: IV fluid resuscitation with thiamine 100mg first (to prevent Wernicke's). Electrolytes replaced. Ondansetron IV 8mg, promethazine 25mg. Doxylamine-B6 scheduled. Dietary counseling: small frequent meals, avoid triggers. Methylprednisolone considered if refractory. Thyroid function normal. H. pylori tested negative. NG tube placed briefly for 24 hours.
column9: Vomiting reduced to 2-3 times daily with IV antiemetics. Tolerating clear liquids by day 2. Discharged day 3 on oral ondansetron and doxylamine-B6 scheduled. Gained 3 pounds over next 2 weeks. Monitoring fetal growth. Symptoms gradually improved by 16 weeks gestation. Full-term uncomplicated delivery.

column4: Severe pain in the big toe
column5: Acute excruciating pain in right first metatarsophalangeal joint, onset overnight after dinner out (steak, shellfish, multiple beers), joint red, swollen, warm, too painful to let sheet touch it
column6: Hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide, hyperlipidemia, obesity BMI 38, no prior gout
column7: Acute Gouty Arthritis (Podagra)
column8: Joint aspiration showed urate crystals (negatively birefringent). Serum uric acid 11.2. Synovial fluid culture negative. Started on indomethacin 50mg TID. Colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg. Joint injection with methylprednisolone. Renal function normal. Dietary counseling: purine restriction, alcohol reduction, increase hydration. Hydrochlorothiazide changed to losartan (uricosuric).
column9: Pain resolved within 48 hours. Full range of motion restored. Discharged day 2. Allopurinol 100mg started 6 weeks after acute attack (uric acid 8.5). Titrated to 300mg - uric acid 4.8. Colchicine prophylaxis during initiation. Dietary modifications maintained. No recurrence of gout attacks at 6-month follow-up.

column4: Chest pain and shortness of breath with nausea
column5: Vague epigastric discomfort radiating to jaw for 1 hour, diaphoresis, severe nausea, fatigue. Initially thought it was indigestion. Daughter insisted she go to ER. EKG changes on arrival.
column6: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes (A1C 9%), hyperlipidemia, obesity, never smoked, family history negative, no prior cardiac history
column7: Inferior STEMI (classic atypical presentation in elderly female)
column8: EKG: ST elevations in II, III, aVF with reciprocal changes. Aspirin and clopidogrel loaded. Heparin IV. Cath lab activated. RCA 100% occluded. Primary PCI with drug-eluting stent placed. TIMI 3 flow restored. IABP not required. Echo post-procedure: EF 50%, inferior hypokinesis. Dual antiplatelet, statin, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor started.
column9: Uncomplicated post-PCI course. No arrhythmias or mechanical complications. Discharged day 4 on dual antiplatelet, metoprolol, lisinopril, atorvastatin. Enrolled in cardiac rehabilitation. Diabetes management optimized. At 6-week follow-up, angina-free, EF improved to 55%. Completed 12-week cardiac rehab program.

column4: Severe menstrual bleeding and abdominal pain
column5: Heavy vaginal bleeding soaking 8 pads per hour for 3 hours, passing large clots, severe cramping, lightheadedness, syncope in bathroom. LMP was 2 months ago. Urine pregnancy test positive.
column6: No prior medical history, irregular periods since menarche, first sexual encounter 3 months ago, not using contraception
column7: Ectopic Pregnancy (Right Tubal, Unruptured) with Hemorrhage
column8: Hemodynamically unstable. Bedside ultrasound: no intrauterine pregnancy, right adnexal mass 3cm, free fluid. Beta-hCG 3800. Two large-bore IVs. Emergent gynecology. OR: Right fallopian tube ectopic, no rupture but active bleeding from distended tube. Right salpingostomy performed - ectopic removed, tube preserved. 1 unit PRBC given.
column9: Hemoglobin stabilized. Beta-hCG trending down appropriately post-op. Discharged day 3. Weekly hCG monitoring until negative. Counseled on future pregnancy risk and early ultrasound. Started on hormonal contraception. Emotional support and counseling provided. No further complications at 3-month follow-up.

column4: Painless rectal bleeding with weight loss
column5: Intermittent bright red blood per rectum over 3 months, attributed to hemorrhoids, but now also noticing fatigue, 10-pound unintentional weight loss, change in stool caliber
column6: Never had a colonoscopy despite being overdue, positive family history (uncle with colon cancer at 55), obesity, sedentary lifestyle
column7: Sigmoid Colon Cancer (T2N0M0, Stage I)
column8: Colonoscopy showed near-obstructing sigmoid mass. CEA 4.2 (mildly elevated). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative for metastasis. Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with en bloc resection. Pathology: T2N0M0 adenocarcinoma, all 18 lymph nodes negative. Stage I - no adjuvant chemotherapy needed. Surgical oncology and colorectal surgery team.
column9: Excellent surgical outcome. Returned to regular diet on day 3. Discharged day 5. Pathology confirmed Stage I - curative resection. CEA normalized at 6 weeks. No adjuvant chemotherapy needed. Colonoscopy surveillance in 1 year per guidelines. Lifestyle modification counseling. No recurrence at 12-month follow-up.

column4: Anxiety, heat intolerance, and heart racing
column5: Palpitations for 4 months, anxiety, feeling hot all the time, weight loss despite eating more, irritability, fine hand tremor, eyes appear more prominent than usual, very fine hair
column6: No prior medical history, two young children, works in marketing, symptoms onset during very stressful period at work - initially attributed to stress
column7: Hyperthyroidism secondary to Graves Disease with Thyroid Eye Disease
column8: TSH undetectable, FT4 markedly elevated, TSI positive. Thyroid ultrasound showed diffuse enlargement with increased flow. Ophthalmology consulted for proptosis - mild active Graves orbitopathy. Methimazole 20mg daily started. Propranolol 20mg TID for symptom control. Selenium supplementation for eye disease. Discussed radioiodine vs surgery vs medical management.
column9: Symptoms improved significantly within 4 weeks. Thyroid function normalizing at 8 weeks. Eye disease stable, not requiring orbital decompression. Patient preferred thyroidectomy for definitive treatment after full discussion. Total thyroidectomy performed at 3 months. Pathology confirmed benign Graves tissue. Hypothyroid post-op, on levothyroxine. Stable at 6 months.

column4: Difficulty breathing at rest and inability to care for self
column5: Progressive dyspnea over 3 months now at rest, confusion, unable to take care of himself, stopped eating, very weak, history of lung cancer treated 2 years ago with new pulmonary and liver lesions found on recent scan
column6: Metastatic NSCLC (progressive after 2 lines of therapy), COPD, CHF, CKD Stage 3, lives alone
column7: Progressive Metastatic NSCLC - Hospice Transition
column8: Goals of care discussion held with patient (when lucid) and daughter. Patient expressed wishes: comfort only, no ICU, no intubation. Palliative care team consulted. Comfort measures initiated: low-dose morphine for dyspnea, lorazepam for air hunger/anxiety, haloperidol for terminal agitation. Supportive positioning, humidity, fan. Family supported at bedside.
column9: Patient transitioned comfortably over 4-day hospital stay. Family present throughout. Died peacefully on day 4 with family at bedside and comfortable. Palliative care chaplain support provided to family. Bereavement follow-up arranged for family. Advanced care planning documentation honored completely.

column4: Swelling and pain of multiple joints
column5: Acute onset painful swollen joints: right knee, left ankle, both wrists, over 3 days, fever 101°F, feels sick. Had urethral discharge and burning urination 2 weeks ago that self-resolved, didn't seek care.
column6: HIV positive (on antiretrovirals, CD4 450, undetectable viral load), no other chronic conditions
column7: Reactive Arthritis (formerly Reiter Syndrome) - post-gonococcal
column8: Joint aspirates: inflammatory, no crystals, culture negative. Urethral swab positive for N. gonorrhoeae. HIV labs stable. Started ceftriaxone 1g IV daily for disseminated gonorrhea.
