Doxycycline for chlamydia co-infection. NSAIDs for arthritis management. Ophthalmology exam showed mild anterior uveitis - topical steroids started. STI partner notification.
column9: Arthritis improved significantly within 1 week of antibiotics. Fever resolved. Discharged day 5 on oral doxycycline to complete 7 days. Uveitis resolved with topical steroids. Rheumatology follow-up for persistent joint symptoms. STI counseling and testing. Full joint recovery at 6 weeks. HIV management continued without change.

column4: Confusion and weakness with abnormal labs
column5: Wife reports 2-week decline in cognition, weakness, constipation, excessive thirst and urination, loss of appetite, bones have been aching for months, calcium level flagged on routine bloodwork as 13.8
column6: Hypertension, CKD Stage 2, recently diagnosed with mild memory concerns - workup was begun
column7: Hypercalcemia of Malignancy (Multiple Myeloma)
column8: Calcium 13.8. Aggressive IV saline hydration 200mL/hr. Zoledronic acid IV given. Calcitonin for rapid effect. Hematology consulted. Serum protein electrophoresis showed M-spike. Bone marrow biopsy: 40% plasma cells. Skeletal survey showed lytic lesions. Diagnosis: Multiple Myeloma IgG kappa. Renal biopsy: myeloma kidney. Staging complete.
column9: Calcium normalized within 48 hours. Confusion resolved. Started on bortezomib, lenalidomide, dexamethasone (VRd). Enrolled in clinical trial. Autologous stem cell transplant planned after induction. At 3-month follow-up: VGPR (very good partial response), M-spike decreased 90%. Preparing for stem cell collection. Dialysis not required.

column4: Severe allergic reaction to medication
column5: 20 minutes after receiving IV ampicillin for a dental procedure, developed generalized urticaria, lip swelling, throat tightness, severe shortness of breath, hypotension (BP 75/40), heart rate 145
column6: No known drug allergies (first time receiving penicillin), asthma (mild, uses albuterol PRN), seasonal allergies
column7: Anaphylaxis to Penicillin (Ampicillin)
column8: IM epinephrine 0.3mg immediately. Ampicillin infusion stopped. IV diphenhydramine 50mg, methylprednisolone 125mg, ranitidine 50mg. 1L IV NS bolus. O2 via non-rebreather mask. Repeat epinephrine required at 5 minutes. Transferred to ICU for 6-hour observation. Biphasic reaction did not occur.
column9: Full recovery within 2 hours of epinephrine. Observation in ICU uneventful. Discharged with 2 EpiPens, medical alert bracelet, allergy documentation in chart. Allergist referral - confirmed IgE-mediated penicillin allergy on testing. Allergy clearly documented across all systems. Educated on cross-reactivity with other beta-lactams.

column4: Pressure ulcer with deep infection
column5: Transferred from nursing home with stage 4 sacral pressure ulcer, fever 103°F, altered mental status, BP 88/52, HR 128, wound with purulent drainage and visible necrotic tissue down to bone
column6: Paraplegia from T6 injury 12 years ago, type 2 diabetes, recurrent UTIs, malnutrition (albumin 2.1)
column7: Sepsis from Infected Stage 4 Pressure Ulcer with Osteomyelitis
column8: Blood cultures x2. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam). Aggressive fluid resuscitation. Plastic surgery and wound care consultation. OR: debridement with partial sacral bone resection - osteomyelitis confirmed. Wound cultures: MRSA and Pseudomonas. Targeted antibiotic therapy 8 weeks. VAC therapy. Nutritional support via feeding tube.
column9: Sepsis resolved over 5 days. Long course of IV antibiotics for osteomyelitis (8 weeks). VAC therapy facilitated wound healing. Transferred back to nursing home with wound care nurses. Pressure relief mattress and repositioning protocol implemented. Albumin improved to 3.2 with nutritional support. Partial wound closure at 3 months. Skin graft planned.

column4: Back pain and new leg weakness
column5: Progressive low back pain for 6 weeks worse at night, now unable to walk without assistance, bilateral leg weakness, numb perineum, no bowel or bladder changes yet. Lost 20 pounds. History of testicular mass treated 2 years ago - follow-up inconsistent
column6: Testicular germ cell tumor treated 2 years ago (surgery only, declined surveillance imaging), otherwise healthy, no follow-up in 18 months
column7: Spinal Cord Compression from Recurrent Testicular Cancer Metastasis
column8: MRI spine: multilevel thoracic vertebral metastases with T4-T6 epidural mass compressing spinal cord. Biopsy confirmed embryonal carcinoma (AFP markedly elevated). Emergency dexamethasone 10mg IV. Radiation oncology: emergent radiation to spine. Oncology: BEP chemotherapy initiated after radiation. Neurosurgery evaluated - radiation preferred over surgery given multilevel disease.
column9: Neurological function improved with radiation and dexamethasone. Leg strength recovered from 2/5 to 4/5. Completed spinal radiation. BEP chemotherapy well-tolerated. AFP trending down significantly after 2 cycles. Ambulatory with cane at 2 months. Continued surveillance with ongoing treatment. Serious but potentially curable given chemo-sensitive tumor type.

column4: Persistent low-grade fever and night sweats
column5: 6 weeks of low-grade fevers (99-100.5°F), drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss of 15 pounds, progressively enlarged neck lymph nodes, fatigue, no infections identified
column6: Hypothyroidism, seasonal allergies, no recent travel or sick contacts, never smoked
column7: Hodgkin Lymphoma (Classical, Nodular Sclerosis)
column8: Excisional biopsy of cervical node: Classical Hodgkin Lymphoma, nodular sclerosis. PET/CT showed bulky mediastinal and cervical adenopathy, no subdiaphragmatic disease. Stage IIA bulky. ABVD chemotherapy initiated (adriamycin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) x6 cycles. Fertility preservation: oocyte cryopreservation prior to chemotherapy.
column9: Interim PET after 2 cycles: complete metabolic response (Deauville 1-2). Continued 6 cycles ABVD. End-of-treatment PET: complete remission. Bleomycin discontinued after cycle 2 to reduce pulmonary toxicity. At 6-month follow-up, in complete remission. Regular surveillance imaging. Discussing possibility of consolidative radiation.

column4: Worsening shortness of breath and chest tightness
column5: Progressive exertional dyspnea, chest tightness, wheezing diagnosed as COPD 2 years ago, now hospitalized 3rd time this year for exacerbation. Severe breathlessness at home found by wife.
column6: COPD GOLD Stage III on triple inhaled therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA), hypertension, depression, 50-pack-year smoking history (current smoker)
column7: Acute Severe COPD Exacerbation
column8: FEV1 28% predicted when stable. SpO2 82% on arrival. High-flow oxygen titrated carefully to avoid hypercapnia. Systemic prednisone 40mg for 5 days. Nebulized bronchodilators every 4 hours. IV magnesium sulfate. Antibiotics (azithromycin - purulent sputum). ABG: mild hypercapnia. BiPAP started - averted intubation. Pulmonary rehabilitation referral.
column9: Avoided intubation with BiPAP. SpO2 stabilized at 90-92%. BiPAP weaned over 4 days. Discharged day 7 on home BiPAP nightly. Triple therapy optimized. Pulmonary rehab enrolled. Smoking cessation: varenicline started. Discussed advance care planning given severe COPD and recurrent hospitalizations. Home oxygen arranged.

column4: Fever and difficulty breathing in child
column5: Fever 104°F for 3 days, increasingly labored breathing, stridor at rest (concerning sound with breathing), drooling and unable to swallow, sitting forward in tripod position, very anxious, voice sounds muffled
column6: Vaccinated except - parents declined Hib vaccine due to personal beliefs, no prior significant illnesses
column7: Acute Epiglottitis (Haemophilus influenzae type b)
column8: Do NOT examine throat due to risk of complete airway obstruction. ENT and anesthesia called immediately to bedside. Taken to OR for controlled intubation under anesthesia. Direct laryngoscopy confirmed bright red edematous epiglottis. Intubated successfully. Blood cultures sent. IV ceftriaxone started. ICU admission intubated. Cultures confirmed H. influenzae type b.
column9: Extubated on day 3 after decrease in epiglottis swelling on repeat scope. Remarkable recovery. IV antibiotics transitioned to oral. Discharged day 6. Full recovery. Vaccination counseled strongly - parents agreed to catch-up immunization schedule. Hib vaccine given prior to discharge. No long-term complications.

column4: Productive cough and fever in immunocompromised patient
column5: HIV positive patient, recent CD4 count 45, not on antiretrovirals or prophylaxis, productive cough with white frothy sputum for 3 weeks, progressive dyspnea, fever, night sweats, O2 sat 88% on room air
column6: HIV (CD4 45, viral load 550,000 - off ART for 1 year due to 'feeling fine'), prior PCP prophylaxis (stopped with ART), distant history of latent TB treated
column7: Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PCP) with AIDS-defining illness
column8: CXR: bilateral interstitial infiltrates. LDH markedly elevated 580. Beta-D-glucan positive. BAL confirmed P. jirovecii. Started TMP-SMX 15mg/kg IV divided q8h plus prednisone 40mg BID (PaO2 <70 on ABG). O2 supplementation. ART held temporarily then restarted after 2 weeks of treatment. ID consultation. HIV Ryan White Program enrollment.
column9: Significant improvement by day 7. Prednisone taper over 21 days. Completed 21 days TMP-SMX. Transitioned to oral TMP-SMX secondary prophylaxis (continue until CD4 >200). ART restarted (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir). Discharged day 14. CD4 at 3 months improved to 180. Viral load undetectable at 6 months.

column4: Recurrent miscarriages and now blood clot
column5: Third pregnancy loss (all first trimester), now presenting with left leg DVT at 14 weeks pregnant. Had workup after 2nd loss showing elevated anticardiolipin antibodies.
column6: No other medical history, second-trimester loss age 28, recurrent first-trimester losses, currently pregnant with IVF conception
column7: Antiphospholipid Syndrome with DVT in Pregnancy
column8: DVT confirmed on doppler ultrasound. Therapeutic LMWH (enoxaparin) started - adjusted for pregnancy weight. Rheumatology and maternal-fetal medicine co-management. Weekly monitoring of platelet count. Antiphospholipid panel confirmed: lupus anticoagulant positive, anticardiolipin IgG elevated x3 confirmatory tests. Low-dose aspirin added.
column9: DVT resolved on 3-month imaging. Pregnancy continued. Fetal growth monitoring intensive. Delivered at 37 weeks via scheduled cesarean - healthy baby. Warfarin restarted postpartum transitioning from LMWH. Lifelong anticoagulation planned given APS with thrombosis and recurrent pregnancy loss. Rheumatology ongoing follow-up.

column4: Urinary incontinence and recurrent UTIs
column5: Presenting for evaluation of urinary incontinence (both stress and urgency), recurrent UTIs (4 in past year), pelvic pressure, and inability to fully empty bladder
column6: Post-menopausal (age 52), hysterectomy at 48, hypertension, osteoarthritis, BMI 27
column7: Pelvic Organ Prolapse with Mixed Urinary Incontinence and Voiding Dysfunction
column8: Urodynamics confirmed mixed incontinence and elevated post-void residual 220mL. Cystoscopy normal. Urogynecology consultation. Conservative management offered: pelvic floor physical therapy, pessary fitting. Patient declined pessary. Elected surgical management: robotic-assisted sacrocolpopexy with midurethral sling.
column9: Surgery performed without complications. Immediate improvement in urinary leakage. Post-void residuals normalized to <50mL. No further UTIs at 6 months. Pelvic floor PT post-operatively. Subjective improvement in quality of life. Continued pelvic floor exercises. Follow-up urogynecology at 3 and 12 months.

column4: Acute knee pain and swelling after sports injury
column5: Felt a pop in left knee when pivoting to kick a soccer ball, immediate swelling within 2 hours (hemarthrosis), complete inability to fully straighten knee, pivot-shift positive on exam, anterior drawer test positive, Lachman positive
column6: Active recreational soccer player, occasional knee aches, no prior major injuries, works as physical education teacher
column7: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) Tear with Medial Meniscus Tear
column8: MRI confirmed complete ACL tear and bucket-handle medial meniscus tear. Orthopedic consultation. Joint aspiration for pain relief. Given occupation and activity level, patient opted for surgical reconstruction. Underwent arthroscopic ACL reconstruction (patellar tendon autograft) with medial meniscus repair. Post-op rehabilitation protocol initiated.
column9: Progressed through 9-month rehabilitation protocol. Achieved greater than 90% limb symmetry index. Returned to recreational soccer at 10 months. Teaching duties modified during recovery. No instability episodes. At 1-year follow-up, full function restored with no knee instability. Continued preventive neuromuscular training.

column4: Breast lump found on self-exam
column5: Self-discovered right breast lump 3 weeks ago, hard and irregular, non-tender, skin dimpling overlying lump, nipple discharge (bloody), right axillary lymph node palpable. Missed last 2 mammograms due to pandemic.
column6: Family history of breast cancer (mother at 55, sister at 48), BRCA1 carrier known for 3 years (had declined prophylactic mastectomy), hypertension, obesity
column7: Invasive Ductal Carcinoma Right Breast (ER+/PR+/HER2-, T2N1M0, Stage IIB)
column8: Core needle biopsy confirmed IDC, grade 2, ER 95%, PR 80%, HER2 negative. MRI breast bilateral. CT staging negative for distant metastases. Multidisciplinary tumor board. Oncotype DX score 24 (intermediate). Neo-adjuvant endocrine therapy or chemotherapy discussion. Patient chose neoadjuvant chemotherapy (AC x4 then paclitaxel x12 weekly).
column9: Good chemotherapy response. Mastectomy performed after neoadjuvant therapy (patient preference given BRCA1). Pathology: near-complete response, ypT1N0. Contralateral prophylactic mastectomy performed. Bilateral reconstruction initiated. Adjuvant endocrine therapy (anastrozole x5-10 years). Genetic counseling for family. Ongoing surveillance with oncology.

column4: Gradually worsening tremor and stiffness
column5: Progressive right-hand resting tremor for 2 years, worsening, now also left-hand tremor. Stiffness and slowness of movement, masked face expression, smaller handwriting, soft voice, occasional falls, shuffling gait
column6: Hypertension, depression (treated with sertraline), no family history of Parkinson's, retired accountant
column7: Parkinson's Disease (de novo diagnosis)
column8: Neurological exam confirmed bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability. DaTscan showed reduced striatal dopamine uptake. MRI brain showed no structural abnormality. Neurology consultation. Initiated carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 TID - excellent response ('levodopa challenge'). Referred to physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy. Exercise program recommended.
column9: Dramatic improvement with levodopa - tremor and stiffness markedly reduced. ADLs improved. Exercise program (LSVT BIG and LOUD) beneficial. Depression management continued with sertraline. Driving assessment performed. Deep brain stimulation discussed for future if wearing-off occurs. Regular neurology follow-up q3-6 months.

column4: Rash and fever after camping
column5: Returned from 10-day camping trip in North Carolina 8 days ago, fever to 103°F for 5 days, severe headache, petechial rash appeared on wrists and ankles 3 days ago now spreading to trunk, muscle aches, fatigue
column6: Healthy, no medications, no chronic conditions, recalled several tick bites during camping trip
column7: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
column8: Classic triad: fever, headache, rash (centripetal spread). Immediately started doxycycline 100mg BID (do NOT wait for confirmatory testing). Platelet count 85,000, mildly elevated LFTs, low sodium. RMSF IgG/IgM serology sent. Rickettsia rickettsii IFA positive. ICU monitoring for vasculitis complications. No organ failure developed.
column9: Rapid improvement within 48-72 hours of doxycycline. Fever broke on day 3. Rash fading. Thrombocytopenia resolved. Completed 7-day course. Discharged day 5. Full recovery. Tick bite prevention education provided. At 2-week follow-up, completely recovered. Serology confirmatory titers resulted and confirmed diagnosis.

column4: Chronic fatigue and widespread pain
column5: 3-year history of widespread musculoskeletal pain involving all 4 quadrants of the body, persistent fatigue (sleeping 10 hours and still exhausted), poor sleep quality, cognitive fog ('fibro fog'), anxiety, multiple tender points on exam, extensive negative workup by PCP
column6: Anxiety disorder, history of childhood trauma, hypertension, BMI 29, insomnia
column7: Fibromyalgia Syndrome
column8: Comprehensive workup negative (ANA, ESR, CRP, thyroid, CBC, CMP all normal). Diagnosis of fibromyalgia established using ACR 2010 criteria. Multimodal treatment plan developed. Started duloxetine 30mg (titrated to 60mg) - dual benefit for pain and anxiety. Sleep hygiene counseling. CBT referral. Graduated aerobic exercise program. Referred to pain management clinic.
column9: Duloxetine provided 40% reduction in pain scores at 8 weeks. Sleep improved with CBT for insomnia. Exercise tolerance increasing gradually. Cognitive fog improving. At 6 months, functional improvement allowing return to part-time work. Continued multimodal management. Acknowledging it is a chronic condition requiring ongoing management.

column4: Severe eye pain and blurred vision
column5: Sudden severe right eye pain, redness, blurred vision, halos around lights this morning. Nausea and vomiting from pain severity. Feels like pressure behind eye. Had episode of intermittent blurred vision in evenings for past month that self-resolved.
column6: Hyperopic (farsighted), no prior eye conditions, African American male, age-related risk factors absent, family history of glaucoma (mother)
column7: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma
column8: IOP 58 mmHg right eye (normal <21). Corneal edema, fixed mid-dilated pupil. Immediately started: acetazolamide 500mg IV, timolol eye drops, pilocarpine 2% drops, prednisolone drops. IOP lowered to 28 within 1 hour. Emergent ophthalmology. Laser peripheral iridotomy performed bilaterally (prophylactic on left). IOP normalized to 14.
column9: IOP controlled after laser treatment. Vision recovered to 20/30 (some residual corneal edema cleared over 2 weeks). Started on timolol and latanoprost eye drops bilaterally. Follow-up with ophthalmology regularly. IOP consistently < 18. Family members advised to have angle assessment. No recurrence of acute attack.

column4: Sudden onset facial drooping and weakness
column5: Wife called 911 - husband suddenly had right facial drooping, slurred speech, and right arm weakness while watching TV. Symptoms began 35 minutes ago per wife. NIHSS 8 on arrival.
column6: Hypertension (poorly controlled), type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obesity, 50-pack-year history (quit 10 years ago), previous TIA 2 years ago on aspirin
column7: Acute Ischemic Stroke - Left MCA, eligible for IV tPA and thrombectomy
column8: Head CT: no hemorrhage, no large core infarct. CT angiography: M2 segment left MCA occlusion. IV alteplase 0.9mg/kg given at 52 minutes from symptom onset. Taken to cath lab: successful thrombectomy with TICI 2b reperfusion. NIHSS improved to 3. MRI confirmed small left MCA territory infarct. Admitted to stroke unit.
column9: Rapid neurological recovery - NIHSS 1 at discharge. Mild residual right-hand clumsiness. Dual antiplatelet therapy x21 days then aspirin alone (TIA was cardioembolic source not found). Intensive risk factor management: BP target <130/80, LDL <70. Enrolled in stroke prevention clinic. Carotid imaging negative. Cardiac monitoring: no AF found. At 3 months, NIHSS 0.

column4: Severe pelvic pain and heavy periods
column5: Debilitating dysmenorrhea starting 1 week before period (not relieved by OTC NSAIDs), pain during intercourse, progressively heavier periods with passage of large clots, pelvic pressure throughout cycle. Tried multiple OCP regimens with partial benefit.
column6: No prior surgeries, infertility workup in progress (trying to conceive for 2 years), mother had similar symptoms (history of hysterectomy for fibroids)
column7: Endometriosis Stage III-IV with Chocolate Cysts (Endometriomas)
column8: Ultrasound: bilateral ovarian endometriomas (right 4cm, left 3cm). MRI pelvis: extensive endometriosis confirmed. Laparoscopic surgery with bilateral endometrioma drainage and excision, extensive peritoneal disease treated with excision. Pathology confirmed endometriosis. Post-op GnRH agonist for 3 months. Fertility treatment resumed.
column9: Dramatic improvement in pain post-surgery. Dysmenorrhea significantly reduced. Resumed fertility treatment - IVF cycle initiated 4 months post-op. Successful IVF transfer, currently 8 weeks pregnant at last follow-up. Long-term suppressive therapy planned after completing desired family size. Regular gynecologic surveillance.

column4: Confusion and dark urine after starting new medication
column5: Started allopurinol for gout 3 weeks ago, then prescribed trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a UTI last week. Now brought in confused with dark urine, pallor, severe weakness, yellow skin
column6: G6PD deficiency (Mediterranean variant, known), gout, hypertension, CKD Stage 2, no prior hemolytic episodes
column7: Hemolytic Anemia (G6PD Deficiency) triggered by TMP-SMX
column8: Hemoglobin 5.8 (baseline 14.2). LDH 2800. Indirect bilirubin 4.2. Peripheral smear: bite cells, Heinz bodies, blister cells. Haptoglobin undetectable. TMP-SMX immediately stopped. Transfused 3 units PRBC. Supportive care. Offending agent identified and documented. G6PD enzyme activity confirmed low.
column9: Hemoglobin stabilized post-transfusion. Hemolysis resolved over 1 week. Discharged with comprehensive list of medications to avoid (oxidative stress triggers). All providers notified of G6PD deficiency via allergy alert. Hematology follow-up. Allopurinol continued (safe in G6PD). Patient educated thoroughly on condition and triggers.

column4: Decreased hearing and ear fullness
column5: Progressive bilateral hearing loss over 10 years, now significantly affecting daily life, difficulty understanding conversation in noisy environments, withdrawal from social activities, asking people to repeat, tinnitus
column6: Retired heavy equipment operator (40 years occupational noise exposure), hypertension, diabetes
column7: Severe Bilateral Sensorineural Hearing Loss (Presbycusis with Noise-Induced Component)
column8: Audiogram: severe SNHL bilaterally (left 70dB, right 65dB HL). Otoscopy normal. No correctable cause found. ENT and audiology evaluation. Cochlear implant evaluation initiated given severe loss. Bilateral cochlear implants recommended and performed sequentially.
column9: Right cochlear implant activated 4 weeks post-surgery with progressive programming. Excellent speech perception improvement - word recognition 85% at 6 months. Left implant surgery and activation 6 months later. Both implants providing significant improvement. Social isolation resolved. Reconnected with family and social activities. Hearing rehabilitation ongoing.

column4: Abdominal pain and elevated liver enzymes
column5: Right upper quadrant discomfort for 6 weeks, elevated liver enzymes found incidentally on routine labs (ALT 380, AST 290), jaundice developing over past week, itching, pale stools, dark urine. Non-drinker.
column6: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's), dry eyes and dry mouth for years, no alcohol use, no known hepatitis risk factors
column7: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)
column8: AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibodies) markedly positive. Liver biopsy: consistent with PBC stage 2-3. MRCP normal bile ducts. Sicca symptoms: Sjogren's syndrome overlap suspected. Rheumatology consulted. Started ursodeoxycholic acid 13-15 mg/kg/day. Cholestyramine for pruritis. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation. Osteoporosis screening (DEXA ordered).
column9: Liver enzymes improved 50% at 6 months on UDCA. Pruritis improved with cholestyramine. Sjogren's confirmed on labial gland biopsy. Continues UDCA with excellent biochemical response. DEXA showed osteopenia - started vitamin D and bisphosphonate. Monitored for disease progression. Hepatology follow-up every 6 months.

column4: Collapse during sports practice
column5: Collapsed during football practice, unconscious for 30 seconds, brief tonic movements witnessed. Coaches called 911. On evaluation: reports prior palpitations and one prior near-syncope during sports that was attributed to dehydration. Family history of brother who died suddenly at age 17.
column6: Otherwise healthy teenage athlete, no prior cardiac diagnosis, takes no medications, brother deceased age 17 (cause not clearly established)
column7: Ventricular Fibrillation Cardiac Arrest - Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
column8: Resuscitated by AED on field - 2 shocks delivered, ROSC achieved. Echo immediately: severe asymmetric septal hypertrophy (IVSd 22mm), LVOTO gradient 45 mmHg at rest. EKG: LVH, deep T wave inversions. Genetic testing: MYBPC3 mutation confirmed. ICD implanted. Beta-blocker therapy. Sports restriction counseled.
column9: Survived resuscitated VF. ICD implanted without complications. Discharged day 7 on metoprolol. Prohibited from competitive sports per HCM guidelines. Family screening: mother and younger sister identified as HCM carriers. ICD implant for sister also recommended. Genetic counseling for entire family. Enrolled in HCM specialty center.

column4: Muscle weakness and difficulty climbing stairs
column5: Progressive proximal muscle weakness over 4 months, difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs, raising arms above head. Heliotrope rash around eyes, violaceous papules over knuckles. Creatine kinase markedly elevated at 4800.
column6: No prior medical history, recently started exercising intensely (attributed weakness to overtraining initially)
column7: Dermatomyositis
column8: CK 4800. MRI muscles: bilateral symmetric proximal muscle edema consistent with inflammatory myopathy. EMG: inflammatory myopathic pattern. Muscle biopsy: perifascicular atrophy, DIF positive. ANA positive, anti-Jo-1 negative, anti-Mi2 positive. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET scan: no malignancy found. Started prednisone 1mg/kg/day. Methotrexate added as steroid-sparing agent.
column9: Muscle strength improving at 8 weeks with normalization of CK to 320. Rash improving. Prednisone taper begun. Methotrexate continued. Pulmonary function tests: normal (no ILD). Annual malignancy surveillance planned. Physical therapy for strength rebuilding. At 6 months, near-normal strength. Prednisone tapered to 10mg.

column4: Chronic diarrhea and weight loss
column5: 10-pound weight loss over 4 months, watery non-bloody diarrhea 6-8 times daily for 5 months, crampy abdominal pain, fatigue, noticed oily floating stools. Had cholecystectomy 2 years ago. Alcohol use: 3-4 drinks per day for 30 years.
column6: Chronic alcohol use disorder, cholecystectomy 2 years ago, type 2 diabetes, hypertension
column7: Chronic Pancreatitis with Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency
column8: CT abdomen: calcifications throughout pancreas, pancreatic duct dilation, pancreatic atrophy - consistent with chronic pancreatitis. Fecal elastase < 100 (severely reduced). 72-hour fecal fat test: markedly elevated. Started pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with meals. Alcohol cessation counseling. Nutrition support. Endoscopy for pancreatic duct decompression considered.
column9: Diarrhea improved dramatically with PERT initiation. Weight stabilized then increased 8 pounds over 2 months. Fat malabsorption resolved. Alcohol cessation: enrolled in program, 3 months sober at follow-up. Diabetes management adjusted (brittle due to pancreatic insufficiency). Pain management addressed. At 6 months, functional improvement, continuing PERT.

column4: Severe anxiety and heart palpitations after stimulant use
column5: Party drug ingestion admitted by patient (MDMA, cocaine, multiple energy drinks). Presenting with palpitations, chest pain, severe anxiety, agitation, diaphoresis, temperature 104.8°F, heart rate 182 irregular
column6: No prior medical history, occasional recreational drug use, anxiety disorder on escitalopram (serotonin syndrome concern)
column7: Sympathomimetic Toxicity with Suspected Serotonin Syndrome
column8: 12-lead EKG: SVT at 182bpm. Lorazepam 2mg IV for agitation and to slow rate. Cyproheptadine 12mg given (serotonin antagonist). Active cooling measures for hyperthermia. IV fluids. Continuous monitoring. Temperature decreased to 101°F within 2 hours. Heart rate improved to 100 bpm sinus tach. Toxicology consulted.
column9: Temperature normalized. Heart rate in sinus tachycardia 95-110. Agitation resolved. Monitored for 24 hours for recurrence. Brief counseling on drug interactions and serotonin syndrome risk. Escitalopram continued. Referred to outpatient substance use counseling. At 1-month follow-up, patient reports no further recreational drug use.

column4: Burning feet and numbness
column5: Burning pain in both feet for 2 years, numbness spreading up to mid-shin level, feels like 'walking on hot coals', worse at night, balance problems in dark, mild hand numbness. Diagnosed as 'neuropathy' by PCP without further workup.
column6: Type 2 diabetes for 15 years (A1C typically 9-10%), hypertension, obesity, metformin for 5 years
column7: Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy with Vitamin B12 Deficiency (Metformin-induced)
column8: HbA1C 10.2%. B12 level critically low at 98 (normal >200) - metformin use for 5 years without monitoring. Nerve conduction study: severe symmetric sensorimotor polyneuropathy. B12 injections IM weekly for 4 weeks then monthly. Diabetes optimization: changed to intensive management targeting A1C <7. Duloxetine 60mg for neuropathic pain.
column9: Burning pain reduced 60% at 3 months with duloxetine and B12 replacement. A1C improved to 7.8%. Numbness partially improved - B12 replaced. Balance exercises instituted. Foot care education. Fall prevention strategies. At 6 months, pain well controlled. Continued B12 monitoring with metformin. Gabapentin added for residual symptoms.

column4: Sudden flank pain and blood in urine
column5: Right-sided flank pain onset during gym workout, radiating to groin and right testicle, gross hematuria (bright red urine), severe nausea, temperature 100.2°F, already had one kidney stone 2 years ago (spontaneously passed)
column6: Recurrent nephrolithiasis, hyperoxaluria on prior 24-hour urine collection, works construction (inadequate fluid intake at work)
column7: Right Urolithiasis - Multiple Renal Calculi (5mm and 3mm)
column8: CT KUB: 5mm right ureteropelvic junction stone with hydronephrosis, 3mm mid-ureteral stone. WBC mildly elevated. Urine culture sent (fever + stone = obstruction until proven otherwise). IV antibiotics started empirically. Pain controlled with ketorolac. Urology consulted - placed ureteral stent given infection and obstruction. Definitive lithotripsy planned.
column9: Stent placed without complication. Fever resolved on antibiotics. Urine culture negative - antibiotics stopped. Ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy 2 weeks later - both stones treated. Stent removed. 24-hour urine: hyperoxaluria confirmed. Dietary oxalate restriction, calcium supplementation with meals. Target urine output 2.5L daily. No recurrence at 1 year.

column4: Memory loss worrying family
column5: Daughter brings in mother - progressive memory loss for 18 months, now forgetting conversations, getting lost in familiar neighborhood twice, unable to manage medications or finances. Mood changes (withdrawn, occasionally agitated). MMSE 18/30.
column6: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine - recent level normal), family history of Alzheimer's (mother)
column7: Alzheimer's Dementia, Mild-to-Moderate Stage
column8: Comprehensive cognitive testing. MRI brain: hippocampal atrophy, cortical atrophy consistent with Alzheimer's. Thyroid, B12, folate normal. RPR negative. Neuropsychological testing confirmed profile consistent with Alzheimer's. Started donepezil 5mg (titrated to 10mg). Memantine added given moderate stage. Family education on caregiving, safety, driving.
column9: Cognitive decline slowed (not reversed) on medications. Driving assessment - deemed unsafe - license relinquished. Home safety evaluation. GPS device for wandering. Finances transferred to daughter per patient's prior expressed wishes (had POA). Enrolled in memory care program. Caregiver support group for daughter. Advanced care planning completed.

column4: Severe abdominal pain after vomiting forcefully
column5: Several hours of severe vomiting and retching from heavy alcohol consumption at party, then sudden severe mid-chest and upper abdominal pain, pain radiates to back, worse with swallowing, saliva accumulating (not swallowing), rapidly deteriorating
column6: Heavy episodic alcohol use, otherwise healthy, works in finance
column7: Boerhaave Syndrome (Spontaneous Esophageal Perforation)
column8: CXR: left pleural effusion, mediastinal widening, pneumomediastinum. CT chest: free air in mediastinum, left pleural effusion with esophageal defect at GEJ. Emergency thoracic surgery. OR: left thoracotomy, esophageal tear identified and primarily repaired, wide pleural drainage, mediastinal washout. Esophagram on day 5 showed intact repair. NPO and TPN initially.
column9: Survived with aggressive surgical management. ICU post-operative course 7 days. Chest tubes removed by day 10. Transitioned to oral liquids on day 12. Discharged day 16. Soft diet for 6 weeks. Dysphagia improving. Alcohol use disorder treatment engaged. At 6 weeks, tolerating regular diet. Full recovery at 3 months.

column4: Progressive leg weakness and difficulty walking
column5: Bilateral lower extremity weakness progressing over 6 months, started with foot drop bilaterally, now using walker, fasciculations noted by patient and physician, speech slightly slurred, no sensory symptoms, no bowel or bladder problems
column6: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, retired teacher, no family history of neurological disease
column7: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)
column8: EMG/NCS: widespread acute and chronic denervation, fasciculations multiple regions (UMN and LMN signs). MRI spine normal. PFTs show early restrictive pattern (FVC 72%). ALS confirmed by El Escorial criteria. Neurologist. Started riluzole 50mg BID. Edaravone considered. Multidisciplinary ALS clinic: neurology, pulmonology, nutrition, speech, PT, OT, social work, palliative care.
column9: Enrolled in multidisciplinary ALS clinic. Riluzole tolerated. Adaptive equipment provided. Communication device arranged as speech changes. Advance care planning: discussion of non-invasive ventilation (patient accepts), feeding tube (patient undecided). Family support critical. Connecting with ALS Association. Clinical trial enrollment discussed. Ongoing compassionate comprehensive care.

column4: Skin rash spreading with swollen glands
column5: Started 3 weeks ago with small painless ulcer on right palm that has healed, now diffuse non-itchy copper-colored rash covering palms, soles, trunk, and face. Enlarged lymph nodes in neck, groin. Mild sore throat. Sexually active, multiple recent partners.
column6: No prior STIs by history, on OCP, no chronic conditions
column7: Secondary Syphilis
column8: RPR reactive 1:128. TPHA confirmatory positive. HIV test negative. Examination: condyloma lata in groin. Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM x1. Partner notification initiated. HIV counseling and PrEP discussion. STI panel complete.
column9: Rash resolved within 3 weeks. RPR at 3 months 1:8 (appropriate 4-fold decrease). At 6 months 1:4. At 12 months 1:2. Considered treatment response. Partners notified - 2 tested positive and treated. Patient started on PrEP. Annual STI screening. Counseled on safe sexual practices.

column4: Sudden onset double vision and drooping eyelid
column5: Woke up this morning with right eyelid droop and double vision, right pupil larger than left and not reacting normally to light, no headache, no other neurological symptoms, not a diabetic
column6: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, no prior neurological history, 40-pack-year smoking history (current)
column7: Right CN III Palsy - Posterior Communicating Artery Aneurysm (ruptured)
column8: Pupil-involving CN III palsy is aneurysm until proven otherwise. Emergency CTA: 6mm right posterior communicating artery aneurysm. Emergency neurosurgery. Endovascular coiling performed within 4 hours. Pre-procedural CT: no subarachnoid blood (unruptured). ICU monitoring. Nimodipine prophylactically. Coiling successful - aneurysm secured.
column9: No subarachnoid hemorrhage occurred. Aneurysm secured successfully. Right CN III palsy began improving at 6 weeks (pupil first, then ptosis and diplopia). Full recovery of CN III function at 4 months. Follow-up CTA at 6 months: no recurrence. Annual imaging surveillance. Smoking cessation counseled - varenicline prescribed.

column4: Fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold all the time
column5: Extreme fatigue not improved by sleep, weight gain of 20 pounds over 8 months without dietary changes, feeling cold when others are comfortable, dry skin, hair loss, constipation, depressed mood, slowed thinking, voice has become hoarse
column6: Prior thyroiditis 10 years ago (TSH normal at that time), depression in remission, hypertension
column7: Hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's Thyroiditis)
column8: TSH markedly elevated at 68 (normal <4.5). FT4 very low. Anti-TPO antibodies strongly positive. Thyroid ultrasound: heterogeneous echotexture consistent with Hashimoto's. Started levothyroxine 50mcg daily (cautious initiation given hypertension). Dose titrated by TSH levels every 6-8 weeks.
column9: Symptoms began improving at 4 weeks (energy first, then weight). TSH normalized to 2.1 at 3 months on levothyroxine 112mcg. Weight returned to baseline. Hair loss stopped. Mood improved (not requiring antidepressant restart). Follow-up every 6-12 months to maintain euthyroid state. Lifetime levothyroxine therapy established.

column4: Inability to urinate and severe lower abdominal pain
column5: Lower abdominal pressure and inability to void for 12 hours, previously normal urination, significant suprapubic pain and distension, no UTI symptoms previously, had been drinking large amounts of fluid at concert
column6: Generally healthy, recent use of over-the-counter cold medication containing pseudoephedrine and diphenhydramine for nasal congestion
column7: Acute Urinary Retention - Drug-Induced (Anticholinergic/Sympathomimetic)
column8: Bladder scan: 900mL. Urethral catheterization placed - immediate pain relief, 920mL drained. Urological evaluation. Urine cultures negative. PSA not indicated in 23-year-old. Cold medications identified as cause (both have urinary retention potential). Catheter removed after 24 hours - successfully voiding. Urodynamics arranged if retention recurs.
column9: Successful voiding after catheter removal. No recurrence at 2-week follow-up. Advised to avoid anticholinergic medications and decongestants. Urodynamic testing showed normal voiding. Discharged with education on offending medications. No underlying urological pathology identified. Full recovery.

column4: Confusion and fall with head injury
column5: Found on floor by home health aide. Unclear how long down. Head laceration, confused (MMSE 12 but baseline 22). Head CT shows subdural hematoma.
column6: Atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 3.2 on arrival), dementia (moderate stage Alzheimer's), hypertension, previous falls (2 last year), osteoporosis
column7: Acute Subdural Hematoma on Warfarin Anticoagulation
column8: INR 3.2. Emergency 4-factor PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) to reverse warfarin. Vitamin K 10mg IV. Neurosurgery consultation. CT showed 8mm right subdural hematoma without significant midline shift. Serial CTs - not expanding. Managed conservatively. Warfarin held. Fall risk assessment. Goals of care discussion with family.
column9: Subdural hematoma stable then slowly resorbing on serial CTs. Mental status returned toward baseline (MMSE 18 on discharge). Warfarin discussed - AF stroke risk vs fall/bleed risk. Decision: switched to apixaban (lower intracranial bleed risk) at reduced dose after 4 weeks. Fall prevention implemented. Discharged to skilled nursing on day 8 with plans to return home.

column4: Painful swollen knee after travel
column5: Right knee swollen, red, warm, extremely painful, unable to weight bear, developed 2 days after returning from Thailand 1 week ago. Also had several episodes of diarrhea during trip (resolved). Unprotected sexual contact during travel.
column6: No prior rheumatological conditions, healthy, active runner, on OCP
column7: Septic Arthritis vs Reactive Arthritis - Gonococcal Arthritis Confirmed
column8: Joint aspiration: turbid fluid, WBC 85,000, no crystals. Empiric ceftriaxone started. Cultures of joint, blood, cervix, urine: N. gonorrhoeae from cervix and blood. Diagnosis: Disseminated Gonococcal Infection with septic arthritis. Ceftriaxone IV 1g daily for 7 days. Sexual contacts notified.
column9: Dramatic improvement within 48 hours of antibiotics. Joint fully recovered by day 7. Transitioned to oral cefixime. HIV test negative - counseled. STI panel: chlamydia co-infection found - azithromycin added. Partners notified - tested and treated. At 4 weeks, full return to running. Counseled on safe sexual practices.

column4: Worsening shortness of breath and leg swelling in cancer patient
column5: Newly diagnosed stage IV ovarian cancer on first cycle of carboplatin/paclitaxel. 3 weeks into treatment presents with progressive dyspnea, right leg significantly swollen and painful, O2 sat 90%.
column6: Stage IV ovarian cancer (newly diagnosed), hypertension, prior DVT 5 years ago (on aspirin only after 3 months anticoagulation)
column7: Pulmonary Embolism and DVT in Active Malignancy (Cancer-Associated Thrombosis)
column8: CT PE confirmed bilateral PE. Doppler: extensive right leg DVT. Anticoagulation with enoxaparin 1mg/kg BID initiated (preferred over DOAC initially in active cancer). IVC filter not recommended. Oncology co-management. Chemotherapy continued after 5 days once anticoagulated. Transitioned to rivaroxaban 15mg BID (CHEST guidelines for cancer VTE).
column9: Dyspnea resolved. DVT improving at 4 weeks. Rivaroxaban continued with planned indefinite anticoagulation while cancer active. Chemotherapy ongoing. At 3-month CT restaging: partial response to carboplatin/paclitaxel. VTE clot resolved. Continued maintenance rivaroxaban. Oncology and hematology co-follow-up.

column4: Fever and ear pulling
column5: High fever 103.8°F for 2 days, crying and pulling at right ear, decreased appetite, fussiness especially when lying flat, decreased hearing per parents, no drainage from ear, recent upper respiratory infection resolved 1 week ago
column6: Recurrent ear infections (3rd this year), attending daycare with 30 children, bottle-fed until age 2, first-hand tobacco smoke exposure
column7: Acute Otitis Media (Right) - Recurrent
column8: Otoscopy: right TM bulging, erythematous, reduced mobility. Left TM normal. Amoxicillin 90mg/kg/day started (first-line). Acetaminophen and ibuprofen alternating for pain and fever. ENT consultation given recurrent AOM. Parent education on risk factors and modification. Tympanostomy tube evaluation planned.
column9: Improvement within 48 hours of antibiotics. Fever resolved. Feeding normalized. ENT scheduled - tympanostomy tubes placed (3rd episode this year plus daycare attendance). Parent counseled on tobacco cessation - referred to quit program. Daycare hygiene recommendations. Only 1 episode of AOM in the subsequent year after tubes.

column4: Sudden onset one-sided facial weakness
column5: Woke up this morning unable to close right eye completely, right facial droop affecting forehead and lower face, difficulty drinking from cup (liquid spills), unable to raise right eyebrow, tingling around right ear, no other neurological symptoms
column6: Generally healthy, minor viral URI resolving over past week, works as software developer
column7: Bell's Palsy (Idiopathic Facial Nerve Palsy)
column8: Peripheral CN VII palsy confirmed (involves forehead - rules out central cause). Lyme serology sent (endemic area). Head MRI normal. Started prednisone 60mg x5 days then tapered over 10 days. Valacyclovir added given severity and URI preceding. Eye care critical: artificial tears, eye tape for sleep, moisture chamber glasses. Eye patch for driving.
column9: Significant improvement at 2 weeks - able to close eye partially. Lyme serology negative. Gradual improvement over 6 weeks - 80% recovery. At 3 months, 95% facial function restored with minor residual asymmetry with effort. Eye fully closing. No synkinesis. At 6 months, complete recovery. Full prognosis was favorable given early treatment.

column4: Persistent cough with blood and weight loss
column5: 8-week progressive dry cough now with blood-tinged sputum, 15-pound weight loss, fatigue, right-sided chest pain, 45-pack-year smoking history, cough attributed to GERD for months
column6: COPD (moderate), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, 45-pack-year history (quit 2 years ago), reflux treated with PPI
column7: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (Adenocarcinoma, T3N2M0, Stage IIIA)
column8: CT chest: 4cm right upper lobe mass, right mediastinal lymphadenopathy. PET/CT: FDG-avid primary tumor and N2 nodes, no distant metastases. Bronchoscopy with biopsy: adenocarcinoma. Molecular testing: EGFR exon 19 deletion mutation found. PFTs: FEV1 58% - borderline resectable. Multidisciplinary tumor board: concurrent chemoradiation with durvalumab consolidation.
column9: Concurrent carboplatin/paclitaxel with 60Gy radiation completed. Significant tumor response. Durvalumab consolidation started (PD-L1 maintenance). Tolerating treatment well. At 6 months post-treatment: near-complete response on PET/CT. EGFR mutation: osimertinib offered after progression (if occurs). Currently on surveillance with excellent response.

column4: Severe palpitations and shortness of breath
column5: Sudden onset palpitations and dyspnea at work, feeling faint, HR 185 irregular on work AED check. Called 911. HR 190 irregular on EKG in ER. Never had palpitations before.
column6: No prior cardiac history, stress and caffeine use (8 cups coffee daily), recently started using stimulant-based weight loss supplement, hypertension undiagnosed until today (BP 165/95)
column7: New-Onset Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response
column8: Rate control with IV metoprolol 5mg x3 doses - HR decreased to 110. TEE: no thrombus. Cardioverted to sinus rhythm (electrical). CHA2DS2-VASc score 1 (hypertension) - anticoagulation discussion. Echocardiogram: normal LV function, no structural abnormality. Stopped weight loss supplement. Hypertension treatment initiated. EP consultation.
column9: Sinus rhythm maintained. Anticoagulation with apixaban started given 1 CHA2DS2-VASc point. Metoprolol started for rate control and hypertension. EP evaluation: no ablation recommended at this time (first episode). Caffeine reduction counseled (from 8 to 1-2 cups). At 3-month follow-up, no recurrent AF. Holter: sinus rhythm throughout.

column4: Sudden vision loss after birth control
column5: Sudden painless vision loss left eye (20/400 from 20/20) with relative afferent pupillary defect. Currently taking combined OCP. Recent migraine with aura history. MRI shows optic nerve enhancement.
column6: Migraine with aura (frequent), on combined OCP for contraception, no smoking, BMI 22, no clotting history
column7: Optic Neuritis (Demyelinating) - First Demyelinating Event (Clinically Isolated Syndrome)
column8: MRI brain: optic nerve enhancement left, 2 periventricular white matter T2 lesions (asymptomatic). LP: oligoclonal bands positive. Ophthalmology: RAPD left, optic disc swelling. High-dose IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x3 days. OCP discontinued (migraine with aura + OCP = increased stroke risk). Neurology MS clinic referral.
column9: Vision improved to 20/25 by 4 weeks post-steroids. Full recovery 20/20 at 3 months. Given MRI lesions + oligoclonal bands = high MS risk. Initiated disease-modifying therapy (ocrelizumab) after detailed counseling. No further clinical events at 12 months. MRI stable. OCP replaced with progestin-only method.

column4: Heartburn refractory to PPI and difficulty swallowing
column5: GERD symptoms for 20 years, on pantoprazole 40mg daily. Increasing difficulty swallowing solids over 6 months, now occasional difficulty with liquids, unintentional weight loss of 8 pounds
column6: Chronic GERD for 20 years on PPI, obesity BMI 40, no Barrett's screening done, hiatal hernia, hypertension
column7: Esophageal Adenocarcinoma (T2N1M0) arising in Barrett's Esophagus
column8: EGD: salmon-colored Barrett's mucosa with 3cm mass at GEJ. Biopsies: invasive adenocarcinoma. Endoscopic ultrasound: T2N1. PET/CT: no distant metastases. Tumor board: neoadjuvant CROSS protocol (carboplatin/paclitaxel + 41.4Gy radiation) then surgery. PEG tube placed for nutrition during chemoradiation.
column9: Completed neoadjuvant CROSS chemoradiation. Restaging PET/CT: good response. Ivor-Lewis esophagectomy performed. Pathology: ypT1N0 (significant downstaging). 18 lymph nodes negative. Nivolumab adjuvant immunotherapy started (PD-L1 positive). At 6 months, tolerating regular diet with modifications. Oncology and surgery follow-up ongoing.

column4: Severe allergic reaction to food at school
column5: School nurse called 911. Student in anaphylaxis after eating peanut butter cookie from birthday celebration at school (known peanut allergy, EpiPen at home not at school per nurse), progressively worse hives, lip swelling, stridor heard
column6: Severe peanut allergy (prior anaphylaxis at age 5), asthma (well-controlled), on OIT peanut protocol - missed last 3 doses
column7: Anaphylaxis - Peanut Exposure During Oral Immunotherapy Gap
column8: IM epinephrine 0.15mg (pediatric) by paramedic on scene. Repeat dose needed x1. IV access in ER. IV diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone, ranitidine. Albuterol nebulization for bronchospasm. Monitoring for biphasic reaction 6 hours. Parents called.
column9: Complete resolution after 2 epinephrine doses and adjuvant therapy. Discharged with 2 EpiPens. School 504 plan updated - EpiPen kept at school. OIT program restarted at lower dose after gap. Family education reinforced. At 3-month follow-up, maintaining OIT without reactions. School allergy action plan updated.

column4: Frequent falls and progressive weakness
column5: Multiple falls in past 3 months (4 falls), proximal muscle weakness making it difficult to rise from chair, bone pain in hips and pelvis, diffuse muscle aches, fatigue. Vegan diet for 5 years without supplementation.
column6: Autoimmune history: celiac disease (on gluten-free diet but not strictly), osteoporosis, lives in northern climate with minimal sun exposure, dark skin
column7: Severe Vitamin D Deficiency with Osteomalacia
column8: 25-OH Vitamin D: 7 (severely deficient). Calcium: 7.8 (mildly low). PTH: markedly elevated 285 (secondary hyperparathyroidism). ALP elevated. Bone density: osteoporosis T-score -3.0. X-ray pelvis: Looser zones (pseudofractures) - pathognomonic for osteomalacia. Ergocalciferol 50,000 IU weekly for 12 weeks then maintenance. Calcium 1500mg daily. Celiac strict dietary adherence.
column9: Remarkable clinical improvement at 8 weeks - bone pain, muscle weakness significantly better. No further falls at 3-month mark. Vitamin D level normalized to 45 at 3 months. PTH and calcium normalized. Bone density improvement at 1-year DEXA. Celiac compliance improved with dietitian support. Physical therapy for strength and balance.

column4: Productive cough and fever not responding to antibiotics
column5: Cough with greenish sputum for 3 weeks, treated twice with azithromycin and then amoxicillin-clavulanate without improvement, fever persisting 101-102°F, weight loss 8 pounds, night sweats, works as microbiologist
column6: HIV negative, no TB exposure reported, born in US, no immunocompromising conditions, completed graduate education in public health
column7: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever) - Pulmonary
column8: History: recent camping trip to Arizona Sonoran Desert 3 weeks prior. CXR: right lower lobe infiltrate with hilar adenopathy. CT chest: cavitary lesion forming. Coccidioides antibodies: IgM positive, IgG rising. Bronchoscopy: BAL confirmed Coccidioides spherules. Fluconazole 400mg daily started. Infectious disease consultation. CNS and disseminated disease ruled out.
column9: Significant improvement at 3 weeks on fluconazole. Fever resolved. Cough improving. Weight returning. Serial Coccidioides titers monitored. Fluconazole planned for minimum 12 months. At 6 months, near-complete radiographic resolution. IgG titers declining. Counseled on avoiding future heavy desert dust exposure. Full recovery anticipated.

column4: Bloody diarrhea and abdominal cramping
column5: Bloody diarrhea 8-10 times daily for 5 days, severe lower abdominal cramping, rectal urgency, unable to make it to the bathroom in time, low-grade fever 100.4°F, significant pain. No recent antibiotics. History of UC.
column6: Ulcerative colitis diagnosed age 38 (usually well-controlled on mesalamine), no recent medication changes, under stress (family illness)
column7: Ulcerative Colitis Severe Flare (Mayo score 9)
column8: Flexible sigmoidoscopy: severe active colitis with friability and ulceration. Stool cultures and C. diff negative. CRP 85, ESR 65, albumin 2.8, hemoglobin 9.8. IV methylprednisolone 60mg daily initiated. Nutritional support. CMV colitis tested (negative). No toxic megacolon on imaging. Gastroenterology. Biologic rescue therapy: infliximab 5mg/kg IV day 3 after steroid failure assessment.
column9: Infliximab induction response: significant improvement by day 6. Diarrhea decreased to 2-3 times daily. Rectal bleeding resolved. Transitioned to oral prednisone. Discharged day 9 on infliximab maintenance (every 8 weeks) plus mesalamine. Prednisone tapered. At 3-month follow-up, in clinical remission. Colonoscopy at 6 months: endoscopic healing.

column4: Severe headache and facial pain
column5: Daily debilitating right-sided facial and head pain for 3 weeks, multiple episodes daily lasting 15-90 minutes, pain described as 'like a hot poker in right eye', associated with right eye watering, red eye, nasal congestion on right side. Pacing during attacks - cannot stay still.
column6: No chronic medical conditions, works as lawyer under high stress, occasional migraine (completely different character from these attacks)
column7: Cluster Headache
column8: International Headache Society criteria met for episodic cluster headache. Oxygen therapy: 100% O2 at 12-15L/min via non-rebreather for 20 minutes aborts attack in 15 minutes. Sumatriptan 6mg SC for breakthrough attacks. Verapamil 240mg ER started as preventive therapy. Prednisone bridge while verapamil reaches therapeutic levels. EKG for baseline before verapamil.
column9: Attacks aborted with 100% oxygen and sumatriptan. Verapamil 240mg ER reduced attack frequency 80% after 3 weeks. Cluster period ended after 6 weeks. Verapamil maintained for 2 months then tapered. Oxygen kept at home for next cluster period. Educated on nocturnal triggers (alcohol, altitude, nitroglycerin). Neurological follow-up.

column4: Chest pain on exertion for 3 months
column5: Exertional chest pressure relieved by rest, occurring with 2 blocks of walking, no rest pain, no radiation, normal resting EKG, negative troponins x2, referred from PCP for further evaluation
column6: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, 20-pack-year smoking history (quit 5 years ago), father had CABG at age 60
column7: Stable Ischemic Heart Disease (Multi-vessel CAD requiring CABG)
column8: Stress nuclear test: large reversible perfusion defect. Catheterization: left main 60% stenosis, LAD 85%, RCA 70% proximal, LCx 75%. EF 55%. Multivessel CAD with left main involvement - CABG preferred over PCI per heart team. CABG x4 vessels (LIMA to LAD, SVG to RCA, SVG to OM, SVG to diagonal) performed.
column9: CABG completed without complications. Discharged day 5. Cardiac rehabilitation enrolled. Angina resolved completely post-CABG. EF maintained at 55% on 6-week echo. Excellent functional improvement - now walks 30 minutes without symptoms. Optimal medical therapy maximized. At 1-year angiography: all grafts patent. Returned to work at 8 weeks.

column4: Severe abdominal pain with vomiting in adolescent
column5: Periumbilical pain for 4 hours migrating to right lower quadrant, fever 101°F, one episode vomiting, anorexia. Labs: WBC 16,000. CT abdomen: abscess adjacent to appendix with adjacent stranding. No free air.
column6: No prior surgeries or hospitalizations, healthy previously, active in dance, no menstrual irregularities
column7: Perforated Appendicitis with Periappendiceal Abscess
column8: CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess: 45mL pus drained. IV piperacillin-tazobactam. Clinical improvement over 48 hours. Interval appendectomy planned for 8-12 weeks after abscess resolution. Appendix not removed acutely due to phlegmon. Weekly follow-up.
column9: Excellent response to antibiotics and drainage. Discharged day 5 after tolerating oral antibiotics. Interval laparoscopic appendectomy performed 10 weeks later - uncomplicated. Pathology confirmed perforated appendicitis. Full recovery. Returned to dance 3 weeks post-interval surgery. No complications.

column4: Worsening confusion and fever in nursing home
column5: Transferred from long-term care facility with 3-day history of confusion (above baseline dementia), fever 103°F, painful urination per aide, foul-smelling urine, signs of dehydration
column6: Vascular dementia (moderate), BPH with recurrent UTIs, chronic indwelling Foley catheter (placed 6 months ago), type 2 diabetes, CHF
column7: Catheter-Associated UTI with Urosepsis and Drug-Resistant Organism
column8: UA: pyuria and bacteriuria. Blood and urine cultures sent. Foley catheter changed. IV fluids. IV ertapenem started (history of ESBL UTIs). Cultures grew ESBL-producing E. coli. Targeted ertapenem continued for 14 days. Mental status improved to near baseline with treatment. Goals of care discussion with family regarding Foley catheter continued use.
column9: Urosepsis resolved over 5 days. Foley catheter removed - trial of void successful. Family and care team agreed to avoid long-term catheter. Alpha-blocker added for BPH. Discharged back to nursing care facility day 8. Infection prevention education. At 1 month, no further UTIs. Nursing facility trained on catheter care to minimize recurrence.

column4: Chest wall pain after minor trauma
column5: Right-sided chest pain after stumbling on stairs yesterday. Not shortness of breath, no hemoptysis. Pain significantly worsens with breathing, laughing, coughing. On direct palpation, point tenderness over right ribs 5-6 laterally. CXR: no PTX.
column6: Breast cancer treated with lumpectomy and radiation to right chest wall 3 years ago, currently on aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole), osteoporosis from estrogen deprivation, BMI 23
column7: Rib Fractures (Ribs 5-6, Right) on Bone-Compromised Host + Evaluation for Metastatic Disease
column8: CT chest: right rib 5 and 6 fractures confirmed. Possible lytic lesion at fracture site - bone scan and PET/CT ordered to rule out pathologic fracture. CT findings reported as possibly benign but bone biopsy recommended. PET/CT: no other sites of uptake. Rib biopsy: reactive bone with no malignant cells. Radiation-associated bone changes, not metastatic.
column9: Reassured - non-pathologic fracture in radiated field. Pain management: NSAIDs with PPI, lidocaine patch, intercostal nerve block provided excellent relief. Incentive spirometry to prevent pneumonia. Anastrozole continued. Bone-strengthening agent (denosumab) added given multiple osteoporotic fractures on aromatase inhibitor. Discharged day 3. Full recovery in 6 weeks.

column4: Severe leg pain and swelling after sport
column5: Severe left shin pain, massive swelling, tense compartments during soccer game. Unable to bear weight. Pain far out of proportion to injury mechanism. Paresthesias in left foot. Pulses intact.
column6: Healthy collegiate soccer player, no prior injuries, on OCP, no bleeding disorders
column7: Acute Compartment Syndrome - Left Anterior Compartment (Sports-Related)
column8: Compartment pressures measured: anterior compartment 55 mmHg (>30 above diastolic = surgical emergency). Emergent fasciotomy of all 4 compartments of left leg within 1 hour. Wounds left open. Return to OR for wound closure at 48 hours. Split-thickness skin graft for area unable to be closed primarily.
column9: Full neurological and vascular recovery. Foot sensation normalized within 24 hours of fasciotomy. Skin graft incorporated well. Physical therapy rehabilitation over 4 months. Returned to soccer at 6 months with no functional deficits. Residual minor scar at graft sites. Educated on recognizing compartment syndrome symptoms.

column4: Sudden onset vertigo and inability to walk
column5: Woke with room spinning violently, unable to stand or walk (falls to left), persistent nausea and vomiting, does not improve with position change. Headache at onset. Unable to touch her nose on finger-nose test.
column6: Hypertension (poorly controlled), diabetes, prior TIA 3 years ago, stopped aspirin on her own 2 months ago
column7: Posterior Circulation Stroke - Left Cerebellar Infarction (PICA territory)
column8: HINTS exam positive for stroke: abnormal head impulse test, direction-changing nystagmus, no skew deviation deviation. MRI diffusion: left cerebellar infarction. Not candidate for tPA (posterior circulation stroke > 4.5 hours from last known well). Aspirin restarted. Admitted to stroke unit. Neurology. Started on antiplatelet and statin. BP management.
column9: Vertigo and ataxia improved over 5 days. No herniation or hydrocephalus. Discharged day 6 to inpatient rehab. Gait and balance rehabilitation. At 3-month follow-up, walking with cane, minimal residual imbalance. Aspirin maintained. Hypertension management maximized (BP now 125/75). At 6 months, independent ambulation without assistive device.

column4: Painless hematuria
column5: Noticed blood in urine (no pain) on 3 occasions over 3 weeks. Smoker, worked in rubber manufacturing for 20 years. No dysuria, no UTI symptoms. PCP sent urology referral after UA confirmed RBCs.
column6: 40-pack-year smoking history (current), occupational chemical exposure (rubber/dye industry) 20 years, hypertension, BPH on tamsulosin
column7: Bladder Transitional Cell Carcinoma (Ta, High-Grade, Non-Muscle-Invasive)
column8: Cystoscopy: 2.5cm papillary right lateral wall lesion. Transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT). Pathology: high-grade urothelial carcinoma, Ta (non-muscle invasive, lamina propria not involved). TURBT specimen complete. Intravesical BCG immunotherapy initiated: 6-week induction course plus maintenance. CT urography: no upper tract disease.
column9: Completed BCG induction and 3-year maintenance protocol. Surveillance cystoscopy every 3 months for 2 years then every 6 months. At 18-month follow-up, all cystoscopies negative. No recurrence. Smoking cessation: nicotine replacement + varenicline, quit at 6 months. Occupational history documented for future risk monitoring.

column4: Pelvic pain and fever in teenager
column5: Lower abdominal/pelvic pain for 4 days, fever 101°F, vaginal discharge (greenish, foul-smelling), bilateral adnexal tenderness on exam, cervical motion tenderness, sexually active with 2 partners in past 3 months, no contraception
column6: No prior STIs reported, no prior pelvic infections, asthma (mild), last Pap smear normal at 16
column7: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) with Tubo-Ovarian Abscess (Right)
column8: TVUS: right tubo-ovarian abscess 4cm. Started IV cefotetan + doxycycline. GC/chlamydia NAAT: both positive. Metronidazole added. Failed outpatient treatment: fever persisting day 3. Interventional radiology drainage of TOA considered - abscess not decreasing. Laparoscopic drainage on day 4. Cultures from abscess: polymicrobial.
column9: Fever resolved after drainage and continued antibiotics. Transitioned to oral doxycycline + metronidazole to complete 14 days. Partners notified and treated. Counseled on long-term fertility implications and importance of contraception and safe sex. HPV vaccination completed. At 6-week follow-up: fully recovered, no abscess recurrence.

column4: Urinary retention and hip pain
column5: Unable to urinate for 10 hours, severe suprapubic pain. Also reports severe right hip pain starting 2 weeks ago, losing weight. Bladder scan 850mL. PSA markedly elevated at 892 on stat labs.
column6: BPH for 8 years on tamsulosin (previously helped urination), no prior malignancy screening for past 5 years, hypertension, type 2 diabetes
column7: Metastatic Prostate Cancer with Urinary Retention and Spinal Metastases
column8: Foley catheter placed immediately. PSA 892. Bone scan: extensive skeletal metastases including spine, pelvis, ribs. Transrectal biopsy: Gleason 9 (4+5) adenocarcinoma. MRI spine: T9 metastasis with cord compression risk. Emergency radiation oncology for T9 lesion (4 fractions). GnRH agonist (leuprolide) initiated. Docetaxel added (mHSPC).
column9: T9 lesion treated without cord compromise. Foley catheter maintained initially. Testosterone nadir achieved at 3 months - PSA decreased to 4.2 (dramatic response). Voiding improved - Foley removed. Continued on ADT + docetaxel. Bone health agent (zoledronic acid) added. Palliative care involved. At 6 months, stable disease with continued PSA response.

column4: Worsening swallowing and drooping facial features
column5: Facial weakness noted by family (bilateral), difficulty swallowing especially pills, bilateral eyelid drooping, mild weakness in arms, fatigue worsening over weeks. No family history. Preceded by diarrheal illness 3 weeks ago.
column6: No chronic conditions, backpacker, recalls eating home-canned vegetables from a trail crew cache 4 days before symptoms started
column7: Foodborne Botulism
column8: Classic cranial nerve palsies + descending weakness. EMG: incremental response pattern. Stool and serum sent for Clostridium botulinum toxin. Home-canned green beans identified as source. Antitoxin (BAT - heptavalent botulinum antitoxin) administered IV from Strategic National Stockpile. ICU monitoring for respiratory failure. FVC monitored every 4 hours.
column9: Antitoxin administered within 24 hours of clinical diagnosis - halted progression. FVC remained above 30% - intubation avoided. Toxin type B confirmed in serum and food source. FDA and state health department notified. Gradual neurological recovery over 6 weeks. Full recovery at 3 months. Public health investigation initiated.

column4: Confusion and inability to cooperate with exam after head injury
column5: Found in park by bystanders, possible assault, head laceration, confused and agitated, GCS 10, pupils unequal (right larger), posturing noted. Blood alcohol positive at 0.18.
column6: Unknown medical history, no ID available, toxicology screen positive for cocaine and alcohol
column7: Traumatic Brain Injury with Acute Epidural Hematoma
column8: CT head: lens-shaped right temporal epidural hematoma with 8mm midline shift, right uncal herniation early signs. Emergency neurosurgery. Craniotomy and hematoma evacuation within 30 minutes of scan. Middle meningeal artery cauterized. ICU post-operative monitoring. Intracranial pressure monitoring placed. ICP controlled with osmotherapy.
column9: Dramatic neurological improvement - GCS 15 by day 2. Excellent prognosis when treated rapidly. Social work engaged. Psychiatry: dual diagnosis. Housing instability identified. Discharged day 8 with outpatient neurosurgery follow-up. CT at 2 weeks: complete resolution, intact bone flap. Outpatient social services engaged. Substance use counseling initiated.

column4: Recurrent syncope and abnormal heart rhythm
column5: Three episodes of sudden loss of consciousness over 2 months, no warning, brief recovery, occurred during exercise on two occasions. Coworker witnessed: patient turned blue, stiffened then went limp.
column6: No prior cardiac history, healthy, marathon runner, no family history of sudden death, no medications
column7: Long QT Syndrome with Ventricular Fibrillation
column8: Resting EKG: QTc 540ms. Echo: normal. Holter: documented non-sustained VT. EP study: inducible VF. Genetic testing: KCNQ1 (LQT1) mutation. All QT-prolonging medications avoided/listed. Beta-blocker (propranolol) initiated. ICD implanted (secondary prevention). Competitive sports prohibited per HRS guidelines. Family screening.
column9: ICD placed without complications. No further episodes of syncope on propranolol. Family cascade screening: daughter found to be mutation carrier - asymptomatic, on beta-blocker prophylaxis. Patient counseled on long-term management, activity restriction rationale. Two ICD shock-free years at follow-up. Close cardiology monitoring.

column4: Progressive difficulty walking and balance issues
column5: Progressive gait difficulty over 1 year, using walker now, frequent falls, urinary urgency and incontinence newly developed, cognitive decline noted by wife (MMSE 22 from 28 last year), magnetic gait (feet shuffling, barely lifting off ground)
column6: Hypertension, prior meningitis at age 35, hypertensive emergency 8 years ago
column7: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH)
column8: Clinical triad: gait apraxia, cognitive decline, urinary incontinence (Hakim's triad). MRI brain: markedly dilated ventricles disproportionate to cortical atrophy, transependymal CSF flow. LP: opening pressure 18 (normal), removed 30mL CSF - marked improvement in gait immediately post-LP (tap test positive). Neurosurgery: ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed.
column9: Dramatic improvement after shunt placement: gait normalized significantly, urinary continence restored, cognitive improvement over 3 months. At 6-month follow-up, MMSE improved to 26. Using walker for safety only, not necessity. Shunt revision required at 9 months for obstruction - resolved promptly. Continues to do well at 18 months.

column4: Severe nausea and abdominal pain during fasting
column5: 33-hour intermittent fasting attempt, now severe upper abdominal pain, intractable nausea, vomiting, weakness, known sickle cell trait. Urine positive for glucose and ketones.
column6: Sickle cell trait (not sickle cell disease), celiac disease (gluten-free diet), intermittent fasting advocate, no diabetes
column7: Euglycemic Diabetic Ketoacidosis (EDKA) - SGLT2 Inhibitor-Associated
column8: Serum glucose only 95. pH 7.19, anion gap 24, large ketones in serum. Recent SGLT2 inhibitor use uncovered (empagliflozin obtained online for weight loss without prescription). SGLT2 inhibitor causing ketone body accumulation without hyperglycemia. IV dextrose + insulin infusion per DKA protocol (modified for euglycemia). SGLT2 inhibitor stopped.
column9: Anion gap closed within 20 hours. Full recovery. Educated on dangers of unsupervised SGLT2 inhibitor use and euglycemic DKA risk with fasting. SGLT2 inhibitor not restarted. Primary care follow-up arranged. Nutritional counseling regarding safe fasting practices. No metabolic complications at 1-month follow-up.

column4: Progressive shortness of breath and leg swelling
column5: Exertional dyspnea worsening over 3 months, bilateral ankle edema, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea x3 pillows. Echo ordered by PCP - called back urgently: EF 15%.
column6: Hypertension for 20 years (uncontrolled, BP averaging 180/100), no prior cardiac events, diabetes type 2, obese, non-adherent to medications
column7: Non-Ischemic Dilated Cardiomyopathy (Hypertensive Cardiomyopathy, EF 15%)
column8: Cath: no obstructive CAD. Echo: severely dilated LV, EF 15%, functional MR 3+, elevated filling pressures. Diuresis with IV furosemide - lost 12 pounds. Started on evidence-based HF regimen: sacubitril-valsartan, metoprolol succinate, spironolactone, empagliflozin. ICD/CRT-D implant consideration. Cardiac rehab. Medication adherence program.
column9: Symptoms improved significantly with diuresis and HF medications. Discharged day 7. CRT-D implanted at 3-month follow-up (QRS 148ms). Echo at 6 months: EF improved to 35% (reverse remodeling). Continuing to improve at 12 months (EF 48%). Medication adherence critical. At 18 months, near-normal EF 50% - rare but possible with appropriate therapy.

column4: Recurrent kidney stones and bone pain
column5: Third kidney stone episode in 2 years. Also has fatigue, bone pain, constipation, excessive thirst. Serum calcium: 12.2 on ED draw. Sister also had kidney stones.
column6: Recurrent nephrolithiasis (calcium oxalate), depression on sertraline, no other chronic conditions, family history of kidney stones
column7: Primary Hyperparathyroidism with Nephrolithiasis and Bone Disease
column8: PTH markedly elevated at 185 (normal <65). Calcium 12.2. 24-hour urine calcium elevated. DEXA: osteoporosis at age 29 (T-score -2.6 spine). 24-hour urine: hypercalciuria. Sestamibi parathyroid scan: right inferior parathyroid adenoma 1.2cm. Endocrine surgery: minimally invasive parathyroidectomy. Intraoperative PTH: dropped from 185 to 22 post-resection (confirmed cure).
column9: Calcium normalized to 9.2 within 48 hours post-op. PTH normal. Hypercalcemia symptoms resolved. No further kidney stones in 18-month follow-up. DEXA at 1 year: significant bone density improvement. Oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation post-op (hungry bone syndrome monitoring). Family members screened - mother found to have asymptomatic hyperPTH.

column4: Acute scrotal swelling and pain after injury
column5: Right scrotal swelling after being struck in groin during basketball. Severe pain, nausea. US ordered urgently.
column6: Healthy, recreational basketball player, no prior urological issues, takes ibuprofen occasionally
column7: Testicular Rupture (Right)
column8: Scrotal ultrasound: disrupted right tunica albuginea, heterogeneous echotexture consistent with rupture. Emergency surgery: right scrotal exploration. Testicular rupture confirmed, extruded seminiferous tubules. Primary repair performed - testis salvaged. Post-op IV antibiotics.
column9: Right testis viable and intact on post-op exam. Pain well controlled. Discharged day 2. Scrotal support and activity restriction. Follow-up ultrasound at 4 weeks: repaired testis with normal flow. Testosterone levels normal. At 3 months, asymptomatic and fully recovered. Prevented unnecessary orchiectomy with prompt surgical exploration.

column4: Worsening ankle swelling and shortness of breath
column5: Bilateral ankle swelling progressively worse over 3 months, worse with prolonged sitting in new work-from-home role, shortness of breath on exertion. TTE ordered: moderate pericardial effusion.
column6: Breast cancer on tamoxifen (5 years into 10-year course, in remission), hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, autoimmune thyroid disease
column7: Pericardial Effusion with Cardiac Tamponade Physiology
column8: Echo: large pericardial effusion with RV diastolic collapse (tamponade physiology). Pulsus paradoxus 18mmHg. Emergency pericardiocentesis: 600mL serosanguinous fluid. Immediate hemodynamic improvement. Fluid: cytology, culture, adenosine deaminase, TSH sent. Cytology: no malignant cells. ADA: negative. Culture: negative. TSH elevated - hypothyroid contributing.
column9: Levothyroxine dose optimized. Pericardial window surgery planned if reaccumulation. At 2-week echo: minimal residual effusion, no tamponade. At 3 months: trace effusion. Etiology likely autoimmune/hypothyroid pericardial disease. NSAIDs and colchicine added for anti-inflammatory effect. No recurrence requiring repeat drainage. Ongoing cardiology follow-up.

column4: Severe chest pain after cocaine use
column5: 28 hours of chest pain (intermittent) after using cocaine at fraternity party. Diaphoretic, tachycardic HR 115. EKG: ST elevation in V1-V4. Troponin rising. Admits cocaine use approximately 90 minutes before chest pain onset.
column6: No prior medical or cardiac history, athletic, no family history of early heart disease, first cocaine use admitted
column7: Cocaine-Induced STEMI (Coronary Artery Spasm/Thrombosis)
column8: Cath lab activated. Coronary arteries: diffuse spasm, thrombus in LAD. Aspiration thrombectomy, IV nitrates, IV calcium channel blocker. No significant fixed stenosis after spasm relief. TIMI 3 flow restored. EF 50% post-procedure. Aspirin. NO beta-blockers (use with caution in cocaine toxicity). Benzodiazepines for anxiety/sympathomimetic effects.
column9: Troponin peaked then trending down. No further chest pain. EF preserved. Discharged day 3. Substance use counseling initiated. Aspirin continued. Cocaine use cessation strongly counseled. At 3-month follow-up: asymptomatic, troponin normalized, repeat echo normal. Engaged in substance use outpatient program.

column4: Progressive right-sided facial pain
column5: Severe lancinating electric shock-like pain on right face, triggered by light touch, chewing, talking, or brushing teeth. Episodes last seconds but occur up to 50 times daily. Been suffering 9 months, tried multiple medications.
column6: Hypertension, diabetes, multiple sclerosis (diagnosed at age 54, PPMS, ambulatory with walker), on ocrelizumab
column7: Trigeminal Neuralgia (Secondary to MS Demyelinating Plaque)
column8: MRI: demyelinating plaque at right trigeminal nerve root entry zone - classic MS-related TN. Prior medication failures: carbamazepine (side effects), baclofen. Oxcarbazepine started (better tolerated than carbamazepine). Neurological: adjusted to 600mg BID. Neurosurgery evaluation for microvascular decompression or radiosurgery offered.
column9: Oxcarbazepine 600mg BID: 80% reduction in attack frequency and severity. Able to eat and talk without triggering attacks. Monitoring sodium levels (hyponatremia risk with oxcarbazepine). MS neurology follow-up. Radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) offered as option for incomplete control or medication intolerance. At 6 months, well-controlled on oxcarbazepine.

column4: High fever and severe joint pain with rash
column5: Spiking fevers to 104°F once or twice daily for 2 weeks, evanescent salmon-colored rash appearing with fevers, severe joint pain in knees, wrists, and ankles, cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, very elevated ferritin (45,000) found on labs
column6: No chronic conditions, works as grocery store employee, no travel, no sick contacts, no insect exposures
column7: Adult-Onset Still's Disease
column8: Extensive infectious workup negative. ANA negative, RF negative, HLA-B27 negative. Ferritin 45,000. WBC 18,000. ESR/CRP markedly elevated. Bone marrow biopsy: reactive changes, no malignancy. Echocardiogram: no pericardial effusion. High-dose NSAIDs initiated - partial response. Prednisone 60mg added. Rheumatology. Anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) considered for steroid-refractory disease.
