column9: Fever resolved on prednisone. Joint pain significantly improved. Ferritin trending down to 3,200. Prednisone taper begun. Anakinra added at 6 weeks for steroid-sparing. At 3 months: in remission with anakinra + low-dose prednisone. Steroid-free at 6 months on anakinra monotherapy. Rheumatology monitoring for disease recurrence.

column4: Difficulty swallowing medications and liquids
column5: Progressive dysphagia to solids for 18 months, now having difficulty with liquids, recent aspiration pneumonia 3 weeks ago, choking on liquids, drooling, no weight loss until recently (8 pounds in 2 months)
column6: Parkinson's disease for 6 years (on carbidopa-levodopa and pramipexole), hypertension, previous aspiration pneumonia
column7: Aspiration Pneumonia with Severe Dysphagia in Parkinson's Disease
column8: Video swallow study: severe oropharyngeal dysphagia with silent aspiration of thin liquids and delayed swallow trigger. Aspiration pneumonia: left lower lobe infiltrate. IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole. Aspiration precautions. Speech-language pathology: texture-modified diet (minced and moist), thickened liquids. PEG tube discussion.
column9: Pneumonia resolved on antibiotics. Accepted thickened liquids and modified diet. PEG tube discussion with family and patient: patient preferred to continue oral intake with modifications accepting aspiration risk. Parkinson's management optimized. Intensive SLP follow-up. Palliative care consultation. Advance care planning: patient expressed wishes regarding future hospitalizations and ventilation.

column4: Severe right lower back pain with fever
column5: Sudden severe right flank pain (constant, not colicky), high fever 104.5°F, rigors, nausea, vomiting, costovertebral angle tenderness, looks toxic. Urine dipstick: large leukocytes, nitrite positive, large blood.
column6: G1P0 (20 weeks pregnant), no prior UTIs, group B Streptococcus negative on earlier screening, mild iron deficiency anemia
column7: Pyelonephritis in Pregnancy with Bacteremia
column8: Urine culture and blood cultures sent. IV ceftriaxone started. Aggressive IV hydration. Fetal monitoring: reassuring tracing. Labs: WBC 22,000, lactate 2.1. Blood culture: E. coli bacteremic. Renal ultrasound: right hydronephrosis (physiologic in pregnancy) - no abscess or obstruction. 48 hours IV then transition to oral antibiotics after sensitivity results.
column9: Clinical improvement within 24 hours. Blood culture sensitivity: E. coli susceptible to cephalosporins. Completed 14-day total course (7 days IV, 7 days oral cefdinir). Fetal wellbeing maintained throughout. Daily fetal monitoring during hospitalization. Nitrofurantoin suppression for remainder of pregnancy. Delivered healthy baby at 38 weeks. No recurrence.

column4: Confusion and memory problems following cardiac surgery
column5: Status post CABG 5 days ago, now with confusion, inability to form new memories, confabulating, visual hallucinations. Wife very distressed. Neuropsych testing: profound anterograde amnesia.
column6: CABG performed 5 days ago for stable CAD, hypertension, on thiamine 0 per nutritional review, admitted poor oral intake pre-op, occasional alcohol use (3-4 drinks/night)
column7: Wernicke's Encephalopathy (Post-Cardiac Surgery Thiamine Deficiency)
column8: Triad: confusion, ophthalmoplegia (noted on careful exam - lateral gaze palsy subtle), ataxia. Thiamine level sent (low). HIGH-DOSE IV THIAMINE 500mg TID for 3 days then BID for 7 more days. Glucose avoided until thiamine given (worsens Wernicke's). MRI brain: characteristic mammillary body and thalamic T2 changes. Alcoholic history further explored.
column9: Dramatic improvement within 48 hours of IV thiamine - confusion largely resolved. Ophthalmoplegia cleared by day 3. Memory improving. MRI changes resolving at 2 weeks. Discharged on oral thiamine. Honest alcohol use history revealed: drinking 8-10 drinks nightly - referred to addiction medicine. Thiamine deficiency caught before Korsakoff's syndrome developed.

column4: Widespread itching and abnormal liver tests
column5: Intense itching all over body worse at night for 6 weeks, no rash, fatigue, abnormal liver tests found on routine bloodwork (elevated ALP 380, GGT 420, normal AST/ALT initially), no jaundice yet, no alcohol
column6: IBD (Ulcerative colitis, currently in remission on mesalamine), no other liver conditions, non-smoker, no alcohol
column7: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) Associated with Ulcerative Colitis
column8: MRCP: diffuse intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile duct stricturing with beading pattern - classic PSC. ANA positive weakly. AMA negative. Liver biopsy: periductal fibrosis (onion skin), stage 2. GI/Hepatology. Ursodeoxycholic acid (no longer recommended but discussed). Cholestyramine for pruritis. Discussed liver transplant listing criteria. Colorectal cancer surveillance (PSC+UC = high risk).
column9: Pruritis improved with cholestyramine and rifampicin added. LFTs monitored. Colonoscopy: UC remains in remission, no dysplasia. Annual colonoscopy surveillance given PSC+UC. Transplant hepatology referral. MELD score currently 8 - stable. Quarterly monitoring of LFTs. Enrolled in PSC registry research. Lifestyle optimization.

column4: Leg weakness and paralysis after aortic surgery
column5: Underwent elective endovascular aortic aneurysm repair (EVAR) 12 hours ago, now unable to move both legs, absent sensation from mid-chest down, bladder and bowel dysfunctional. BP 95/60.
column6: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (8cm), hypertension, CKD Stage 2, peripheral vascular disease, 50-pack-year history (quit 2 years ago)
column7: Spinal Cord Ischemia Complicating EVAR
column8: Emergency: BP raised with vasopressors (MAP target >85 for spinal cord perfusion). CSF drainage catheter placed - CSF pressure reduced to 10cmH2O. Emergent MRI spine: T2 hyperintensity anterior spinal cord - anterior cord syndrome pattern. High-dose methylprednisolone (no proven benefit but used). Vascular surgery and neurosurgery emergency consultation.
column9: Partial neurological recovery with CSF drainage and MAP optimization. Lower extremity strength: from 0/5 to 2/5 proximally at 48 hours. Some bladder function returned. Aggressive inpatient rehabilitation. At 6-month follow-up: ambulatory with walker, bowel care program effective, bladder function partial (intermittent catheterization). Adapting with rehabilitation support.

column4: Painless lump in neck for 3 months
column5: Noticed enlarged right neck lymph node 3 months ago - initially thought it was an infection but hasn't gone away. Now 2 lymph nodes on right neck palpable, no pain, no fever, no night sweats, no weight loss.
column6: EBV mononucleosis at age 18, otherwise healthy, no travel, no pets, non-smoker, no alcohol
column7: Follicular Lymphoma (Grade 1, Stage II)
column8: Excisional biopsy: follicular lymphoma, grade 1. PET/CT: bilateral cervical and mediastinal adenopathy only - Stage II. Bone marrow biopsy: 5% involvement. FLIPI score 1 (low risk). Multidisciplinary tumor board. Given low-grade histology, low tumor burden, Stage II: watch and wait strategy vs localized radiation vs rituximab. Patient well-informed, chose watch and wait.
column9: Enrolled in active surveillance. PET/CT every 6 months. No progression at 12 months. Educating patient on signs of transformation (rapid growth, constitutional symptoms). Psychological support for 'living with cancer' anxiety addressed. Clinical trial enrollment discussed. Quality of life excellent at 18-month surveillance.

column4: Severe sunburn-like rash and mucous membrane involvement
column5: Diffuse tender erythematous rash starting face and trunk, now involving 25% body surface area, mucous membranes severely involved (mouth, eyes, genitals), skin peeling off in sheets, started taking trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for UTI 10 days ago
column6: HIV positive (CD4 320, on efavirenz-based ART), no prior drug reactions, hypertension
column7: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome/Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (SJS-TEN) from TMP-SMX
column8: TMP-SMX immediately stopped. Burn unit consultation: 28% BSA involved (TEN diagnosis). Dermatology and ophthalmology. Admit to ICU/burn unit. IV immunoglobulin 1g/kg x3 days (cyclosporine and corticosteroids controversial). Wound care twice daily. Aggressive fluid management. Enteral nutrition. Eye care: ophthalmic lubricants, eyelid separation.
column9: Survived with intensive burn unit care. Epidermal regrowth beginning at 2 weeks. Transferred out of ICU at day 14. Significant scarring. Ophthalmologic complications (symblepharon) requiring long-term ocular management. Discharged day 28 to wound care. ART regimen changed to avoid sulfonamide cross-reactors. Full allergy alert in all systems.

column4: Vision problems and headache in one eye
column5: Temporal headache on right side for 3 weeks, scalp tenderness when brushing hair, jaw pain when chewing, new onset vision changes right eye (blurred), ESR 95 found on routine labs, CRP 12, age at highest risk
column6: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, rheumatoid arthritis on low-dose prednisone 5mg, prior normal temporal artery biopsy 10 years ago (headache thought to be migraine)
column7: Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) with Vision Threat
column8: ESR 95, CRP 12, platelets 480. Temporal artery thickened, tender, nodular on right. IMMEDIATE high-dose prednisone 60mg daily (vision loss risk - never delay for biopsy!). Temporal artery biopsy within 72 hours: positive for giant cell arteritis with giant cells and intimal hyperplasia. Ophthalmology: early ischemic optic neuropathy right eye.
column9: Vision stabilized - no further deterioration after steroid initiation. Maintained 20/40 right eye (some permanent damage before treatment). Prednisone 60mg continued for 4 weeks then gradual taper over 12-18 months guided by symptoms and ESR/CRP. Tocilizumab (IL-6 inhibitor) added for steroid-sparing. Bone protection. At 12 months, prednisone 10mg, disease controlled.

column4: Severe joint pain after sore throat
column5: Bilateral knee swelling and pain, right ankle pain, left wrist swelling starting 3 weeks after untreated strep throat in a developing country setting. Now in US for work. Fever 101°F, new heart murmur on exam.
column6: No chronic conditions, lives and works in US for 2 years, originally from sub-Saharan Africa, no prior rheumatic fever, no prior antibiotic access
column7: Acute Rheumatic Fever with Carditis
column8: ASO titer markedly elevated 850. Echo: mitral regurgitation (new), thickened mitral valve leaflets - carditis confirmed. Jones criteria: 2 major (carditis + migratory polyarthritis), 1 minor (fever) + positive streptococcal evidence = definitive ARF. Benzathine penicillin G 1.2M units IM given. Aspirin 100mg/kg/day for arthritis. Monthly penicillin prophylaxis long-term.
column9: Arthritis resolved dramatically within 48 hours of aspirin. Fever resolved. Carditis monitored with serial echos. Mitral regurgitation mild and stable at 1 month. Long-term penicillin prophylaxis: benzathine PCN G IM every 4 weeks (10 years minimum or age 40, given carditis). Rheumatic heart disease clinic follow-up. Educated on sore throat treatment importance.

column4: Unresponsive at nursing home
column5: Nursing home staff called 911 - patient found unresponsive, no breathing, no pulse. CPR initiated by nursing staff for 3 minutes. EMS continued CPR. Initial rhythm: pulseless electrical activity. Family living in another state, healthcare proxy unknown.
column6: Advanced dementia (severe, bedridden for 1 year), malnutrition (albumin 1.8), end-stage CKD (on HD 3x/week), CHF (EF 20%), 88 years old, multiple prior hospitalizations
column7: Cardiopulmonary Arrest - End-Stage Multiple Organ Failure (DNAR status not established)
column8: CPR continued per protocol. ROSC achieved after 18 minutes. Intubated and on vasopressors. Family contacted - daughter arrived. Goals of care discussion held: patient had previously expressed 'not wanting to be kept alive by machines' to family but no documentation. Family decisions: transition to comfort-focused care after ICU day 2 discussion.
column9: After two days in ICU on maximal support with no meaningful recovery and consistent with patient's expressed wishes, family agreed to withdraw life-sustaining treatment. Patient died comfortably with family present on day 3. Palliative care supported family throughout. Case prompted facility to implement systematic POLST completion for all residents.

column4: Painful skin lesions and mouth sores
column5: Painful oral ulcers for 6 weeks (cannot eat solid food), now developing painful blistering skin lesions on trunk and scalp, some lesions weeping and crusting. Skin biopsy from PCP showed acantholysis.
column6: No prior autoimmune history, Jewish ancestry, hypertension, takes lisinopril
column7: Pemphigus Vulgaris
column8: Desmoglein 1 and 3 antibodies markedly elevated. Direct immunofluorescence: IgG fishnet pattern (pathognomonic for pemphigus vulgaris). Dermatology and oral surgery consultation. High-dose prednisone 1mg/kg. Rituximab 1000mg IV x2 doses (2 weeks apart) for steroid-sparing. Wound care. Oral clobetasol gel. Nutritional support: liquid supplements.
column9: Oral lesions improving at 4 weeks. Skin blisters healing. Desmoglein antibodies decreasing at 3 months. Prednisone taper initiated after rituximab. In clinical remission at 6 months on low-dose prednisone. Rituximab retreatment planned at 18-month mark. Monitoring for rituximab infections complications. PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX given immunosuppression.

column4: Blood sugar over 1000 and confusion
column5: Husband called 911: wife has been drinking a lot and urinating more for 5 days, now very confused, can't wake her. Blood sugar 1,240 in ER. No ketones on urine dipstick. Osmolality 380.
column6: Type 2 diabetes on metformin only (A1C 12% at last visit), hypertension, obesity BMI 42, missed last 4 endocrinology appointments, runs out of medications frequently
column7: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
column8: No ketones (differentiates from DKA). Fluid deficit estimated 8-10L. Extremely careful IV rehydration (normal saline initially). Insulin infusion at low rate to allow osmolality correction at controlled rate. Electrolyte replacement (especially potassium). Continuous neurological monitoring (rapid osmolality correction risk). Deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis (HHS high DVT risk).
column9: Blood sugar normalized over 36 hours (too-rapid correction avoided). Osmolality normalized. Mental status fully recovered. Insulin drip transitioned to subcutaneous regimen. Comprehensive diabetes education. Social work: medication assistance program enrolled. Endocrinology follow-up weekly initially. A1C at 3 months: 8.2%. Adherence significantly improved. No recurrence.

column4: Painful genital lesion
column5: Multiple painful genital ulcers appeared 5 days ago, preceded by tingling/burning, lymph nodes in groin swollen and tender, low-grade fever, flu-like illness last week, sexually active with male partner
column6: HIV negative, MSM, on PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine), tested 3 months ago, recently received mpox vaccine (1 of 2 doses)
column7: Herpes Simplex Virus Type 2 - Primary Genital Herpes
column8: Clinical appearance consistent with primary HSV. HSV PCR swab from ulcer: HSV-2 positive. HIV test: negative. RPR: negative. Other STI panel: GC/chlamydia negative. Initiated acyclovir 400mg TID for 7-10 days (primary episode). Counseled extensively on HSV transmission, asymptomatic shedding, disclosure, condom use.
column9: Lesions crusted and healing by day 10. Lymphadenopathy resolved. Counseled on recurrence risk, prodromal symptoms, and suppressive therapy option. Started on valacyclovir 500mg daily (suppressive therapy given frequent recurrence risk in MSM with HIV exposure risk). PrEP continued. Annual STI testing recommended. Second mpox vaccine dose completed.

column4: New onset abnormal movements
column5: Wife reports husband has had increasing jerky involuntary movements of his arms and face for 3 months, balance problems, difficulty swallowing, rapidly progressive dementia (MMSE 18 one month ago, now 8)
column6: Hypertension, well-controlled. No family history of neurological disease, no recent travel, no new medications
column7: Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (Sporadic CJD)
column8: MRI brain: DWI hyperintensities in cortical ribboning pattern and basal ganglia - highly specific for CJD. EEG: periodic sharp wave complexes. CSF 14-3-3 protein positive. RT-QuIC: positive (new highly sensitive/specific test). Neurology. No effective treatment. Palliative care consultation immediately. Family meeting: devastating diagnosis shared with compassion. Goals of care discussed.
column9: Rapidly progressive. Goals of care: comfort-focused from diagnosis. No hospitalization unless for comfort symptom management. Hospice enrolled. Patient died 6 weeks after initial presentation. Family supported throughout with palliative care team. Autopsy offered for disease surveillance and family closure - family accepted. Prion disease registry reported.

column4: Severe hip pain and inability to walk after steroid treatment
column5: Hip pain worsening for 8 months, started during long course of prednisone for autoimmune hepatitis, now unable to bear weight on right hip, MRI shows characteristic changes
column6: Autoimmune hepatitis on prednisone 30mg (on high dose for 2 years), no prior hip problems, prior Cushingoid features from steroids
column7: Avascular Necrosis (AVN) of Right Femoral Head - Steroid-Induced
column8: MRI: right femoral head AVN with subchondral collapse. ARCO Stage III. Left hip also showing early Stage I changes on MRI. Orthopedic surgery consultation. Right hip: total hip arthroplasty recommended given collapse. Left hip: bisphosphonate and core decompression considered for Stage I. Prednisone taper maximized. Transition to azathioprine as steroid-sparing for autoimmune hepatitis.
column9: Right total hip arthroplasty performed - excellent outcome. Immediate pain relief. Physical therapy and weight bearing day 1. Left hip: core decompression + bisphosphonate. Prednisone tapered to 5mg with azathioprine maintaining hepatitis control. At 6-month follow-up, walking without assistance. Left hip stable on MRI. Autoimmune hepatitis in remission.

column4: Sudden severe dizziness and vomiting
column5: Severe vertigo onset while at rest, room spinning, unable to walk, severe nausea and vomiting, right-sided hearing loss and tinnitus, fullness in right ear. HINTS exam reassuring for peripheral etiology.
column6: No prior vertigo or hearing problems, no cardiovascular risk factors, healthy and active, works as accountant
column7: Acute Labyrinthitis (Viral) with Sudden Sensorineural Hearing Loss
column8: HINTS exam: normal head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, no skew deviation (peripheral lesion). MRI brain and MRI IAC (internal auditory canals): normal. Audiogram: right SSNHL (40dB across all frequencies). Emergency prednisone 60mg daily for SSNHL (time-sensitive - best within 2-4 weeks of onset). Meclizine for vertigo.
column9: Vertigo improved significantly over 5 days. Walking independently by day 7. Hearing: 30% recovery at 2 weeks on prednisone. Intratympanic dexamethasone injections x3 for additional hearing recovery. At 3 months, hearing improved to 25dB (significant recovery). Tinnitus persisting but less bothersome. Vestibular rehabilitation for residual imbalance. No recurrence.

column4: Recurrent pancreatitis and abdominal pain
column5: Fourth hospitalization for pancreatitis in 2 years. Severe upper abdominal pain radiating to back, nausea, vomiting, lipase 2,400. Drinks 6-8 beers daily. CT: pseudocyst 6cm.
column6: Alcohol use disorder (20-year history), hypertension, type 2 diabetes (likely pancreatogenic), prior mild pancreatitis x3, depression on sertraline
column7: Acute on Chronic Pancreatitis with Pseudocyst - Alcohol-Induced
column8: IV fluid resuscitation. Nil per os. IV morphine and ketorolac for pain. Pseudocyst: asymptomatic/no infected features - observe. Lipase trending down. Nutrition: oral soft diet introduced day 4. Gastroenterology and addiction medicine. MRCP: chronic pancreatic ductal changes. Social work for alcohol cessation resources. Motivational interviewing.
column9: Discharged day 7 after clinical improvement. Pseudocyst resolved on 6-week US. Referred to gastroenterologist for ERCP evaluation of duct anatomy. Addiction medicine: enrolled in intensive outpatient alcohol treatment. At 3-month follow-up, 60 days sober (verified). No further pancreatitis episodes at 6 months while sober. A1C improving.

column4: Seizure at school
column5: Student had witnessed tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds during lunch, recovered over 10 minutes. Denies prior seizures. Parents report she's had episodes of 'spacing out' during class for years (attributed to daydreaming). No headache, no fever.
column6: No known medical conditions, no medications, no family history of epilepsy, normal development, excellent student
column7: Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy (JME) with Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizure
column8: EEG: 4-6 Hz generalized spike-wave and polyspike-wave discharges, photoparoxysmal response. MRI brain: normal. Detailed history revealed: morning myoclonic jerks (dropping things, bumping into furniture) since age 13 - thought normal. Typical absence-like episodes. Classic JME. Started on levetiracetam 500mg BID (valproate discussed but deferred given reproductive age).
column9: No further GTC seizures on levetiracetam. Morning myoclonus eliminated. Driving restricted (6-month seizure-free requirement per state). Education on seizure triggers: sleep deprivation, alcohol, flickering lights. School nurse and teachers notified per parental consent. At 6-month follow-up, seizure-free, driving privileges restored. Lifetime AED therapy likely required.

column4: Progressive weakness and sensory loss in legs
column5: Progressive leg weakness over 3 weeks, started with tingling in toes, now ascending to knees, difficulty walking, areflexia on exam, respiratory muscle involvement feared - MRC sum score 48. Diarrheal illness 4 weeks prior.
column6: Healthy, active cyclist, Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis treated 4 weeks ago, no chronic conditions
column7: Guillain-Barre Syndrome (AIDP) - Post-Campylobacter
column8: CSF: albuminocytologic dissociation (protein 120, cell count 2). NCS/EMG: severe demyelinating polyneuropathy. FVC 68% - monitoring. Admitted for respiratory monitoring. IVIG 0.4g/kg x5 days (equivalent to plasmapheresis). Physical and occupational therapy. Pain management: gabapentin for neuropathic pain. ICU-level monitoring for FVC decline.
column9: FVC stabilized then improved. Did not require intubation. Weakness nadir at day 14 then improving. Transferred to inpatient rehab on day 18. Intensive rehabilitation over 6 weeks. At 3 months: walking with minimal assistance. At 6 months: near-complete recovery, cycling again. Residual mild foot numbness. Excellent prognosis for AIDP variant.

column4: Painless jaundice and weight loss
column5: Progressively deepening jaundice for 6 weeks, pale stools, dark urine, 18-pound weight loss, no abdominal pain (painless jaundice), new-onset diabetes recently. CT: mass in head of pancreas with dilated bile and pancreatic ducts.
column6: New-onset diabetes (diagnosed 2 months ago - could be related), no alcohol use, non-smoker, no family history of pancreatic cancer
column7: Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma (Potentially Resectable, T2N0M0)
column8: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: 2.5cm pancreatic head mass, no vascular involvement, no metastases. CA 19-9 markedly elevated. ERCP: biliary stent placed for decompression. EUS-guided biopsy: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. PET/CT: no distant disease. MDT tumor board: borderline resectable. Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX planned followed by Whipple procedure.
column9: Completed 4 months neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX. Restaging CT: stable/slight reduction. CA 19-9 decreased 60%. Underwent pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple). Pathology: R0 resection, ypT2N0M0 (2 lymph nodes examined, negative). Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. At 18-month follow-up: no evidence of recurrence - favorable outcome. Ongoing surveillance.

column4: Severe morning stiffness and hand swelling
column5: Progressive bilateral hand and wrist stiffness every morning lasting 2-3 hours, symmetric swelling in MCPs and PIPs, fatigue, low-grade fever. Symptoms gradually worsening over 6 months. Very functional impairment affecting her work as a pianist.
column6: No prior inflammatory conditions, healthy, no family history of autoimmune disease, non-smoker
column7: Rheumatoid Arthritis (Seropositive, Early)
column8: RF 180, anti-CCP antibody > 340 (strongly positive). CRP 42, ESR 68. Joint X-rays: periarticular osteopenia, early erosion left 4th MCP. CDAI 28 (high disease activity). Rheumatology. Methotrexate 15mg weekly + folic acid. Hydroxychloroquine 400mg daily. Prednisone bridge 10mg x6 weeks. Occupational therapy. Joint protection education.
column9: DAS28 improved from 6.2 to 3.1 at 12 weeks (moderate disease activity). Methotrexate uptitrated to 20mg. Morning stiffness decreased to 20-30 minutes. Hand function improved - resumed playing piano. Etanercept added at 6 months for insufficient response. At 12 months: DAS28 1.8 (low disease activity approaching remission). Continuing to optimize treatment.

column4: Difficulty initiating urination and weak stream
column5: Progressive urinary hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, post-void dribbling, nocturia 4-5 times, symptoms worsening over 2 years. AUA symptom score 22 (severe). Post-void residual 280mL.
column6: BPH, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, erectile dysfunction, no prior prostate cancer workup (declined PSA testing)
column7: Bladder Outlet Obstruction from BPH - Failed Medical Management
column8: PSA: 3.8 (age-appropriate, prostate 55gm). MRI prostate: enlarged prostate, no suspicious lesions. Urodynamics: obstructed pattern. Failed alpha-blocker + 5-ARI combination. Urology: surgical options discussed. Patient chose Greenlight laser PVP (photoselective vaporization of prostate) over TURP for lower bleeding risk given anticoagulation needs.
column9: PVP performed - 45g tissue vaporized. Foley removed day 1. Excellent flow rate improvement post-procedure. Post-void residual 30mL. AUA score 6 (mild) at 6 weeks. No incontinence. Minimal retrograde ejaculation. PSA decreased to 1.2 at 3 months. Sexual function maintained (no TURP retrograde ejaculation). Urology follow-up annually.

column4: Fever and sore throat with enlarged lymph nodes
column5: High fever for 5 days, severe sore throat, significant posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly on exam, extreme fatigue (can't get out of bed), white exudate on tonsils, petechiae on palate, heterophile antibody test positive
column6: Generally healthy, college student, returned from studying abroad in Europe 3 weeks ago, no prior mononucleosis
column7: Infectious Mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr Virus)
column8: Monospot positive. EBV IgM VCA positive, EA positive. CBC: lymphocytosis with 25% atypical lymphocytes. LFTs mildly elevated (hepatitis). Spleen palpable 3cm below costal margin. Supportive care: acetaminophen for fever and pain, hydration, rest. Steroids considered for airway compromise risk. Contact sports prohibited (splenic rupture risk 4-6 weeks). Amoxicillin avoided.
column9: Fever resolved by day 8. Sore throat improving. Returned to college at 3 weeks with activity restrictions. LFTs normalized at 6 weeks. Repeat ultrasound: splenomegaly resolved at 6 weeks. Cleared for contact sports at 8 weeks. Fatigue persisting until 3 months - warned about post-infectious fatigue. Full recovery at 4 months.

column4: Severe facial pain and swollen face
column5: Right-sided facial swelling rapidly worsening over 24 hours, severe throbbing right tooth pain for 3 days (upper molar), fever 103°F, difficulty opening mouth (trismus), right eye partially swollen closed
column6: Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (A1C 11%), dental phobia (no dental care for 8 years), hypertension, obesity
column7: Dentoalveolar Abscess with Ludwig's Angina Threat - Rapidly Spreading Facial Cellulitis
column8: CT face/neck: extensive right masticator space and buccal space cellulitis extending to infratemporal fossa, no airway compromise yet. WBC 24,000. Blood cultures sent. IV piperacillin-tazobactam + metronidazole. Oral and maxillofacial surgery: extraction of right upper molar, I&D of abscess. Airway monitoring continuous.
column9: Infection controlled with I&D and IV antibiotics. Airway remained stable. Trismus resolved over 5 days. Transitioned to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. Blood cultures negative. Diabetes management optimized urgently (glucose running 350-400 during admission). HbA1C 11% - referred to endocrinology urgently. Dental care plan established with supportive dentistry. Discharged day 7.

column4: Bloody urine and foamy urine since starting new medication
column5: Gross hematuria for 3 days, frothy urine, bilateral ankle edema, blood pressure significantly elevated (BP 165/100 from 120/70 baseline). Started ibuprofen 800mg TID 4 weeks ago for sports injury without PCP supervision.
column6: Generally healthy, competitive cyclist, no prior renal disease, occasional NSAID use in the past, father has hypertension
column7: NSAID-Induced Acute Interstitial Nephritis with Nephrotic Syndrome Features
column8: Creatinine 3.4 (baseline unknown, est. 1.0). Urinalysis: 3+ protein, 3+ blood, no casts initially then eosinophiluria found. 24-hour urine protein: 4.2g. Renal biopsy: acute interstitial nephritis with focal glomerulonephritis (NSAID-induced). Ibuprofen stopped immediately. Prednisone 60mg daily. Nephrology consultation.
column9: Creatinine improved to 1.6 at 4 weeks on prednisone. Proteinuria decreased to 0.5g/24h. Blood pressure normalized off NSAIDs. Prednisone tapered over 8 weeks. Creatinine near baseline 1.1 at 3 months. Counseled: never use NSAIDs again. COX-2 inhibitor with nephrology approval considered for future pain. Full renal recovery achieved.

column4: Collapse and bradycardia in drug store
column5: Patient collapsed in pharmacy while picking up medications, brief loss of consciousness, bystanders helped, found bradycardic at 32 bpm, BP 78/50, diaphoretic. Had just taken her new medication.
column6: Complete heart block from drug interaction - had recently been started on diltiazem (cardiologist) and a beta-blocker (PCP) for separate indications without cross-communication. Hypertension, heart failure (EF 40%), COPD.
column7: Symptomatic Complete Heart Block - Iatrogenic Bradycardia
column8: Atropine 1mg IV x2 with partial improvement. Temporary transvenous pacemaker placed emergently. Heart rate stabilized. Diltiazem and metoprolol both held. AV nodal blocking agents causing complete heart block when combined. Electrophysiology consultation. Permanent pacemaker implanted. Medication reconciliation performed across all providers.
column9: Permanent pacemaker placed without complications. Heart rate maintained. Both diltiazem and beta-blocker reconsidered: beta-blocker continued (needed for CHF) at lower dose after pacing ensures heart rate support. Diltiazem discontinued. Communication sent to all prescribing providers. At 3 months: no further syncope, pacemaker functioning appropriately, medications rationalized.

column4: Rapidly progressive rash and high fever
column5: High fever 105°F for 2 days, petechial rash appearing on extremities now spreading to trunk, severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia. School-aged child recently had similar illness in same household 5 days ago (hospitalized elsewhere for meningitis).
column6: Healthy, no chronic conditions, works as elementary school teacher, not up to date on meningococcal vaccine
column7: Meningococcal Meningitis with Meningococcemia (Neisseria meningitidis serogroup C)
column8: IMMEDIATE: blood cultures drawn, then empiric IV ceftriaxone 2g IV (do not delay for LP if herniation risk). LP: bacterial meningitis CSF pattern. Dexamethasone before/with first antibiotics. Non-blanching petechial rash - meningococcemia. ICU. Cultures: N. meningitidis serogroup C. Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts: rifampin or ciprofloxacin. Public health notification.
column9: Survived with aggressive treatment. Fever resolved by day 4. No hearing loss. Skin necrosis at several petechiae sites - minor scarring. Completed 7-day ceftriaxone course. Discharged day 10. Meningococcal vaccination given at discharge (and for all close contacts). Full recovery with no neurological sequelae. Public health investigation completed.

column4: Abdominal swelling and confusion
column5: Progressive abdominal swelling for 3 months, now confusion for 2 days, asterixis, jaundice, bilateral leg edema. Known cirrhosis but patient admits continued daily alcohol use.
column6: Alcoholic cirrhosis (MELD 24 when last checked 4 months ago), portal hypertension with known varices, ascites previously managed with diuretics, hepatitis C (untreated), type 2 diabetes
column7: Hepatic Encephalopathy with Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
column8: Diagnostic paracentesis: PMN > 250/mm3 (SBP). IV cefotaxime 2g q8h for 5 days. Albumin 1.5g/kg day 1 and 1g/kg day 3 (reduces renal failure risk in SBP). Lactulose 30mL q1h until 2-3 bowel movements. Rifaximin added. TIPS evaluation for refractory ascites. Hepatology: Hepatitis C treatment initiated (ledipasvir/sofosbuvir - cirrhosis-appropriate regimen).
column9: SBP treated. Mental status improved to grade 1 encephalopathy. Ascites managed with therapeutic paracentesis and secondary prophylaxis (norfloxacin). HCV SVR achieved at 12 weeks (cured). Alcohol cessation: agreed to counseling. MELD improved to 18 with HCV cure and alcohol cessation. Removed from transplant waitlist - potential delisting standard met. Continued close follow-up.

column4: Swollen painful fingers and tightening skin
column5: Fingers have been swollen and very stiff for 8 months, skin on fingers and face feels tight, painful fingertip ulcers on right index and middle fingers, Raynaud's phenomenon turning fingers blue/white/red with cold, difficulty swallowing and acid reflux worsening
column6: No prior autoimmune diagnosis, Raynaud's noted since age 25 attributed to stress, works in cold environment, non-smoker
column7: Systemic Sclerosis (Limited Cutaneous Scleroderma - CREST Syndrome)
column8: ANA positive 1:640 nucleolar. Anti-centromere antibody positive. Echo: no pulmonary hypertension yet (high risk with limited SSc). Pulmonary function tests: mild restrictive pattern. High-resolution CT chest: bibasilar interstitial fibrosis early. Skin score: modified Rodnan = 12. Rheumatology. CCB (nifedipine) for Raynaud's. Sildenafil added for digital ulcers. Omeprazole for GERD. Mycophenolate for ILD.
column9: Digital ulcers healed. Raynaud's improved with nifedipine. Pulmonary function stable at 6 months on mycophenolate. Annual echo for PH screening. HRCT annually for ILD monitoring. At 12 months, stable with multidisciplinary management. Enrolled in SSc registry. Patient support group connected. Long-term prognosis discussed honestly.

column4: Chest pain after heavy lifting at gym
column5: Sharp right-sided chest pain immediately after maximal bench press at gym, dyspnea worsening with movement, diminished breath sounds right side, tracheal deviation to left, oxygen saturation dropping to 88%
column6: Marfan syndrome (known, on losartan, history of pneumothorax 3 years ago), tall thin body habitus, annual cardiac monitoring
column7: Spontaneous Pneumothorax - Large Right (in Marfan Syndrome)
column8: Immediate: needle decompression right 2nd intercostal space (tension PTX concern). Rush of air - decompressed. Oxygen 100%. Chest tube placed. 1.5L of air evacuated. Lung re-expanded on CXR. Thoracic surgery consultation. Discussion of pleurodesis given recurrent pneumothorax in Marfan's. Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) with pleurodesis planned.
column9: Lung fully re-expanded. Chest tube on water seal then clamped - no air leak. Removed day 3. Discharged day 5. VATS pleurodesis planned electively in 6 weeks. VATS performed - right mechanical pleurodesis. No further pneumothorax at 18-month follow-up. Aortic root monitoring continued (4.2cm stable). Activity restrictions counseled.

column4: Sudden onset left arm and jaw pain during yardwork
column5: Crushing left arm and jaw pain during yardwork in summer heat, profuse sweating, extreme fatigue, nausea. Called husband. Initially refused to go to ER (thought it was muscle strain). Husband brought her in 3 hours after onset.
column6: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes (poor control, A1C 10%), BMI 38, family history negative, very sedentary, never smoked
column7: STEMI (Left Circumflex) - Delayed Presentation
column8: EKG: lateral ST elevations. Door to balloon time 74 minutes (delayed due to 3-hour onset-to-door time). Cath: complete LCx occlusion. PCI with stent. TIMI 3 flow restored. Echo: lateral wall akinesis, EF 40%. Enrolled in STEMI protocol. Anti-platelet, statin, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor initiated.
column9: Recovery complicated by mild heart failure requiring diuresis (EF 40%). Discharged day 5. Cardiac rehab enrolled. Diabetes management intensified (A1C goal <8%). At 6-month echo: EF improved to 48% with remodeling prevention therapy. No angina. Returned to walking program. Education on delay in seeking care addressed and resolved with future action plan.

column4: Recurrent oral thrush and vaginal infections
column5: Oral thrush (white patches confirmed) recurring monthly for 6 months, recurrent vaginal candidiasis (4 episodes this year), fatigue, 15-pound weight loss, night sweats. Has not had an HIV test in 10 years.
column6: No chronic conditions (believed), last medical care 10 years ago, divorced 5 years ago, no recent sexual activity per patient, works as social worker
column7: HIV Infection with AIDS-Defining Illness (CD4 42)
column8: HIV antibody/p24 antigen test: REACTIVE. Confirmatory Western blot: positive. CD4 count 42, viral load 450,000. Cryptococcal antigen negative. CMV PCR negative. MAC blood culture negative. Baseline resistance testing. ART initiated (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir AF) after resistance testing. PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX). MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin weekly). Fluconazole for candidiasis.
column9: ART tolerated well. Viral load undetectable at 3 months. CD4 improved to 185. PCP prophylaxis discontinued when CD4 >200. Candidiasis resolved. No AIDS-defining illnesses in follow-up. Partner notification counseled - patient disclosed to partners. Ryan White program enrollment. Infectious disease follow-up every 3 months. Psychosocial support for HIV diagnosis adjustment.

column4: Sudden onset low back pain with severe radiation to groin
column5: Previously healthy man with sudden severe low back pain radiating to groin, pain pulsating with heartbeat, feels like heart beating in abdomen, blood pressure difference between arms (left 140, right 90), diaphoretic, BP falling
column6: Hypertension (poorly controlled), BMI 36, 30-pack-year history (current smoker), family history of aortic aneurysm (father operated at age 62)
column7: Type B Aortic Dissection (Stanford) Extending to Iliac Arteries
column8: CTA: type B aortic dissection from left subclavian to iliac bifurcation. Right iliac compromised (BP difference). Pain control with IV morphine. IV labetalol (heart rate and BP control - target HR <60, SBP <120). ICU monitoring. Vascular surgery: initially medical management. Right iliac ischemia worsened - hybrid TEVAR with right iliac stenting on day 3.
column9: Survived with medical management plus hybrid intervention for malperfusion. 14-day ICU stay. Strict BP control maintained. Discharged on labetalol, amlodipine, chlorthalidone triple therapy. Smoking cessation: varenicline started. CT surveillance at 1, 3, 6 months then annually. At 6-month CT: dissection stable. Aortic diameter stable. Rigorous hypertension management emphasized.

column4: Redness and discharge from both eyes
column5: Bilateral eye redness for 3 days, profuse purulent discharge (pillow stained every morning), significant eyelid swelling, burning and pain in both eyes, sexually active male with urethral discharge noted on exam that patient had dismissed
column6: No chronic conditions, MSM, on PrEP, last STI testing 6 months ago, recent unprotected receptive anal intercourse
column7: Gonococcal Conjunctivitis (Bilateral - Hyperacute)
column8: Corneal threat: ulceration risk within hours if untreated. Conjunctival swabs sent. IMMEDIATE ceftriaxone 1g IM (single dose). Hourly eye irrigation. Ophthalmology emergency consultation: no corneal involvement yet. GC NAAT urethral swab positive. Pharyngeal and rectal swabs positive. IV ceftriaxone for disseminated concern. Azithromycin for chlamydia co-treatment.
column9: Rapid improvement within 24-48 hours of ceftriaxone. Discharge resolved. No corneal damage. Completed full STI treatment. HIV test negative. PrEP maintained. Partner notification for all recent contacts. Follow-up STI testing at 3 months. Ophthalmology follow-up: corneas clear. Educated on all routes of gonococcal transmission and prevention.

column4: Chronic rectal pain and pressure
column5: Rectal pressure and pain for 3 months, now noticing a mass at his anal opening, bleeding with bowel movements, altered bowel habits. Presented due to partner's insistence.
column6: HIV positive (well-controlled on ART, CD4 580, undetectable), MSM, HPV positive (high-risk types 16/18 found on anal Pap 2 years ago - lost to follow-up for HRA), no prior anal cancer screening
column7: Anal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (T2N0M0, Stage II) - HPV-Related
column8: High-resolution anoscopy: 2.5cm squamous cell carcinoma of anal canal. Biopsy confirmed. PET/CT: no regional nodes, no distant disease. HIV-related immunosuppression: not a contraindication to treatment. Multidisciplinary team: concurrent chemoradiation (mitomycin C + 5-FU + 54Gy) - definitive treatment. Colostomy avoided.
column9: Completed concurrent chemoradiation. Significant acute proctitis managed with supportive care. At 3-month assessment: complete clinical response. Biopsy negative. At 12-month HRA: no residual/recurrent carcinoma. ART continued throughout. CD4 maintained above 400 during treatment. Annual anal surveillance with HRA. HPV vaccination completed (despite prior infection - vaccine for other types).

column4: Hoarse voice and trouble swallowing for months
column5: Progressive hoarseness for 5 months, now difficulty swallowing solid foods, 12-pound weight loss, heavy alcohol use and tobacco. Examined in office: left vocal cord fixed on laryngoscopy.
column6: 50-pack-year smoking history (current), heavy alcohol use (>10 drinks/day for 30 years), GERD, hypertension, no prior head and neck issues
column7: Supraglottic Laryngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (T3N2cM0, Stage IVA)
column8: Biopsy from office laryngoscopy: squamous cell carcinoma. PET/CT: primary laryngeal mass, bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, no distant metastases. MDT: organ preservation approach - concurrent cisplatin/radiation vs upfront surgery. Patient elected chemoradiation (larynx preservation). Cisplatin 100mg/m2 q3 weeks + 70Gy over 7 weeks. PEG tube placed pre-treatment.
column9: Completed concurrent chemoradiation with significant but manageable mucositis and dysphagia. PEG tube used throughout treatment. At 3-month laryngoscopy and PET/CT: complete response. Larynx preserved, vocal cord mobile. Swallowing rehabilitation with SLP. PEG removed at 4 months. Hoarseness persisting but communicating. At 12 months, disease-free.

column4: Severe abdominal cramping and bloody diarrhea after camping
column5: Returned from camping trip 5 days ago, now with bloody diarrhea 8-12 times daily, severe cramping, fever 101°F. Drank stream water during trip despite filter. Ate at communal cookout with possible undercooked hamburgers.
column6: Generally healthy, no GI conditions, no medications, no prior GI infections
column7: Hemorrhagic E. coli O157:H7 Infection with Early HUS Signs
column8: Bloody diarrhea + fever: E. coli O157:H7 suspected. Stool culture sent (including Shiga-toxin producing E. coli). AVOID antibiotics (worsen HUS risk). Supportive care: careful IV hydration (avoid nephrotoxic agents). CBC: platelets 110,000 and falling. Creatinine 1.8 (baseline est. 0.8). Hematology: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia on peripheral smear. HUS developing.
column9: Hemolytic uremic syndrome confirmed: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI. Nephrology: renal replacement therapy avoided - creatinine peaked at 2.4 then improved. No dialysis required. Platelet count recovered by day 7. Stool culture confirmed STEC O157:H7. Health department notified. Full renal recovery at 6 weeks.

column4: Persistent genital warts and abnormal Pap smear
column5: Recurrent vulvar condyloma despite multiple treatments. Pap smear: high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL). Colposcopy and cervical biopsy recommended urgently by gynecologist.
column6: HPV positive (types 16 and 18), immunosuppressed on mycophenolate for lupus nephritis, prednisone 10mg daily, no prior cervical dysplasia on previous Paps, all previous Paps normal until this year
column7: Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia Grade 3 (CIN 3) with VIN 3
column8: Colposcopy: acetowhite lesion entire TZ, CIN 3 biopsy. Loop electrosurgical excision procedure (LEEP) performed. Vulvar biopsy: VIN 3. Imiquimod for VIN. Immunosuppression minimized as much as possible while controlling lupus. HPV vaccination completed (prevent other types). 6-month Pap/HPV co-testing post-LEEP.
column9: LEEP margins clear - CIN 3 excised. VIN 3 partially responding to imiquimod - laser ablation performed for residual lesion. Lupus stable on reduced immunosuppression. At 6-month co-test: HPV positive but cytology normal (expected given immunosuppression). Annual surveillance with co-testing. Gynecologic oncology involvement. Patient compliant with intensive follow-up.

column4: Painful red eye with vision change
column5: Severe right eye pain, redness, photophobia, tearing, decreased vision in right eye. Vesicular rash around right eye and forehead extending to the tip of nose (Hutchinson's sign). Fever 100.2°F.
column6: Hypertension, diabetes, hypothyroidism, had Shingrix vaccine first dose only (never completed series), recent chemotherapy for breast cancer completed 3 months ago
column7: Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus (HZO) with Acute Retinal Necrosis Threat
column8: Hutchinson's sign: high risk for ocular involvement. Ophthalmology emergency consultation: corneal involvement (dendrites), uveitis, elevated IOP. IV acyclovir initiated immediately (severe HZO). Topical steroid with antiviral coverage for uveitis. IOP lowering drops. Pain management. Neurology evaluation for CNS involvement.
column9: Corneal involvement treated successfully. No ARN developed. Uveitis resolved with topical steroids. IOP normalized. Oral valacyclovir after IV course for total 14 days treatment. Vision returned to baseline. Gabapentin for postherpetic neuralgia prevention. Completed Shingrix series 6 months after recovery. Ophthalmology follow-up for potential late complications.

column4: Fever and muscle pain after river rafting
column5: 10 days after white-water river rafting in Hawaii, developed high fever (104°F), severe muscle aches (especially calves), headache, red eyes (conjunctival injection), nausea. Labs: elevated creatinine, elevated bilirubin.
column6: Healthy outdoor enthusiast, no chronic conditions, no vaccinations for travel (emergency trip), had abrasion on shin from previous rock climbing
column7: Leptospirosis (Weil's Disease)
column8: Classic Weil's disease presentation: fever + conjunctival injection + myalgia + AKI + jaundice. Blood and urine leptospiral cultures sent. Serology (MAT) sent. IV penicillin G started immediately. Supportive care. Creatinine 3.2, bilirubin 6.8. Cardiac monitoring (myocarditis risk). Platelet count 65,000 (thrombocytopenia). ICU-level monitoring.
column9: Responded to IV penicillin over 5 days. Fever resolved day 4. Creatinine improving to 1.8 by day 5. Bilirubin normalizing. Platelet count recovering. No pulmonary hemorrhage (ARDS risk). Completed 7 days IV penicillin then oral doxycycline to complete 14 days. Discharged day 8. Full renal recovery at 6 weeks. Hawaii DOH notified. Vaccination counseled.

column4: Crushing chest pain and elevated troponin - no obstructive CAD
column5: Classic STEMI presentation - crushing chest pain, diaphoresis, EKG changes. Emergency PCI performed: no obstructive coronary artery disease found. Troponin markedly elevated. Echo: apical ballooning pattern on echo - classic.
column6: Recent severe emotional stressor (wife died 3 weeks ago), hypertension, no prior cardiac history, no other cardiovascular risk factors, denies chest pain prior
column7: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy (Stress Cardiomyopathy/Broken Heart Syndrome)
column8: Diagnosis confirmed: apical ballooning, absence of obstructive CAD, preceding emotional stressor (Bereaved). EF acutely 25%. No intraventricular thrombus on echo. Supportive care. ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker (with caution). Anticoagulation if LV thrombus develops. No catecholamines. Supportive monitoring. Grief counseling referral.
column9: EF improved to 45% at 2 weeks and 58% at 6 weeks (near-complete recovery - typical for Takotsubo). No complications. Discharged day 5. ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker for 3 months during recovery. Grief counseling engaged. Bereavement support group. At 6-month follow-up, EF normal, no angina, adjusting to life after loss. Very low recurrence rate counseled.

column4: Worsening shortness of breath - cancer treatment complication
column5: Stage IV non-small cell lung cancer on nivolumab immunotherapy, presenting with progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fever 101°F, O2 sat 87% on room air. 8 weeks into immunotherapy cycle 4.
column6: NSCLC stage IV EGFR-wildtype on nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor), hypertension, hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, prior mild COPD (FEV1 72%)
column7: Immune-Related Adverse Event: Severe Pneumonitis (Grade 3) from Checkpoint Inhibitor
column8: CT chest: new bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidation not present on prior scan 6 weeks ago. Bronchoscopy with BAL: no infection. Nivolumab HELD permanently (Grade 3 = permanent discontinuation). Prednisone 2mg/kg/day. ICU monitoring. O2 supplementation. Infectious workup negative. Pulmonology consultation.
column9: Dramatic improvement at 10 days of prednisone. Repeat CT at 3 weeks: 75% resolution. O2 requirements decreased. Prednisone gradually tapered over 8 weeks. Nivolumab not rechallengeable given Grade 3 severity. Oncology: pivoting to new treatment (docetaxel). At 3-month restaging: partial response to new therapy. Pulmonology follow-up.

column4: Rash on palms and soles plus painless oral ulcers
column5: Painless oral ulcers appeared 2 weeks ago, now non-itchy rash appeared on palms and soles (unusual distribution), lymphadenopathy in neck and groin, low-grade fever, fatigue, flu-like illness 6 weeks ago that resolved. Sexually active.
column6: Generally healthy, no chronic conditions, had unprotected sex with a new male partner 8 weeks ago
column7: Secondary Syphilis (Same Presentation as Case 102 but Different Patient)
column8: RPR reactive 1:256. TPHA confirmatory positive. HIV: negative. Other STI panel complete. Condyloma lata in groin area. Classic secondary syphilis. Benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM x1. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction counseled. Partner notified - tested and treated (primary syphilis). STI counseling. PrEP discussion given HIV risk behavior.
column9: Rash resolved at 4 weeks. RPR at 6 months: 1:16 (appropriate response). STI testing quarterly given ongoing HIV risk. PrEP started. Engaged in sexual health clinic follow-up. Partners treated. No late syphilis complications. Education on syphilis stages and reinfection risk - one prior infection does not provide immunity.

column4: Leg pain and ulcers that won't heal
column5: Bilateral leg ulcers for 4 months (not healing despite wound care from PCP), severe leg pain at rest, toes dusky and cold. ABI 0.35 bilaterally.
column6: Diabetes (A1C 11%), peripheral arterial disease (known but not treated aggressively), hypertension, 50-pack-year history (quit 2 years ago), CKD Stage 3
column7: Critical Limb Ischemia with Diabetic Foot Ulcers - Bilateral
column8: Vascular surgery emergency consultation. CT angiography: bilateral severe multi-level arterial disease (iliac, SFA, tibial). Bilateral limb salvage attempted: right leg angioplasty + bypass. Left leg: poor targets for revascularization. Wound care center involvement. Aggressive diabetes management (insulin regimen). Right leg: improved perfusion. Left: ischemia persisting.
column9: Right leg: wound healing initiated post-revascularization. ABI improved to 0.62 right. Left leg: insufficient revascularization options - below-knee amputation recommended and performed. Prosthetic limb fitting. Vascular rehab. A1C improved to 8.2%. Continued bilateral wound care for right foot ulcer (healing). Smoking cessation maintenance. At 6 months, ambulating with prosthetic and cane.

column4: Rash and joint pain weeks after hiking
column5: Expanding circular red rash on right thigh (now 12cm, started as small spot after hike 3 weeks ago), joint pain in knees, mild facial drooping noticed by friend, fatigue, headache. Lives in Connecticut.
column6: No chronic conditions, outdoor enthusiast, works as trail guide, no prior tick illnesses
column7: Disseminated Lyme Disease (Stage 2) with Facial Palsy and Arthritis
column8: Classic erythema migrans (multiple secondary lesions now). Lyme ELISA positive, Western blot confirmatory (IgM and IgG both positive). CSF not indicated (no meningitis signs). Facial palsy (CN VII) - Lyme neuroborreliosis. Doxycycline 100mg BID for 21 days (neuroborreliosis: IV ceftriaxone for meningitis, but CN VII palsy can be treated with oral doxycycline). Eye care for incomplete closure.
column9: Erythema migrans resolved at 2 weeks. Facial palsy improved at 4 weeks, complete resolution at 8 weeks. Joint pain resolved. At 3-month follow-up: no symptoms. Lyme ELISA declining. Tick bite prevention education reinforced given occupational risk. Full recovery with no post-Lyme syndrome. Completed doxycycline course without complications.

column4: Severe low back pain and rectal bleeding in cancer patient
column5: Presenting to ED with severe low back pain radiating down both legs (bilateral sciatica), rectal bleeding, on bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) for metastatic colon cancer, HR 128, BP 88/55
column6: Metastatic colon cancer on bevacizumab + FOLFIRI chemotherapy, prior sigmoid colectomy, pelvic radiation for local recurrence, hypertension, anticoagulation for PE (rivaroxaban)
column7: Gastrointestinal Hemorrhage and Bevacizumab-Associated GI Perforation
column8: CT abdomen: free air near anastomosis consistent with perforation. Active GI bleeding. Bevacizumab immediately stopped (anti-VEGF = perforation risk). Rivaroxaban reversed (andexanet alfa). Emergency surgery: perforation identified and repaired, bleeding source cauterized. ICU post-operatively. Goals of care reviewed with oncology team - can bevacizumab restart? (typically not).
column9: Survived surgical intervention. ICU for 5 days. Nutrition via TPN then NG tube. Discharged to step-down facility after 2-week hospitalization. Oncology: FOLFIRI continued without bevacizumab. Anticoagulation restarted (rivaroxaban) at reduced dose. Disease assessment: continued response to chemotherapy without anti-VEGF. Palliative care continues to co-manage.

column4: Eye pain and decreased vision in young adult
column5: Severe left eye pain, redness, photophobia, decreased vision, whitish deposit visible in anterior chamber, started wearing contact lenses while swimming 3 weeks ago, doesn't remove lenses overnight or for swimming
column6: Contact lens wearer for 5 years, previously healthy, no prior eye issues, works as software developer, habitual overnight lens use
column7: Acanthamoeba Keratitis (Contact Lens-Associated)
column8: Slit lamp: ring-shaped corneal infiltrate, 'dirty' anterior chamber, radial keratoneuritis - classic Acanthamoeba pattern. Confocal microscopy: Acanthamoeba cysts confirmed. Corneal scraping: Acanthamoeba on culture. PHMB 0.02% + propamidine 0.1% eye drops hourly for 7 days. Contact lenses permanently discontinued. Pain management (severe). Ophthalmology infectious disease specialist.
column9: Vision recovering gradually over 3 months of intensive treatment. No corneal transplant required (in this case). Treatment for 6 months total with gradual tapering. At 6 months, vision 20/40 (from 20/200) with residual corneal scarring. Glasses fitted. Educated thoroughly on contact lens hygiene and water exposure. Permanent contact lens avoidance advised.

column4: Intractable vomiting and weight loss - no GI cause found
column5: 18 months of cyclic nausea and vomiting episodes lasting 3-5 days occurring every 3-4 weeks, accompanied by hot baths providing some relief (spends up to 4 hours in hot shower during episodes), extensive GI workup normal, 20-pound weight loss, marijuana use daily for 5 years
column6: Daily cannabis use (5 years), anxiety disorder on escitalopram, no GI diagnoses despite extensive testing, no pregnancies
column7: Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome (CHS)
column8: Classic CHS triad: cyclic emesis, hot bathing compulsion, cannabis use (>1 year). Extensive prior testing (multiple EGDs, capsule endoscopy, MRIs, CT scans - all normal). Capsaicin cream applied to abdomen during acute episode: immediate relief (diagnostic and therapeutic). Haloperidol 5mg IV: resolved acute episode. Cannabis cessation counseling initiated.
column9: Acute episode terminated with haloperidol and capsaicin cream. Discharged with smoking cessation resources. Counseled extensively on CHS: only permanent cure is cannabis cessation. At 3-month follow-up: 2 months cannabis-free - no vomiting episodes. At 6 months, resumed cannabis use - symptoms returned. Renewed cessation discussion. Currently 6-month sober from cannabis, episode-free.

column4: Sudden painless vision loss in a diabetic
column5: Complete vision loss in right eye overnight in a diabetic patient with poorly controlled disease. No pain. Dilated exam by ophthalmologist: vitreous hemorrhage obscuring view.
column6: Type 2 diabetes (A1C 12.5%), hypertension, dyslipidemia, obesity, no prior eye exams for 5 years, never had eye injections
column7: Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy with Vitreous Hemorrhage
column8: Fundus obscured by vitreous hemorrhage. B-scan ultrasound: no retinal detachment. Left eye exam: proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) - extensive new vessels, laser panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) performed immediately on left eye to prevent similar hemorrhage. Right eye: pan-retinal photocoagulation placed around vitreous hemorrhage when visible, anti-VEGF injection.
column9: Right eye vitreous hemorrhage slowly clearing over 8 weeks. Vision improving gradually. PPV (vitrectomy) performed at 10 weeks for persistent non-clearing hemorrhage. Post-vitrectomy vision: 20/60 and improving. Left eye: stable after PRP. Endocrinology urgently involved: A1C improved to 8.0 at 3 months with intensive management. Regular ophthalmology every 3 months.

column4: Seizure in pregnancy
column5: 28 weeks pregnant, witnessed tonic-clonic seizure at home by husband, no prior seizure history, blood pressure 190/115 measured at home. HR 130. No prior prenatal care.
column6: First pregnancy, no prior medical history, recently moved and hasn't established care, no family history of eclampsia, no headaches previously
column7: Eclampsia (Seizure in Pregnancy)
column8: Magnesium sulfate 4g IV loading dose, then 1-2g/hr infusion immediately. IV labetalol for BP control. Fetal monitoring: late decelerations initially stabilizing. Labs: platelets 95,000, elevated LFTs, elevated creatinine - HELLP syndrome. Decision: emergency cesarean section at 28 weeks given severe HELLP + eclampsia. NICU team present.
column9: Baby born 28 weeks, 1100g, admitted to NICU. Mother hemodynamically stabilized post-C-section. Magnesium for 24 hours post-delivery. BP gradually normalized by day 4. HELLP resolved. Discharged day 6. Baby remained in NICU for 6 weeks then discharged home. No maternal neurological sequelae. Pre-conception counseling for next pregnancy: early prenatal care critical.

column4: Progressive tremor worse with intention
column5: Bilateral arm tremor worsening over 5 years, particularly noticeable when reaching for objects or drinking from a cup (intention tremor), ataxic gait, scanning dysarthria, difficulty with fine motor tasks. Drinks alcohol daily (reported tremor improves with alcohol).
column6: Alcohol use disorder (admitted daily use to suppress tremor), hypertension, depression, no family history of tremor, retired surgeon (tremor ended career)
column7: Essential Tremor (Severe) with Alcohol Use Disorder (Self-Medicating)
column8: Detailed neurological exam: classic postural and intention tremor, no resting tremor, no Parkinsonism. Head CT/MRI: normal. DaTscan: normal (rules out Parkinson's). Propranolol 80mg daily (uptitrated to 160mg) - partial response. Primidone 250mg BID - additional response. Addiction medicine co-management for alcohol use disorder. Deep brain stimulation evaluation initiated.
column9: DBS surgery performed (bilateral VIM thalamus). Dramatic tremor reduction (80%) allowing meal independence. Alcohol use disorder treated concurrently: naltrexone + therapy. 6-month sober at follow-up. DBS programming optimized. Fine motor function improved enough to perform in low-risk volunteer medical activities. Quality of life dramatically improved.

column4: Leg weakness and breathing difficulty
column5: Symmetric ascending weakness starting feet 2 weeks ago, now unable to walk, rising to involve thighs and lower abdomen, shortness of breath on exertion, recent COVID-19 infection 4 weeks ago. FVC 55% on spirometry.
column6: COVID-19 infection 4 weeks ago (mild, not hospitalized), no prior neurological conditions, works as physical therapist
column7: Post-COVID Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS - AIDP)
column8: Post-COVID GBS well-documented. LP: classic albuminocytologic dissociation. NCS/EMG: demyelinating pattern. FVC 55% - close monitoring, ICU ready. IVIG 0.4g/kg x5 days initiated. GI/GU monitoring. DVT prophylaxis. Pain management. PT/OT consultation. Respiratory therapy standby.
column9: FVC improved to 68% with treatment. Weakness nadir at day 10. IVIG completed. FVC stabilizing. Did not require intubation. Transferred to rehab unit after ICU monitoring. At 6-week inpatient rehab, walking with assistance. At 3 months, walking independently with minimal weakness. Returned to modified physical therapy work at 6 months. Full recovery expected.

column4: Abdominal mass found incidentally on imaging
column5: CT scan performed for trauma (minor car accident) incidentally revealed 12cm right adrenal mass. Patient had no symptoms related to adrenal mass. Noted: episodic hypertensive crises with headaches and sweating for 2 years thought to be anxiety.
column6: Hypertension (controlled on 3 agents), anxiety disorder, no prior surgeries, no family history of endocrine tumors
column7: Pheochromocytoma (Right Adrenal, 12cm)
column8: 24-hour urine metanephrines: markedly elevated (normetanephrine 850 times normal). Plasma fractionated metanephrines: similarly elevated. MRI adrenal: 12cm heterogeneous mass with hemorrhage. Genetic testing: SDHB mutation identified (hereditary pheochromocytoma syndrome). Whole-body MIBG scan: no metastases. MANDATORY alpha-blockade first (phenoxybenzamine 10mg BID uptitrating) before surgery.
column9: Alpha-blocked for 2 weeks then beta-blocker added. BP normalized. Laparoscopic (hand-assisted) right adrenalectomy. Intraoperative hemodynamic stability achieved with alpha-blockade. Pathology: pheochromocytoma. Annual surveillance for recurrence (SDHB = higher malignancy risk). Genetic counseling for family. Hypertensive crises resolved completely post-op.

column4: Foot wound that won't heal
column5: Right great toe wound for 4 months, started as a blister, now a 3cm ulcer with surrounding cellulitis, probing to bone possible. Patient admits he can't feel his feet. First time seeing a doctor in 3 years.
column6: Type 2 diabetes (A1C not recently measured - likely very high), hypertension, obesity, neuropathy symptoms for years (ignored), no podiatry or ophthalmology care
column7: Diabetic Foot Osteomyelitis with Charcot Neuroarthropathy
column8: Probe-to-bone positive (high specificity for osteomyelitis). MRI: osteomyelitis right first metatarsal + surrounding soft tissue infection. A1C: 13.8%. Glucose 380. Bone biopsy: Staphylococcus aureus MSSA. 6-week course of targeted antibiotics (nafcillin IV then transition to oral). Off-loading device. Podiatric surgery: limited resection of infected metatarsal bone. Diabetes management intensive.
column9: Osteomyelitis treated successfully. Wound healed at 10 weeks. A1C improved to 10.2 at 3 months then 8.8 at 6 months with intensive management. Neuropathy evaluated: severe DSPN confirmed. Ophthalmology: proliferative diabetic retinopathy found. Laser treatment initiated. Foot care education critical. Podiatry every 6-8 weeks. Vascular surgery: ABI 0.75 - close monitoring.

column4: Eye pain, redness, and vision changes in elderly diabetic
column5: Severe right eye pain, redness, photophobia, watery discharge, vision blurred to light perception only. On exam: corneal haze, hypopyon (white blood cells layering in anterior chamber), very elevated IOP 52mmHg.
column6: Type 2 diabetes (poorly controlled), recent cataract surgery right eye 3 weeks ago, hypertension, immunocompromised (on methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis)
column7: Acute Endophthalmitis (Post-Cataract Surgery) - Staphylococcus epidermidis
column8: Post-surgical endophthalmitis - sight-threatening emergency. Immediate vitreous tap and injection of intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime). Emergency vitrectomy performed (pars plana vitrectomy) given poor initial presentation. Intravitreal and IV antibiotics. Cultures from vitreous: Staphylococcus epidermidis (most common post-cataract organism, sensitive to vancomycin).
column9: Vision improved to 20/200 after vitrectomy (some permanent damage). Further improvement with continued treatment to 20/80 at 3 months. Long-term follow-up with retina specialist. Second cataract surgery on left eye deferred with modified protocol and extended antibiotic prophylaxis. Methotrexate temporarily held during treatment.

column4: Sudden onset abdominal pain and black stools
column5: Acute epigastric pain 6/10, black tarry stools for 2 days, lightheadedness, weakness. Takes daily ibuprofen 800mg for gym-related shoulder pain without gastroprotection. No alcohol.
column6: Healthy, no prior GI history, competitive bodybuilder, high protein diet, takes NSAIDs, creatine, protein supplements, no other medications
column7: Peptic Ulcer Disease with Gastrointestinal Bleeding - NSAID-Induced
column8: Hemoglobin 9.2 (estimated baseline 15). 2 units PRBC transfused. IV pantoprazole. Emergency EGD: 2cm gastric ulcer, posterior wall, visible vessel - injected with epinephrine and clipped. Biopsy for H. pylori: negative. NSAIDs permanently discontinued. PPI 40mg BID for 8 weeks. Discussed NSAID alternatives for muscle pain.
column9: Hemoglobin stabilized at 12.1. No rebleeding post-endoscopy. Discharged day 3 on PPI. 8-week PPI course completed. Repeat EGD: ulcer healed completely. H. pylori negative confirmed. Shoulder pain: physical therapy and topical diclofenac as NSAID alternative. PPI continued as long as any NSAID use. At 6 months, fully recovered.

column4: Increasingly severe leg pain after knee replacement
column5: Right knee replacement 2 weeks ago, now with increasingly severe pain, swelling, erythema, and warmth at surgical site. Fever 102.5°F. Wound with purulent drainage.
column6: Total knee arthroplasty 2 weeks ago, type 2 diabetes (A1C 10%), obesity BMI 44, prior MRSA skin infection 2 years ago
column7: Periprosthetic Joint Infection (PJI) - Right Knee (MRSA)
column8: ESR 125, CRP 22. Joint aspiration: WBC 85,000 (diagnostic for PJI per criteria). Gram stain: gram-positive cocci. Culture: MRSA. Orthopedic surgery: staged procedure planned. Stage 1: irrigation and debridement with retention of well-fixed prosthesis (DAIR) with prosthesis exchange. IV vancomycin (MRSA). Infectious disease consultation.
column9: DAIR performed. IV vancomycin for 6 weeks. Vancomycin troughs and AUC monitored. Wound healing. Inflammatory markers normalizing. Oral suppressive therapy: TMP-SMX post IV course. Diabetes management optimized urgently (A1C goal <8% for wound healing). At 6-month follow-up: no signs of recurrent infection, implant stable, walking well. A1C improved to 8.2%.

column4: Difficulty controlling blood pressure and new kidney problem
column5: Referred for uncontrolled hypertension on 4 medications, new onset hypokalemia (K+ 2.9), metabolic alkalosis, muscle weakness, nocturia. Adrenal CT showed 2.2cm left adrenal adenoma.
column6: Hypertension resistant to 4 antihypertensives (amlodipine, lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, doxazosin), hypokalemia, new CKD Stage 2
column7: Primary Hyperaldosteronism (Conn's Syndrome) - Left Adrenal Adenoma
column8: Aldosterone-to-renin ratio markedly elevated (>30, confirmatory >100 after salt loading). Aldosterone 42ng/dL, renin suppressed. CT adrenal: 2.2cm left adenoma. Adrenal vein sampling (AVS): confirmed left-sided unilateral aldosterone excess. Endocrinology and urology. Laparoscopic left adrenalectomy. Spironolactone as bridge pre-operatively.
column9: Post-adrenalectomy: aldosterone normalized within 24 hours. Blood pressure control dramatically improved on 2 medications (from 4). Potassium normalized. No further hypokalemia. At 3-month follow-up: off diuretic and alpha-blocker. BP well-controlled on amlodipine + ACE inhibitor. CKD improved to Stage 1. Significant quality of life improvement.

column4: Painful neck and arm pain after rear-end collision
column5: Rear-end motor vehicle accident 4 days ago, neck pain that initially improved but now worsening, right arm numbness and tingling, weakness in right hand grip, unable to perform fine motor tasks, imaging ordered by PCP
column6: No prior cervical spine issues, previously healthy, works as dental hygienist (occupational neck strain is baseline)
column7: Cervical Disc Herniation (C6-7) with C7 Radiculopathy - Post-Traumatic
column8: MRI cervical spine: large right paracentral C6-7 disc herniation compressing right C7 nerve root. EMG/NCS confirmed right C7 radiculopathy. Initial conservative management: cervical epidural steroid injection, physical therapy, gabapentin for radicular pain, cervical collar for 2 weeks. Surgical evaluation for persistent deficit.
column9: Significant improvement with epidural steroid injection at 3 weeks. Hand grip strength improving. Physical therapy continuing. At 6 weeks: 80% improvement in symptoms. Returned to modified work with occupational therapy input. At 3 months: near-complete recovery. Surgery avoided. Ergonomic workstation modification. Follow-up MRI at 6 months: disc herniation decreased in size.

column4: Confusion and worsening headaches after travel
column5: Returned from 2-month volunteer work in rural Central Africa, now with progressive daily headaches worsening over 6 weeks, confusion, personality changes, difficulty sleeping at night with excessive daytime sleepiness, fever
column6: Generally healthy, no chronic conditions, no malaria prophylaxis taken during trip (couldn't afford), no vaccinations for travel
column7: Human African Trypanosomiasis (Sleeping Sickness) - Stage 2 (CNS Involvement)
column8: WHO Stage 2: CSF with trypanosomes confirmed. Lumbar puncture: WBC 22 in CSF, trypanosomes identified on wet prep. Thick and thin blood smear: Trypanosoma brucei identified. Serology positive. CDC consultation. Nifurtimox-eflornithine combination therapy (NECT) initiated - for stage 2 HAT. IV eflornithine + oral nifurtimox x10 days per WHO protocol.
column9: Remarkable improvement in neurological symptoms within 5 days. Fever resolved. Confusion clearing. Sleep-wake cycle normalizing. Completed 10-day NECT course. CSF cleared of trypanosomes on repeat LP. Follow-up CSF at 3, 6, 12, 24 months per WHO protocol (late relapse possible). At 12-month follow-up, fully recovered. CDC surveillance case reported. Volunteered for community education on HAT prevention.

column4: Severe joint pain and rash after travel
column5: Acute onset fever 103°F, severe bilateral joint pain and swelling (ankles, wrists, fingers), rash on trunk and limbs, headache, significant myalgias. Returned from Caribbean vacation 8 days ago. Cannot grip anything due to hand pain.
column6: No chronic conditions, healthy, active, first international travel, no travel vaccines for short trip
column7: Chikungunya Fever
column8: Clinical presentation + travel to endemic area highly suggestive. Chikungunya PCR positive (within 1 week of symptom onset). Dengue PCR negative. Zika serology negative. Supportive care: NSAIDs/acetaminophen for fever and arthralgia (avoid aspirin until dengue excluded). IV hydration. Rest. No specific antiviral. Vector-borne disease reporting.
column9: Fever resolved by day 7 but arthralgia persisted for 4 weeks (classic chikungunya). Gradual improvement. NSAIDs provided for post-infectious arthralgia (can persist months). At 3-month follow-up, persistent mild arthralgia - residual post-chikungunya arthritis. Low-dose hydroxychloroquine prescribed for chronic arthralgia. Fully functional at 6 months. Educated on mosquito bite prevention.

column4: New rash and joint pain in lupus patient
column5: Known SLE patient presenting with worsening facial rash (butterfly distribution), swollen painful fingers, fever 101°F, new hair loss, oral ulcers. Last labs 3 months ago showed normal complement and anti-dsDNA undetectable.
column6: SLE diagnosed age 35 (on hydroxychloroquine), hypothyroidism, recently started trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a UTI last week
column7: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Flare (TMP-SMX-Induced)
column8: Anti-dsDNA now 1:160 (was undetectable). C3 50 (low). C4 8 (low). UA: trace protein, mild hematuria. Drug-induced lupus flare or worsening SLE: TMP-SMX known trigger. TMP-SMX stopped immediately. Rheumatology. Prednisone 40mg daily for flare. Mycophenolate mofetil added for renal protection. UTI treated with an alternative antibiotic.
column9: Rash improved at 2 weeks off TMP-SMX and on prednisone. Joint pain resolving. Complement normalizing at 6 weeks. Prednisone taper over 3 months. Mycophenolate 2g/day continued for renal protection. At 6-month follow-up, in clinical remission. Anti-dsDNA declining. Sulfamethoxazole-containing antibiotics permanently contraindicated - documented in chart.

column4: Difficulty walking and urinary problems together
column5: Progressive shuffling gait, bilateral leg stiffness, urinary urgency with incontinence (new), mild cognitive slowing (able to work but wife notices changes), all symptoms over 18 months. Multiple falls. MRI brain done by PCP: enlarged ventricles.
column6: Hypertension, prior herpes meningitis age 45, no family history of neurological disease, retired banker, bilateral knee osteoarthritis
column7: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH) - Second Case
column8: Hakim's triad confirmed: gait apraxia (magnetic gait) + urinary incontinence + cognitive slowing. MRI: ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy, periventricular signal. LP tap test: removed 30mL CSF - gait improved immediately (positive tap test - predictive of shunt response). Cisternography confirming NPH pattern. VP shunt placed.
column9: Dramatic post-shunt improvement: gait normalized, urinary incontinence resolved, cognition clearer. At 3 months, independent ambulation without walker. Wife notes return to pre-illness personality and memory. Shunt functioning appropriately on imaging. At 12 months, continued excellent response. Distinguishing NPH from other dementias important: treatable cause.

column4: Flushing, diarrhea, and heart valve disease
column5: 3-year history of daily facial flushing episodes (redness, warmth), watery diarrhea 6-8 times daily, new heart murmur found on physical exam, wheezing episodes. Recently noted right-sided heart valve changes on echo ordered by PCP.
column6: No chronic conditions, otherwise healthy, works as real estate agent, moderate alcohol consumption, no family history of these symptoms
column7: Carcinoid Syndrome from Metastatic Midgut Carcinoid Tumor
column8: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA markedly elevated. Chromogranin A elevated 4x normal. CT abdomen: 1.5cm ileal primary with multiple liver metastases. Gallium-68 DOTATATE PET: extensive hepatic uptake, no bone metastases. Echo: carcinoid heart disease - tricuspid regurgitation (right-sided, classic for carcinoid). Octreotide LAR 30mg IM monthly started. Carcinoid crisis prevention for all procedures.
column9: Carcinoid syndrome symptoms improved 70% on octreotide LAR. Flushing decreased, diarrhea controlled. Liver-directed therapy: TACE performed for dominant hepatic lesions. Cardiac surgery for tricuspid valve repair planned. Everolimus added for progression. At 12 months, stable disease. Carcinoid heart disease: monitored closely, valve repair deferred. Quality of life good.

column4: Sudden onset paralysis after physical activity
column5: While swimming competitively, developed sudden bilateral leg weakness, progressing over 2 hours to complete paraplegia, sensory level at T10, bladder retention. Preceded by severe mid-back pain during swim.
column6: No known medical history, elite collegiate swimmer, family history of hypertension (mother), no prior neurological symptoms, no prior trauma
column7: Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (Spinal Cord Infarction - Anterior Spinal Artery Syndrome)
column8: Emergency MRI spine: T10 anterior horn signal change (owl's eye sign) - anterior cord syndrome. MRI brain: normal. CTA spine: no vascular malformation. No disc herniation compressing cord. Lab workup: no hypercoagulable state, negative antiphospholipid antibodies. No treatment proven effective. Supportive care. High-dose methylprednisolone controversial - given in consultation with neurology.
column9: Incomplete recovery - significant but not complete improvement. Lower extremity strength: 0/5 acutely, improved to 3/5 (antigravity) at 6 months. Bladder: intermittent catheterization program established. Intensive inpatient rehabilitation for 8 weeks. At 12 months: ambulatory with forearm crutches. Return to modified aquatic therapy (non-competitive). Significant life adjustment required. Psychological support throughout.

column4: Weight gain and mood changes
column5: 15-pound weight gain over 6 months despite no dietary changes, moon face appearance, buffalo hump noted by wife, prominent stretch marks (purple) on abdomen, elevated blood sugar (not diabetic previously), mood lability, easy bruising, hypertension worsening
column6: Hypertension (previously controlled), no diabetes, takes no oral steroids, no prior endocrine issues, works as contractor
column7: Cushing's Syndrome from Adrenal Adenoma (Endogenous)
column8: 24-hour urine free cortisol markedly elevated x3 confirmatory collections. Late-night salivary cortisol elevated. 1mg DST: cortisol not suppressed. ACTH suppressed (ACTH-independent, adrenal source). MRI adrenal: 3.5cm left adrenal adenoma. Laparoscopic left adrenalectomy planned. Pre-operative medical preparation: metyrapone to control hypercortisolism.
column9: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy performed. Post-op: cortisol supplement needed (adrenal insufficiency in remaining adrenal from chronic suppression). Hydrocortisone taper over 6-12 months as remaining adrenal recovers function. At 3 months: weight normalizing, blood pressure controlled on 1 drug (down from 3), glucose normalized, mood improved. Cushingoid features resolving. Full recovery at 12 months.

column4: Thunderclap headache and neck stiffness during intercourse
column5: Sudden severe headache (worst of life, reaching maximum intensity within seconds) during sexual intercourse, associated neck stiffness, vomited once. Denied prior similar headaches. Presented to ED 2 hours after onset.
column6: Generally healthy, hypertension (well-controlled on lisinopril), migraine history (none like this), not on anticoagulation
column7: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage - Perimesencephalic Pattern (Non-Aneurysmal)
column8: CT head: subarachnoid blood in perimesencephalic cisterns (classic perimesencephalic pattern). LP: xanthochromia confirmed. CTA: no aneurysm identified. MRI/MRA: no vascular malformation. Neurosurgery consulted: perimesencephalic SAH - benign variant. Nimodipine per protocol. ICU monitoring for vasospasm (lower risk in perimesencephalic vs aneurysmal SAH). Coiling not indicated.
column9: No vasospasm developed. Gradual improvement. Discharged day 7 on nimodipine (completed 21 days). Repeat CTA at 3 months: normal - no occult aneurysm. Perimesencephalic SAH: benign prognosis, low recurrence risk. Returned to work after 4 weeks. Sexual activity counseling: no long-term restriction. Headache resolved completely. Annual imaging not required for perimesencephalic variant.
